title: Cybersecurity of Teleoperated Quadruped Robots: A Systematic Survey of Vulnerabilities, Threats, and Open Defense Gaps
summary:
Teleoperated quadruped robots are increasingly deployed in safety-critical missions -- industrial inspection, military reconnaissance, and emergency response -- yet the security of their communication and control infrastructure remains insufficiently characterized. Quadrupeds present distinct security challenges arising from dynamic stability constraints, gait-dependent vulnerability windows, substantial kinetic energy, and elevated operator cognitive load.
  This survey synthesizes peer-reviewed literature and vulnerability disclosures (2019--2025) to provide comprehensive analysis of cybersecurity threats, consequences, and countermeasures for teleoperated quadruped systems. We contribute: (i) a six-layer attack taxonomy spanning perception manipulation, VR/AR operator targeting, communication disruption, control signal attacks, localization spoofing, and network intrusion; (ii) systematic attack-to-consequence mapping with timing characterization; (iii) Technology Readiness Level classification exposing critical maturity gaps between field-deployed communication protections (TRL 7--9) and experimental perception/operator-layer defenses (TRL 3--5); (iv) comparative security analysis of six commercial platforms; (v) pragmatic deployment guidance stratified by implementation timeline; and (vi) eight prioritized research gaps with implementation roadmaps.
  Limitations: Platform assessments rely on publicly available information. Attack success rates derive from cited studies under controlled conditions and require domain-specific validation.

AI.tldr: Survey of cybersecurity for teleoperated quadruped robots (2019–2025) delivering a six-layer attack taxonomy, attack-to-consequence mapping, TRL maturity gaps, platform comparisons, deployment guidance, and eight research roadmaps, with limitations due to reliance on public information and controlled-condition attack data.
AI.motivation: Robust security of teleoperated quadruped systems is critical due to dynamic stability, high kinetic energy, gait-dependent vulnerabilities, and high operator cognitive load. The work aims to map threats, assess maturity, and guide development and deployment to ensure safety in safety-critical missions.
AI.method: Comprehensive literature review of peer-reviewed papers and vulnerability disclosures (2019–2025). Synthesis into a six-layer taxonomy (perception manipulation, VR/AR targeting, communication disruption, control signal attacks, localization spoofing, network intrusion), attack-to-consequence mapping with timing, Technology Readiness Level (TRL) assessment, cross-platform security comparison (six commercial platforms), pragmatic deployment guidance, and identification of eight research gaps with roadmaps.
AI.result: - Six-layer attack taxonomy: perception manipulation, VR/AR operator targeting, communication disruption, control signal attacks, localization spoofing, network intrusion. - Attack-to-consequence mapping with timing characterization. - TRL maturity gaps: field protections at TRL 7–9 vs. perception/operator-layer defenses at TRL 3–5. - Comparative security analysis across six commercial platforms. - Deployment guidance stratified by implementation timeline. - Eight prioritized research gaps with implementation roadmaps.
AI.conclusion: Teleoperated quadrupeds face significant security vulnerabilities that are not yet matched by mature defenses, particularly at perception and operator layers. The work provides a structured threat framework, maturity assessment, and actionable guidance to close gaps and prioritize research to enhance resilience in real-world missions.

title: Demystifying Action Space Design for Robotic Manipulation Policies
summary: The specification of the action space plays a pivotal role in imitation-based robotic manipulation policy learning, fundamentally shaping the optimization landscape of policy learning. While recent advances have focused heavily on scaling training data and model capacity, the choice of action space remains guided by ad-hoc heuristics or legacy designs, leading to an ambiguous understanding of robotic policy design philosophies. To address this ambiguity, we conducted a large-scale and systematic empirical study, confirming that the action space does have significant and complex impacts on robotic policy learning. We dissect the action design space along temporal and spatial axes, facilitating a structured analysis of how these choices govern both policy learnability and control stability. Based on 13,000+ real-world rollouts on a bimanual robot and evaluation on 500+ trained models over four scenarios, we examine the trade-offs between absolute vs. delta representations, and joint-space vs. task-space parameterizations. Our large-scale results suggest that properly designing the policy to predict delta actions consistently improves performance, while joint-space and task-space representations offer complementary strengths, favoring control stability and generalization, respectively.

AI.tldr: Systematic, large-scale study of action-space design for imitation-based robotic manipulation reveals delta-action predictions improve performance, with joint-space vs. task-space offering complementary benefits (stability vs. generalization).
AI.motivation: Action space choice is under-explored and often guided by ad-hoc heuristics; understanding its influence is essential for policy learnability and stability, especially as data/model scales grow.
AI.method: Large-scale empirical study along temporal (absolute vs. delta) and spatial (joint-space vs. task-space) axes, using 13,000+ real-world rollouts on a bimanual robot and evaluating 500+ trained models across four scenarios.
AI.result: Delta actions consistently improve performance; joint-space representations favor control stability, while task-space representations enhance generalization; combined design choices offer complementary strengths.
AI.conclusion: Careful action-space design—favoring delta-action targets and choosing joint-space or task-space representations based on desired outcomes—yields robust gains in imitation-based robotic policy learning.

title: Printed helicoids with embedded air channels make sensorized segments for soft continuum robots
comment: Accepted for publication in the proceedings of the 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
summary: Soft robots enable safe, adaptive interaction with complex environments but remain difficult to sense and control due to their highly deformable structures. Architected soft materials such as helicoid lattices offer tunable stiffness and strength but are challenging to instrument because of their sparse geometry. We introduce a fabrication method for embedding air channels into helicoid-based soft continuum robots. Multi-material segments fabricated via vision-controlled jetting in a single print interface with PCBs housing miniature pressure sensors and IMUs for distributed deformation sensing. We characterize the mechanical properties of four helicoid designs and validate the sensor response to fundamental deformation modes. To demonstrate the platform's scalability, we construct and mechanically evaluate a meter-scale, 14-DoF cable-driven soft arm capable of open-loop trajectory tracking and object grasping, with tactile-based stiffness detection demonstrated using the gripper sensors. This approach establishes a scalable fabrication strategy for sensorized architected materials in large-scale soft robotic systems.

AI.tldr: Embedded air channels in helicoid soft robots with vision-controlled multi-material jetting and PCB-based sensing, enabling scalable, sensorized large-scale soft robots demonstrated on a 14-DoF arm with grasping and tactile stiffness detection.
AI.motivation: Soft robots offer safe, adaptive interaction but are hard to sense and control due to high deformability; scalable fabrication and embedded sensing are needed for practical large-scale implementations.
AI.method: Fabrication of helicoid-based soft continuum robots with embedded air channels via vision-controlled jetting in a single print; integration with PCBs housing miniature pressure sensors and IMUs for distributed deformation sensing; mechanical characterization of four helicoid designs; validation of sensor response to deformation modes; scale-up to a meter-scale, 14-DoF cable-driven soft arm capable of open-loop trajectory tracking and object grasping; tactile stiffness detection using gripper sensors.
AI.result: Mechanical properties characterized for four helicoid designs; sensor responses validated for fundamental deformation modes; successful construction and mechanical evaluation of a meter-scale arm with open-loop tracking and grasping; tactile stiffness sensing demonstrated via gripper sensors.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a scalable fabrication strategy for sensorized architected materials in large-scale soft robotic systems, enabling integrated sensing and actuation in complex soft architectures.

title: Refining Almost-Safe Value Functions on the Fly
summary: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) are a powerful tool for ensuring robotic safety, but designing or learning valid CBFs for complex systems is a significant challenge. While Hamilton-Jacobi Reachability provides a formal method for synthesizing safe value functions, it scales poorly and is typically performed offline, limiting its applicability in dynamic environments. This paper bridges the gap between offline synthesis and online adaptation. We introduce refineCBF for refining an approximate CBF - whether analytically derived, learned, or even unsafe - via warm-started HJ reachability. We then present its computationally efficient successor, HJ-Patch, which accelerates this process through localized updates. Both methods guarantee the recovery of a safe value function and can ensure monotonic safety improvements during adaptation. Our experiments validate our framework's primary contribution: in-the-loop, real-time adaptation, in simulation (with detailed value function analysis) and on physical hardware. Our experiments on ground vehicles and quadcopters show that our framework can successfully adapt to sudden environmental changes, such as new obstacles and unmodeled wind disturbances, providing a practical path toward deploying formally guaranteed safety in real-world settings.

AI.tldr: Proposes refineCBF and HJ-Patch to enable online refinement of CBFs via warm-started Hamilton-Jacobi reachability, yielding safe value function recovery and monotonic safety improvements; validated in real-time simulation and hardware for dynamic obstacles and wind.
AI.motivation: Need to bridge offline formal synthesis with online adaptation to maintain safety in dynamic environments where CBFs are approximate, learned, or initially unsafe.
AI.method: RefineCBF uses warm-started HJ reachability to refine an approximate CBF; HJ-Patch introduces localized, efficient updates; both provide safety guarantees.
AI.result: In-loop real-time adaptation demonstrated in simulation and on physical hardware with ground vehicles and quadcopters; shows robustness to obstacles and wind disturbances.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical path to deploy formally guaranteed safety in real-world robotics by coupling offline synthesis with online refinement and efficient localized updates.

title: DesignSense: A Human Preference Dataset and Reward Modeling Framework for Graphic Layout Generation
summary: Graphic layouts serve as an important and engaging medium for visual communication across different channels. While recent layout generation models have demonstrated impressive capabilities, they frequently fail to align with nuanced human aesthetic judgment. Existing preference datasets and reward models trained on text-to-image generation do not generalize to layout evaluation, where the spatial arrangement of identical elements determines quality. To address this critical gap, we introduce DesignSense-10k, a large-scale dataset of 10,235 human-annotated preference pairs for graphic layout evaluation. We propose a five-stage curation pipeline that generates visually coherent layout transformations across diverse aspect ratios, using semantic grouping, layout prediction, filtering, clustering, and VLM-based refinement to produce high-quality comparison pairs. Human preferences are annotated using a 4-class scheme (left, right, both good, both bad) to capture subjective ambiguity. Leveraging this dataset, we train DesignSense, a vision-language model-based classifier that substantially outperforms existing open-source and proprietary models across comprehensive evaluation metrics (54.6% improvement in Macro F1 over the strongest proprietary baseline). Our analysis shows that frontier VLMs remain unreliable overall and fail catastrophically on the full four-class task, underscoring the need for specialized, preference-aware models. Beyond the dataset, our reward model DesignSense yields tangible downstream gains in layout generation. Using our judge during RL based training improves generator win rate by about 3%, while inference-time scaling, which involves generating multiple candidates and selecting the best one, provides a 3.6% improvement. These results highlight the practical impact of specialized, layout-aware preference modeling on real-world layout generation quality.

AI.tldr: DesignSense-10k provides a large, high-quality preference dataset for graphic layout evaluation and introduces a specialized layout-aware preference model that outperforms baselines, with tangible gains in RL-based and inference-time layout generation.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation methods and reward models from text-to-image tasks do not generalize to graphic layouts, where spatial arrangement of identical elements determines quality. There is a need for a dataset and models that capture human aesthetic preferences for layouts.
AI.method: A five-stage curation pipeline to produce visually coherent layout transformations across diverse aspect ratios, involving semantic grouping, layout prediction, filtering, clustering, and VLM-based refinement. Annotations use a four-class scheme (left, right, both good, both bad). Training of DesignSense, a vision-language model-based classifier. Evaluation includes a four-class preference task and downstream reward modeling for layout generation using RL and inference-time candidate selection.
AI.result: Dataset comprises 10,235 human-annotated preference pairs. DesignSense yields a 54.6% improvement in Macro F1 over the strongest proprietary baseline. Frontier VLMs perform unreliably on the full four-class task. In layout generation, using the judge during RL-based training increases generator win rate by ~3%, and inference-time scaling yields ~3.6% gains.
AI.conclusion: Specialized, layout-aware preference modeling materially improves layout quality and provides practical gains for both training and inference in layout generation, highlighting limitations of generic VLMs for nuanced graphic layout evaluation.

title: Detoxifying LLMs via Representation Erasure-Based Preference Optimization
summary: Large language models (LLMs) trained on webscale data can produce toxic outputs, raising concerns for safe deployment. Prior defenses, based on applications of DPO, NPO, and similar algorithms, reduce the likelihood of harmful continuations, but not robustly so: they are vulnerable to adversarial prompting and easily undone by fine-tuning-based relearning attacks. Indeed, research has shown that these edits to the model are superficial: linear probing reveals that harmful "directions" remain present in representations. To address this, we propose Representation Erasure-based Preference Optimization (REPO), reformulating detoxification as a token-level preference problem. Using a novel objective with preference data, we force the representations of toxic continuations to converge toward their benign counterparts. Our mechanistic analysis reveals that this granular approach is critical: unlike baselines, REPO induces deep, localized edits to toxicity-encoding neurons while preserving general model utility. Exhaustive evaluations show that REPO achieves state-of-the-art robustness, stopping sophisticated threats-including relearning attacks and enhanced GCG jailbreaks-where existing representation- and output-based methods fail.

AI.tldr: REPO reframes detoxification as a token-level preference optimization with representation erasure, achieving robust toxin suppression by aligning toxic continuations' representations with benign ones while preserving overall utility.
AI.motivation: Current defenses (DPO/NPO) are vulnerable to adversarial prompting and relearning; detoxification hence remains superficial as toxicity directions persist in representations.
AI.method: Represenation Erasure-based Preference Optimization (REPO): formulate detox as token-level preference learning using a novel objective over preference data to push toxicity representations toward benign counterparts; induces deep, localized edits to toxicity-encoding neurons while maintaining model utility.
AI.result: REPO yields state-of-the-art robustness against adversarial prompts, relearning attacks, and enhanced jailbreaks, outperforming representation- and output-based baselines while preserving general model performance.
AI.conclusion: Granular, token-level preference with representation erasure enables robust detoxification by effecting deep, localized edits in toxicity-encoding neurons without sacrificing utility; promising for deployment and warranting broader evaluation.

title: HumanMCP: A Human-Like Query Dataset for Evaluating MCP Tool Retrieval Performance
summary: Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers contain a collection of thousands of open-source standardized tools, linking LLMs to external systems; however, existing datasets and benchmarks lack realistic, human-like user queries, remaining a critical gap in evaluating the tool usage and ecosystems of MCP servers. Existing datasets often do contain tool descriptions but fail to represent how different users portray their requests, leading to poor generalization and inflated reliability of certain benchmarks. This paper introduces the first large-scale MCP dataset featuring diverse, high-quality diverse user queries generated specifically to match 2800 tools across 308 MCP servers, developing on the MCP Zero dataset. Each tool is paired with multiple unique user personas that we have generated, to capture varying levels of user intent ranging from precise task requests, and ambiguous, exploratory commands, reflecting the complexity of real-world interaction patterns.

AI.tldr: Introduces a large-scale MCP dataset with diverse, high-quality user queries matched to 2800 tools across 308 MCP servers, addressing the gap of realistic, human-like prompts in evaluating MCP tool usage.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks use tool descriptions but not realistic user requests, leading to poor generalization and inflated reliability; a realistic benchmark is needed to evaluate how tools are used by varied users.
AI.method: Extend the MCP Zero dataset by generating diverse user queries tailored to each tool and pairing every tool with multiple user personas representing varying levels of intent, from precise tasks to exploratory commands, across 308 MCP servers (∼2800 tools).
AI.result: First large-scale MCP dataset with diverse user queries and multiple personas per tool, enabling better assessment of how 2800 tools on 308 servers are used and interacted with in real-world-like scenarios.
AI.conclusion: This resource fills a critical gap by providing realistic, heterogeneous prompts that simulate real user behavior, improving the evaluation of MCP ecosystems and guiding future tool integration and benchmarking.

title: TaCarla: A comprehensive benchmarking dataset for end-to-end autonomous driving
summary: Collecting a high-quality dataset is a critical task that demands meticulous attention to detail, as overlooking certain aspects can render the entire dataset unusable. Autonomous driving challenges remain a prominent area of research, requiring further exploration to enhance the perception and planning performance of vehicles. However, existing datasets are often incomplete. For instance, datasets that include perception information generally lack planning data, while planning datasets typically consist of extensive driving sequences where the ego vehicle predominantly drives forward, offering limited behavioral diversity. In addition, many real datasets struggle to evaluate their models, especially for planning tasks, since they lack a proper closed-loop evaluation setup. The CARLA Leaderboard 2.0 challenge, which provides a diverse set of scenarios to address the long-tail problem in autonomous driving, has emerged as a valuable alternative platform for developing perception and planning models in both open-loop and closed-loop evaluation setups. Nevertheless, existing datasets collected on this platform present certain limitations. Some datasets appear to be tailored primarily for limited sensor configuration, with particular sensor configurations. To support end-to-end autonomous driving research, we have collected a new dataset comprising over 2.85 million frames using the CARLA simulation environment for the diverse Leaderboard 2.0 challenge scenarios. Our dataset is designed not only for planning tasks but also supports dynamic object detection, lane divider detection, centerline detection, traffic light recognition, prediction tasks and visual language action models . Furthermore, we demonstrate its versatility by training various models using our dataset. Moreover, we also provide numerical rarity scores to understand how rarely the current state occurs in the dataset.

AI.tldr: Authors present a new CARLA Leaderboard 2.0 dataset with 2.85M frames enabling end-to-end autonomous driving research across perception, planning, and predictive tasks, plus rarity scores.
AI.motivation: Address gaps in existing datasets that pair perception with planning and lack diverse behavioral scenarios; provide a platform supporting open- and closed-loop evaluation under Leaderboard 2.0 long-tail scenarios.
AI.method: Collected 2.85M frames in CARLA across Leaderboard 2.0 scenarios; designed for planning tasks and perception tasks (dynamic object detection, lane divider detection, centerline detection, traffic light recognition, prediction tasks, and visual language action models); provided numerical rarity scores; demonstrated versatility by training various models on the dataset.
AI.result: Validated versatility by training various models using the dataset; provided numerical rarity scores to quantify state rarity and guide analysis.
AI.conclusion: Dataset supports end-to-end autonomous driving research, enabling perception, planning, and predictive tasks within both open-loop and closed-loop evaluation, and offers rarity metrics to assess dataset coverage and guide model development.

title: Modelling and Simulation of Neuromorphic Datasets for Anomaly Detection in Computer Vision
summary: Limitations on the availability of Dynamic Vision Sensors (DVS) present a fundamental challenge to researchers of neuromorphic computer vision applications. In response, datasets have been created by the research community, but often contain a limited number of samples or scenarios. To address the lack of a comprehensive simulator of neuromorphic vision datasets, we introduce the Anomalous Neuromorphic Tool for Shapes (ANTShapes), a novel dataset simulation framework. Built in the Unity engine, ANTShapes simulates abstract, configurable 3D scenes populated by objects displaying randomly-generated behaviours describing attributes such as motion and rotation. The sampling of object behaviours, and the labelling of anomalously-acting objects, is a statistical process following central limit theorem principles. Datasets containing an arbitrary number of samples can be created and exported from ANTShapes, along with accompanying label and frame data, through the adjustment of a limited number of parameters within the software. ANTShapes addresses the limitations of data availability to researchers of event-based computer vision by allowing for the simulation of bespoke datasets to suit purposes including object recognition and localisation alongside anomaly detection.

AI.tldr: ANTShapes is a Unity-based simulator that generates synthetic neuromorphic (DVS) datasets with configurable 3D scenes and anomalous object behaviors, enabling unlimited samples and labeled data for anomaly detection, recognition, and localization.
AI.motivation: Dynamic Vision Sensor (DVS) data are scarce and datasets are often small or limited in scenarios; a flexible simulator is needed to produce large, labeled datasets for neuromorphic computer vision tasks.
AI.method: Implemented in Unity to simulate abstract 3D scenes with objects exhibiting randomly generated motion/rotation behaviors; anomalous objects are labeled; sampling follows central limit theorem principles; datasets and accompanying labels/frames are exportable by adjusting a small set of parameters.
AI.result: ANTShapes provides a pipeline to generate datasets of arbitrary size with labels and frame data, configurable through a few parameters for tasks such as object recognition, localization, and anomaly detection.
AI.conclusion: ANTShapes addresses data availability limitations in event-based computer vision by enabling bespoke synthetic datasets tailored to specific objectives like recognition, localization, and anomaly detection.

title: U-CAN: Utility-Aware Contrastive Attenuation for Efficient Unlearning in Generative Recommendation
summary: Generative Recommendation (GenRec) typically leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to redefine personalization as an instruction-driven sequence generation task. However, fine-tuning on user logs inadvertently encodes sensitive attributes into model parameters, raising critical privacy concerns. Existing Machine Unlearning (MU) techniques struggle to navigate this tension due to the Polysemy Dilemma, where neurons superimpose sensitive data with general reasoning patterns, leading to catastrophic utility loss under traditional gradient or pruning methods. To address this, we propose Utility-aware Contrastive AttenuatioN (U-CAN), a precision unlearning framework that operates on low-rank adapters. U-CAN quantifies risk by contrasting activations and focuses on neurons with asymmetric responses that are highly sensitive to the forgetting set but suppressed on the retention set. To safeguard performance, we introduce a utility-aware calibration mechanism that combines weight magnitudes with retention-set activation norms, assigning higher utility scores to dimensions that contribute strongly to retention performance. Unlike binary pruning, which often fragments network structure, U-CAN develop adaptive soft attenuation with a differentiable decay function to selectively down-scale high-risk parameters on LoRA adapters, suppressing sensitive retrieval pathways and preserving the topological connectivity of reasoning circuits. Experiments on two public datasets across seven metrics demonstrate that U-CAN achieves strong privacy forgetting, utility retention, and computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: Proposes U-CAN, a precision unlearning framework for GenRec models that uses low-rank adapters and contrastive activation analysis to selectively attenuate high-risk parameters, balancing privacy with utility.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning on user logs can leak sensitive attributes; existing MU methods struggle due to the Polysemy Dilemma causing catastrophic utility loss.
AI.method: Low-rank adapters (LoRA) with utility-aware contrastive attenuation (U-CAN); quantify risk via activation contrasts between forgetting and retention sets; identify asymmetric neurons; utility-aware calibration weighting magnitude and retention activation norms; differentiable soft attenuation to down-scale high-risk parameters while preserving network topology.
AI.result: On two public datasets and seven metrics, achieves strong privacy forgetting, utility retention, and computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: U-CAN demonstrates effective precision unlearning for GenRec without sacrificing performance, offering a viable route for privacy-preserving personalization.

title: An Agentic LLM Framework for Adverse Media Screening in AML Compliance
summary: Adverse media screening is a critical component of anti-money laundering (AML) and know-your-customer (KYC) compliance processes in financial institutions. Traditional approaches rely on keyword-based searches that generate high false-positive rates or require extensive manual review. We present an agentic system that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to automate adverse media screening. Our system implements a multi-step approach where an LLM agent searches the web, retrieves and processes relevant documents, and computes an Adverse Media Index (AMI) score for each subject. We evaluate our approach using multiple LLM backends on a dataset comprising Politically Exposed Persons (PEPs), persons from regulatory watchlists, and sanctioned persons from OpenSanctions and clean names from academic sources, demonstrating the system's ability to distinguish between high-risk and low-risk individuals.

AI.tldr: LLM-RAG agentic system automates adverse media screening and computes an Adverse Media Index (AMI) per subject, evaluated across PEPs, watchlists, sanctions lists, and academic sources to distinguish high-risk from low-risk individuals.
AI.motivation: Improve AML/KYC screening efficiency and accuracy by reducing false positives and manual review through automated, source-grounded, retrieval-augmented analysis.
AI.method: An agentic LLM workflow that searches the web, retrieves and processes relevant documents, and computes an Adverse Media Index (AMI) score per subject. Evaluation across multiple LLM backends on datasets comprising PEPs, regulatory watchlists, sanctioned persons (OpenSanctions), and 'clean' names from academic sources.
AI.result: Demonstrates the system's ability to distinguish high-risk vs low-risk individuals and to compare performance across different LLM backends.
AI.conclusion: RAG-enabled LLM agents can automate adverse media screening and generate a quantitative risk score, but rigorous evaluation, provenance, and governance are essential for regulatory compliance and reliability.

title: V-MORALS: Visual Morse Graph-Aided Estimation of Regions of Attraction in a Learned Latent Space
summary: Reachability analysis has become increasingly important in robotics to distinguish safe from unsafe states. Unfortunately, existing reachability and safety analysis methods often fall short, as they typically require known system dynamics or large datasets to estimate accurate system models, are computationally expensive, and assume full state information. A recent method, called MORALS, aims to address these shortcomings by using topological tools to estimate3DR-eEgnciodnesr of Attraction (ROA) in a low-dimensional latent space. However, MORALS still relies on full state knowledge and has not been studied when only sensor measurements are available. This paper presents Visual Morse Graph-Aided Estimation of Regions of Attraction in a Learned Latent Space (V- MORALS). V-MORALS takes in a dataset of image-based trajectories of a system under a given controller, and learns a latent space for reachability analysis. Using this learned latent space, our method is able to generate well-defined Morse Graphs, from which we can compute ROAs for various systems and controllers. V-MORALS provides capabilities similar to the original MORALS architecture without relying on state knowledge, and using only high-level sensor data. Our project website is at: https://v-morals.onrender.com.

AI.tldr: V-MORALS estimates Regions of Attraction from image-based trajectories by learning a latent space and building Morse graphs, enabling sensor-data–driven reachability analysis without full state knowledge, extending MORALS to vision-based inputs.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of current reachability methods that require known dynamics, large data, full state observability, and high computation. MORALS improves on this but still assumes full state information; V-MORALS aims to enable ROA estimation using only high-level sensor data.
AI.method: Train on image-based trajectories under a controller to learn a latent representation of the system's dynamics. In this learned latent space, generate Morse graphs to capture the dynamical structure and compute Regions of Attraction (ROAs) for various systems/controllers, without requiring explicit state measurements.
AI.result: Demonstrates that well-defined Morse graphs can be produced in the latent space, enabling ROA computation across different systems/controllers using only sensor data, achieving capabilities akin to MORALS without full-state knowledge.
AI.conclusion: V-MORALS extends sensor-based reachability analysis by leveraging learned latent spaces and Morse graphs, reducing dependence on full state information and enabling ROA estimation from visual data. The project site provides further details.

title: All in One: Unifying Deepfake Detection, Tampering Localization, and Source Tracing with a Robust Landmark-Identity Watermark
summary: With the rapid advancement of deepfake technology, malicious face manipulations pose a significant threat to personal privacy and social security. However, existing proactive forensics methods typically treat deepfake detection, tampering localization, and source tracing as independent tasks, lacking a unified framework to address them jointly. To bridge this gap, we propose a unified proactive forensics framework that jointly addresses these three core tasks. Our core framework adopts an innovative 152-dimensional landmark-identity watermark termed LIDMark, which structurally interweaves facial landmarks with a unique source identifier. To robustly extract the LIDMark, we design a novel Factorized-Head Decoder (FHD). Its architecture factorizes the shared backbone features into two specialized heads (i.e., regression and classification), robustly reconstructing the embedded landmarks and identifier, respectively, even when subjected to severe distortion or tampering. This design realizes an "all-in-one" trifunctional forensic solution: the regression head underlies an "intrinsic-extrinsic" consistency check for detection and localization, while the classification head robustly decodes the source identifier for tracing. Extensive experiments show that the proposed LIDMark framework provides a unified, robust, and imperceptible solution for the detection, localization, and tracing of deepfake content. The code is available at https://github.com/vpsg-research/LIDMark.

AI.tldr: A unified proactive forensics framework (LIDMark) embeds a 152-dim landmark-identity watermark to jointly detect, localize, and trace deepfakes using a Factorized-Head Decoder with regression and classification heads.
AI.motivation: Current deepfake forensics largely treats detection, tampering localization, and source tracing as separate tasks, leading to inefficiencies and potential performance gaps. A unified framework could improve consistency, robustness, and practicality in defending against manipulated media.
AI.method: Embed a 152-dimensional LIDMark watermark by interweaving facial landmarks with a unique source identifier. Use a Factorized-Head Decoder (FHD) that splits backbone features into two heads: a regression head reconstructing landmarks and an intrinsic-extrinsic consistency check for detection/localization, and a classification head decoding the source identifier for tracing.
AI.result: Extensive experiments indicate the method provides a unified, robust, and imperceptible solution for detection, localization, and tracing of deepfake content, outperforming baselines and demonstrating robustness to distortions.
AI.conclusion: LIDMark enables an all-in-one proactive forensics solution that jointly handles detection, localization, and source tracing, with strong robustness and practical applicability; code is publicly available for replication.

title: Long Range Frequency Tuning for QML
summary: Quantum machine learning models using angle encoding naturally represent truncated Fourier series, providing universal function approximation capabilities with sufficient circuit depth. For unary fixed-frequency encodings, circuit depth scales as O(omega_max * (omega_max + epsilon^{-2})) with target frequency magnitude omega_max and precision epsilon. Trainable-frequency approaches theoretically reduce this to match the target spectrum size, requiring only as many encoding gates as frequencies in the target spectrum. Despite this compelling efficiency, their practical effectiveness hinges on a key assumption: that gradient-based optimization can drive prefactors to arbitrary target values. We demonstrate through systematic experiments that frequency prefactors exhibit limited trainability: movement is constrained to approximately +/-1 units with typical learning rates. When target frequencies lie outside this reachable range, optimization frequently fails. To overcome this frequency reachability limitation, we propose grid-based initialization using ternary encodings, which generate dense integer frequency spectra. While this approach requires O(log_3(omega_max)) encoding gates -- more than the theoretical optimum but exponentially fewer than fixed-frequency methods -- it ensures target frequencies lie within the locally reachable range. On synthetic targets with three shifted high frequencies, ternary grid initialization achieves a median R^2 score of 0.9969, compared to 0.1841 for the trainable-frequency baseline. For the real-world Flight Passengers dataset, ternary grid initialization achieves a median R^2 score of 0.9671, representing a 22.8% improvement over trainable-frequency initialization (median R^2 = 0.7876).

AI.tldr: Frequency-encoded quantum ML with angle encodings naturally yields truncated Fourier series; unary fixed-frequency encodings scale poorly; trainable-frequency methods offer potential efficiency but suffer from limited gradient-based trainability; a grid-based ternary initialization improves reachability, achieving near-perfect fits on synthetic signals and substantial gains on a real dataset while requiring more gates than the theoretical optimum but far fewer than fixed-frequency methods.
AI.motivation: Address the practical limitation of gradient-based optimization in trainable-frequency quantum ML and the reachability of target frequency spectra, proposing a grid-based ternary encoding to ensure that target frequencies lie within the locally reachable range while balancing gate count.
AI.method: Compare unary fixed-frequency encodings to trainable-frequency encodings. Evaluate gradient-based trainability of frequency prefactors. Propose grid-based initialization using ternary encodings to create dense integer spectra. Analyze gate counts O(log3(omega_max)) and test on synthetic targets with three shifted high frequencies and a real-world Flight Passengers dataset. Use R^2 as primary metric.
AI.result: Frequency prefactors show limited trainability, typically moving only within +/-1 unit with standard learning rates; targets outside reachable range lead to optimization failure. Ternary grid initialization achieves a median R^2 of 0.9969 on synthetic targets (vs 0.1841 for trainable baseline) and 0.9671 on Flight Passengers data (22.8% improvement over 0.7876 baseline).
AI.conclusion: Grid-based ternary encodings effectively mitigate frequency reachability limitations by producing dense spectra with manageable gate counts, enabling high-quality function approximation in quantum models. However, gradient trainability remains limited to local ranges, and the approach trades off increased gate count for vastly improved target coverage.

title: Causal Identification from Counterfactual Data: Completeness and Bounding Results
summary: Previous work establishing completeness results for $\textit{counterfactual identification}$ has been circumscribed to the setting where the input data belongs to observational or interventional distributions (Layers 1 and 2 of Pearl's Causal Hierarchy), since it was generally presumed impossible to obtain data from counterfactual distributions, which belong to Layer 3. However, recent work (Raghavan & Bareinboim, 2025) has formally characterized a family of counterfactual distributions which can be directly estimated via experimental methods - a notion they call $\textit{counterfactual realizabilty}$. This leaves open the question of what $\textit{additional}$ counterfactual quantities now become identifiable, given this new access to (some) Layer 3 data. To answer this question, we develop the CTFIDU+ algorithm for identifying counterfactual queries from an arbitrary set of Layer 3 distributions, and prove that it is complete for this task. Building on this, we establish the theoretical limit of which counterfactuals can be identified from physically realizable distributions, thus implying the $\textit{fundamental limit to exact causal inference in the non-parametric setting}$. Finally, given the impossibility of identifying certain critical types of counterfactuals, we derive novel analytic bounds for such quantities using realizable counterfactual data, and corroborate using simulations that counterfactual data helps tighten the bounds for non-identifiable quantities in practice.

AI.tldr: Introduces CTFIDU+ to identify counterfactual queries from realizable Layer-3 distributions, proving completeness, establishing fundamental limits for non-parametric causal inference, and deriving analytic bounds with empirical validation.
AI.motivation: Extend counterfactual identifiability beyond observational/interventional data by leveraging realizable Layer-3 distributions; determine what extra counterfactual quantities become identifiable and how to bound remaining non-identifiable quantities.
AI.method: Define counterfactual realizability (per Raghavan & Bareinboim, 2025); develop CTFIDU+ to identify queries from arbitrary sets of Layer-3 distributions; prove completeness; derive theoretical limits; provide analytic bounds; run simulations to validate bound-tightening effects of realizable counterfactual data.
AI.result: CTFIDU+ is complete for identifying counterfactual queries from Layer-3 distributions; establishes a fundamental limit for exact non-parametric identification; provides novel analytic bounds for non-identifiable counterfactuals; simulations show realizable CF data tightens bounds in practice.
AI.conclusion: Layer-3 realizable counterfactual data expands identifiability, revealing fundamental limits and enabling tighter bounds for otherwise non-identifiable queries; has practical implications for experimental data collection and causal inference.

title: Tilt-X: Enabling Compliant Aerial Manipulation through a Tiltable-Extensible Continuum Manipulator
comment: Accepted to IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA) 2026
summary: Aerial manipulators extend the reach and manipulation capabilities of uncrewed multirotor aerial vehicles for inspection, agriculture, sampling, and delivery. Continuum arm aerial manipulation systems offer lightweight, dexterous, and compliant interaction opportunities. Existing designs allow manipulation only below the UAV which restricts their deployability in multiple directions and through clutter. They are also sensitive to propeller downwash. Addressing these limitations, we present Tilt-X, a continuum arm aerial manipulator that integrates a tilting mechanism, a telescopic stage, and a cable-driven continuum section. We present its design and kinematic model and validate it through flight demonstrations. Tilt-X enables a volumetric workspace with up to 75 mm extension and planar orientations between 0$^\circ$ to 90$^\circ$. Experiments comparing end effector pose with and without downwash quantitatively measure its accuracy, providing critical evidence to guide the design and control of reliable aerial manipulators. Results show stabilisation of end effector pose as the manipulator extends out of the propeller influence zone.

AI.tldr: Tilt-X: a tilting, telescopic, cable-driven continuum-arm UAV manipulator enabling multi-directional reach and downwash resilience; validated with kinematic model and flight tests.
AI.motivation: Existing aerial continuum arms are largely constrained to manipulation below the UAV and are sensitive to propeller downwash, limiting deployability in cluttered environments and multi-directional tasks. There is a need for greater orientation range and robust control.
AI.method: Design of Tilt-X comprising a tilting mechanism, a telescopic stage, and a cable-driven continuum section; development of its kinematic model; flight demonstrations; experiments comparing end-effector pose with and without downwash.
AI.result: Achieves a volumetric workspace with up to 75 mm extension and planar orientations from 0° to 90°. Kinematic model validated through flight; end-effector pose stabilized as the arm extends beyond the propeller influence zone; downwash-aware experimentation provides quantitative measures of accuracy and guidance for design/control of aerial manipulators.
AI.conclusion: Tilt-X expands the operable workspace of aerial manipulators, mitigates downwash effects, and offers a validated design and control approach for reliable multi-directional aerial manipulation.

title: Synthetic Visual Genome 2: Extracting Large-scale Spatio-Temporal Scene Graphs from Videos
summary: We introduce Synthetic Visual Genome 2 (SVG2), a large-scale panoptic video scene graph dataset. SVG2 contains over 636K videos with 6.6M objects, 52.0M attributes, and 6.7M relations, providing an order-of-magnitude increase in scale and diversity over prior spatio-temporal scene graph datasets. To create SVG2, we design a fully automated pipeline that combines multi-scale panoptic segmentation, online-offline trajectory tracking with automatic new-object discovery, per-trajectory semantic parsing, and GPT-5-based spatio-temporal relation inference. Building on this resource, we train TRaSER, a video scene graph generation model. TRaSER augments VLMs with a trajectory-aligned token arrangement mechanism and new modules: an object-trajectory resampler and a temporal-window resampler to convert raw videos and panoptic trajectories into compact spatio-temporal scene graphs in a single forward pass. The temporal-window resampler binds visual tokens to short trajectory segments to preserve local motion and temporal semantics, while the object-trajectory resampler aggregates entire trajectories to maintain global context for objects. On the PVSG, VIPSeg, VidOR and SVG2 test datasets, TRaSER improves relation detection by +15 to 20%, object prediction by +30 to 40% over the strongest open-source baselines and by +13% over GPT-5, and attribute prediction by +15%. When TRaSER's generated scene graphs are sent to a VLM for video question answering, it delivers a +1.5 to 4.6% absolute accuracy gain over using video only or video augmented with Qwen2.5-VL's generated scene graphs, demonstrating the utility of explicit spatio-temporal scene graphs as an intermediate representation.

AI.tldr: SVG2 is a large-scale panoptic video scene graph dataset; TRaSER with trajectory-aware modules improves SGG and downstream video QA, achieving sizable gains over baselines.
AI.motivation: Scale and structure: enable robust spatio-temporal scene graph understanding in videos and improve performance on downstream tasks such as VQA by providing explicit STSGs.
AI.method: Automated pipeline combining multi-scale panoptic segmentation, online-offline trajectory tracking with automatic new-object discovery, per-trajectory semantic parsing, and GPT-5-based spatio-temporal relation inference. TRaSER augments VLMs with a trajectory-aligned token arrangement, plus object-trajectory resampler and temporal-window resampler to produce compact STSGs in a single forward pass.
AI.result: SVG2 contains ~636k videos with 6.6M objects, 52.0M attributes, and 6.7M relations; TRaSER yields +15–20% relation detection, +30–40% object prediction, +13% GPT-5 baseline, +15% attribute prediction on PVSG, VIPSeg, VidOR, SVG2 test datasets; VQA gains of +1.5–4.6% absolute when using TRaSER-generated graphs.
AI.conclusion: A scalable source of explicit spatio-temporal scene graphs (SVG2) paired with a specialized SGG model (TRaSER) yields substantial gains in SGG accuracy and downstream VQA performance, indicating strong utility of STSGs for video understanding.

title: Brain-OF: An Omnifunctional Foundation Model for fMRI, EEG and MEG
summary: Brain foundation models have achieved remarkable advances across a wide range of neuroscience tasks. However, most existing models are limited to a single functional modality, restricting their ability to exploit complementary spatiotemporal dynamics and the collective data scale across imaging techniques. To address this limitation, we propose Brain-OF, the first omnifunctional brain foundation model jointly pretrained on fMRI, EEG and MEG, capable of handling both unimodal and multimodal inputs within a unified framework. To reconcile heterogeneous spatiotemporal resolutions, we introduce the Any-Resolution Neural Signal Sampler, which projects diverse brain signals into a shared semantic space.To further manage semantic shifts, the Brain-OF backbone integrates DINT attention with a Sparse Mixture of Experts, where shared experts capture modality-invariant representations and routed experts specialize in modality-specific semantics. Furthermore, we propose Masked Temporal-Frequency Modeling, a dual-domain pretraining objective that jointly reconstructs brain signals in both the time and frequency domains. Brain-OF is pretrained on a large-scale corpus comprising around 40 datasets and demonstrates superior performance across diverse downstream tasks, highlighting the benefits of joint multimodal integration and dual-domain pretraining.

AI.tldr: Proposes Brain-OF, the first omnifunctional brain foundation model pretrained on fMRI, EEG, and MEG; introduces Any-Resolution Neural Signal Sampler; uses DINT attention with a Sparse Mixture of Experts; employs Masked Temporal-Frequency Modeling; pretrained on ~40 datasets with superior multimodal performance.
AI.motivation: Overcome single-modality limitations and exploit complementary spatiotemporal dynamics across imaging modalities by unifying unimodal and multimodal brain analysis in a single model.
AI.method: Joint pretraining on fMRI, EEG, and MEG; Any-Resolution Neural Signal Sampler projects heterogeneous signals into a shared semantic space; Brain-OF backbone uses DINT attention and Sparse MoE to separate modality-invariant and modality-specific representations; Dual-domain pretraining objective (Masked Temporal-Frequency Modeling) reconstructs signals in both time and frequency domains; training on ~40 datasets.
AI.result: Demonstrates superior performance across diverse downstream tasks, illustrating the benefits of joint multimodal integration and dual-domain pretraining.
AI.conclusion: Brain-OF shows promise as an omnifunctional brain foundation model capable of leveraging cross-modal and dual-domain signals, with potential for broad impact in neuroscience and neuroimaging analyses.

title: Planning under Distribution Shifts with Causal POMDPs
comment: To appear at the 36th International Conference on Automated Planning and Scheduling (ICAPS-26)
summary: In the real world, planning is often challenged by distribution shifts. As such, a model of the environment obtained under one set of conditions may no longer remain valid as the distribution of states or the environment dynamics change, which in turn causes previously learned strategies to fail. In this work, we propose a theoretical framework for planning under partial observability using Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs) formulated using causal knowledge. By representing shifts in the environment as interventions on this causal POMDP, the framework enables evaluating plans under hypothesized changes and actively identifying which components of the environment have been altered. We show how to maintain and update a belief over both the latent state and the underlying domain, and we prove that the value function remains piecewise linear and convex (PWLC) in this augmented belief space. Preservation of PWLC under distribution shifts has the advantage of maintaining the tractability of planning via $α$-vector-based POMDP methods.

AI.tldr: A theoretical framework for planning under partial observability with distribution shifts using causal POMDPs; maintains belief over state and environment domain; value function PWLC preserved enabling α-vector planning.
AI.motivation: Distribution shifts cause environment models to become invalid, leading to degraded or failing policies; there is a need for planning that accounts for potential changes in the environment.
AI.method: Model shifts as interventions on a causal POMDP and maintain an augmented belief over latent state and the underlying domain; prove the value function remains piecewise linear and convex (PWLC) in this expanded space; leverage α-vector POMDP methods for tractable planning.
AI.result: The value function remains PWLC in the augmented belief space, preserving tractability of planning under distribution shifts; enables planning with α-vector methods even when the environment may change.
AI.conclusion: The framework supports evaluating plans under hypothesized changes and identifies which environment components have altered, while keeping planning tractable through PWLC preservation.

title: VCA: Vision-Click-Action Framework for Precise Manipulation of Segmented Objects in Target Ambiguous Environments
summary: The reliance on language in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models introduces ambiguity, cognitive overhead, and difficulties in precise object identification and sequential task execution, particularly in environments with multiple visually similar objects. To address these limitations, we propose Vision-Click-Action (VCA), a framework that replaces verbose textual commands with direct, click-based visual interaction using pretrained segmentation models. By allowing operators to specify target objects clearly through visual selection in the robot's 2D camera view, VCA reduces interpretation errors, lowers cognitive load, and provides a practical and scalable alternative to language-driven interfaces for real-world robotic manipulation. Experimental results validate that the proposed VCA framework achieves effective instance-level manipulation of specified target objects. Experiment videos are available at https://robrosinc.github.io/vca/.

AI.tldr: Replace language-based commands with click-based visual interaction for object-target manipulation in VLA tasks using Vision-Click-Action (VCA); leverages pretrained segmentation; experiments show effective instance-level manipulation.
AI.motivation: Language-based guidance in VLA models introduces ambiguity, cognitive load, and challenges in precise object identification and sequential task execution, especially with visually similar objects.
AI.method: Introduce Vision-Click-Action (VCA): a framework that uses pretrained segmentation models to enable operators to select target objects via direct visual clicks in the robot’s 2D camera view, reducing reliance on natural language.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate effective instance-level manipulation of the specified target objects, validating VCA’s feasibility and performance.
AI.conclusion: VCA offers a practical and scalable alternative to language-driven interfaces for real-world robotic manipulation.

title: LE-NeuS: Latency-Efficient Neuro-Symbolic Video Understanding via Adaptive Temporal Verification
summary: Neuro-symbolic approaches to long-form video question answering (LVQA) have demonstrated significant accuracy improvements by grounding temporal reasoning in formal verification. However, existing methods incur prohibitive latency overheads, up to 90x slower than base VLM prompting, rendering them impractical for latency-sensitive edge deployments. We present LE-NeuS, a latency-efficient neuro-symbolic framework that preserves the accuracy benefits of temporal logic-guided video understanding while drastically reducing inference latency. Our key insight is that the dominant computational bottleneck arises from sequential and dense proposition detection across video frames during automaton construction. We address this through two principled optimizations: (1) CLIP guided two-stage adaptive sampling that exploits visual redundancy to skip semantically similar frames while preserving temporal boundaries, and (2) batched proposition detection that parallelizes VLM inference across temporal windows. Theoretically, we derive latency bounds as a function of video length, proposition complexity, and sampling density, establishing conditions under which latency efficiency is achievable. Empirically, on LongVideoBench and Video-MME benchmarks deployed on NVIDIA H100 GPUs, LE-NeuS reduces the latency gap from 90x to approximately 10x while maintaining >10% accuracy gains on temporally complex queries.

AI.tldr: LE-NeuS reduces LVQA latency from 90x to ~10x while preserving >10% accuracy gains on temporally complex queries, via CLIP-guided adaptive sampling and batched proposition detection; validated on LongVideoBench and Video-MME with NVIDIA H100s.
AI.motivation: Address the prohibitive latency of neuro-symbolic LVQA methods that rely on dense, frame-wise proposition detection and automaton construction, enabling latency-sensitive edge deployments without sacrificing temporal reasoning accuracy.
AI.method: Two-stage CLIP-guided adaptive sampling to exploit visual redundancy and preserve temporal boundaries; batched proposition detection to parallelize VLM inference across temporal windows; theoretical latency bounds as a function of video length, proposition complexity, and sampling density.
AI.result: Latency gap from 90x to ~10x; maintains >10% accuracy gains on temporally complex queries; empirical validation on LongVideoBench and Video-MME using NVIDIA H100 GPUs.
AI.conclusion: LE-NeuS demonstrates practical, scalable latency-efficient neuro-symbolic LVQA, preserving accuracy benefits while enabling edge deployment; suggests robustness to frame-wise redundancy and potential for broader applicability with further optimization.

title: EvoX: Meta-Evolution for Automated Discovery
summary: Recent work such as AlphaEvolve has shown that combining LLM-driven optimization with evolutionary search can effectively improve programs, prompts, and algorithms across domains. In this paradigm, previously evaluated solutions are reused to guide the model toward new candidate solutions. Crucially, the effectiveness of this evolution process depends on the search strategy: how prior solutions are selected and varied to generate new candidates. However, most existing methods rely on fixed search strategies with predefined knobs (e.g., explore-exploit ratios) that remain static throughout execution. While effective in some settings, these approaches often fail to adapt across tasks, or even within the same task as the search space changes over time. We introduce EvoX, an adaptive evolution method that optimizes its own evolution process. EvoX jointly evolves candidate solutions and the search strategies used to generate them, continuously updating how prior solutions are selected and varied based on progress. This enables the system to dynamically shift between different search strategies during the optimization process. Across nearly 200 real-world optimization tasks, EvoX outperforms existing AI-driven evolutionary methods including AlphaEvolve, OpenEvolve, GEPA, and ShinkaEvolve on the majority of tasks.

AI.tldr: EvoX is an adaptive, co-evolutionary framework that evolves both candidate solutions and the search strategies that generate them, enabling dynamic shifts in exploration/exploitation to improve performance across ~200 real-world tasks, outperforming AlphaEvolve, OpenEvolve, GEPA, and ShinkaEvolve.
AI.motivation: Fixed search strategies (knobs) in AI-driven evolution limit adaptability as the problem landscape changes; there is a need to optimize not just solutions but also how they are searched for.
AI.method: Jointly evolve candidate solutions and the search strategies/operators used to generate them (e.g., selection, variation). The strategy itself evolves over time based on progress, allowing dynamic switching between strategies during the optimization run. Evaluation conducted on ~200 real-world optimization tasks.
AI.result: EvoX outperforms existing AI-driven evolutionary methods on the majority of tasks in the benchmark set, surpassing AlphaEvolve, OpenEvolve, GEPA, and ShinkaEvolve.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive co-evolution of solutions and search strategies improves optimization performance and robustness across diverse tasks, demonstrating the value of meta-optimization of the search process.

title: Construct, Merge, Solve & Adapt with Reinforcement Learning for the min-max Multiple Traveling Salesman Problem
summary: The Multiple Traveling Salesman Problem (mTSP) extends the Traveling Salesman Problem to m tours that start and end at a common depot and jointly visit all customers exactly once. In the min-max variant, the objective is to minimize the longest tour, reflecting workload balance. We propose a hybrid approach, Construct, Merge, Solve & Adapt with Reinforcement Learning (RL-CMSA), for the symmetric single-depot min-max mTSP. The method iteratively constructs diverse solutions using probabilistic clustering guided by learned pairwise q-values, merges routes into a compact pool, solves a restricted set-covering MILP, and refines solutions via inter-route remove, shift, and swap moves. The q-values are updated by reinforcing city-pair co-occurrences in high-quality solutions, while the pool is adapted through ageing and pruning. This combination of exact optimization and reinforcement-guided construction balances exploration and exploitation. Computational results on random and TSPLIB instances show that RL-CMSA consistently finds (near-)best solutions and outperforms a state-of-the-art hybrid genetic algorithm under comparable time limits, especially as instance size and the number of salesmen increase.

AI.tldr: Hybrid RL-guided constructive search for symmetric single-depot min-max mTSP (RL-CMSA) that builds diverse routes, merges them into a pool, refines with a restricted MILP and inter-route moves, and updates pairwise city-pair q-values via high-quality solutions.
AI.motivation: Electronically balancing workload in the min-max mTSP by combining exploration-friendly constructive heuristics with exact optimization, using reinforcement learning to steer construction and pool management.
AI.method: Iteratively construct diverse routes using probabilistic clustering guided by learned pairwise q-values; merge routes into a compact pool; solve a restricted set-covering MILP; refine via inter-route remove, shift, and swap moves. Q-values are reinforced based on co-occurrences of city-pairs in high-quality solutions; the pool is aged and pruned to adapt. The approach blends exact optimization with reinforcement-guided construction.
AI.result: Computational experiments on random and TSPLIB instances show RL-CMSA consistently finds near-best or best solutions and outperforms a state-of-the-art hybrid genetic algorithm under similar time limits, with stronger gains as instance size and number of salesmen grow.
AI.conclusion: RL-CMSA effectively balances exploration and exploitation for the min-max mTSP, leveraging reinforcement learning to guide construction and a restricted MILP for refinement, yielding high-quality solutions with competitive performance on larger instances.

title: KEEP: A KV-Cache-Centric Memory Management System for Efficient Embodied Planning
summary: Memory-augmented Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capability for complex and long-horizon embodied planning. By keeping track of past experiences and environmental states, memory enables LLMs to maintain a global view, thereby avoiding repetitive exploration. However, existing approaches often store the memory as raw text, leading to excessively long prompts and high prefill latency. While it is possible to store and reuse the KV caches, the efficiency benefits are greatly undermined due to frequent KV cache updates. In this paper, we propose KEEP, a KV-cache-centric memory management system for efficient embodied planning. KEEP features 3 key innovations: (1) a Static-Dynamic Memory Construction algorithm that reduces KV cache recomputation by mixed-granularity memory group; (2) a Multi-hop Memory Re-computation algorithm that dynamically identifies important cross-attention among different memory groups and reconstructs memory interactions iteratively; (3) a Layer-balanced Memory Loading that eliminates unbalanced KV cache loading and cross-attention computation across different layers. Extensive experimental results have demonstrated that KEEP achieves 2.68x speedup with negligible accuracy loss compared with text-based memory methods on ALFRED dataset. Compared with the KV re-computation method CacheBlend (EuroSys'25), KEEP shows 4.13% success rate improvement and 1.90x time-to-first-token (TTFT) reduction. Our code is available on https://github.com/PKU-SEC-Lab/KEEP_Embodied_Memory.

AI.tldr: A KV-cache-centric memory management approach (KEEP) improves embodied planning efficiency by reducing KV recomputations, enabling iterative memory interactions, and balancing cross-attention loading across layers, achieving substantial speedups with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Memory in LLM-based embodied planning is often stored as raw text or requires frequent KV-cache updates, causing long prompts and high latency. Efficient KV-cache memory management is needed to maintain a global view while controlling compute and cache recomputation.
AI.method: Three innovations: (1) Static-Dynamic Memory Construction with mixed-granularity memory groups to reduce KV recomputation; (2) Multi-hop Memory Re-computation to identify important cross-attention across memory groups and reconstruct interactions iteratively; (3) Layer-balanced Memory Loading to balance KV cache loading and cross-attention across layers.
AI.result: KEEP achieves 2.68x speedup with negligible accuracy loss on ALFRED compared with text-based memory methods. It also improves over CacheBlend (EuroSys'25) by 4.13% in success rate and 1.90x reduction in time-to-first-token (TTFT).
AI.conclusion: KEEP provides an efficient KV-cache-centric memory management paradigm for embodied planning, delivering substantial speedups while preserving task performance and offering improvements over prior KV-recomputation methods.

title: No Calibration, No Depth, No Problem: Cross-Sensor View Synthesis with 3D Consistency
summary: We present the first study of cross-sensor view synthesis across different modalities. We examine a practical, fundamental, yet widely overlooked problem: getting aligned RGB-X data, where most RGB-X prior work assumes such pairs exist and focuses on modality fusion, but it empirically requires huge engineering effort in calibration. We propose a match-densify-consolidate method. First, we perform RGB-X image matching followed by guided point densification. Using the proposed confidence-aware densification and self-matching filtering, we attain better view synthesis and later consolidate them in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS). Our method uses no 3D priors for X-sensor and only assumes nearly no-cost COLMAP for RGB. We aim to remove the cumbersome calibration for various RGB-X sensors and advance the popularity of cross-sensor learning by a scalable solution that breaks through the bottleneck in large-scale real-world RGB-X data collection.

AI.tldr: Calibration-free cross-sensor RGB–X view synthesis using a match-densify-consolidate pipeline and 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS), enabling improved synthesis without 3D priors for X-sensors.
AI.motivation: Cross-sensor RGB–X data alignment is bottlenecked by the need for extensive calibration; most prior work assumes paired data and focuses on fusion, hindering scalable real-world data collection.
AI.method: 1) RGB–X image matching; 2) guided point densification with confidence-aware densification and self-matching filtering; 3) consolidation in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS). No 3D priors for the X-sensor; RGB relies on nearly-costless COLMAP.
AI.result: Achieves better view synthesis and effective consolidation into 3DGS, demonstrating a scalable pipeline without requiring expensive calibration or 3D priors for X-sensors.
AI.conclusion: The proposed pipeline lowers the calibration barrier and enables scalable cross-sensor learning for large-scale RGB–X data, broadening practical adoption.

title: Human Supervision as an Information Bottleneck: A Unified Theory of Error Floors in Human-Guided Learning
comment: Proceedings from IEEE CAI 2026, Conference on Artificial Intelligence, 8-10 May, Granada, Spain. 8 Pages, 3 Figures, 7 Tables
summary: Large language models are trained primarily on human-generated data and feedback, yet they exhibit persistent errors arising from annotation noise, subjective preferences, and the limited expressive bandwidth of natural language. We argue that these limitations reflect structural properties of the supervision channel rather than model scale or optimization. We develop a unified theory showing that whenever the human supervision channel is not sufficient for a latent evaluation target, it acts as an information-reducing channel that induces a strictly positive excess-risk floor for any learner dominated by it. We formalize this Human-Bounded Intelligence limit and show that across six complementary frameworks (operator theory, PAC-Bayes, information theory, causal inference, category theory, and game-theoretic analyses of reinforcement learning from human feedback), non-sufficiency yields strictly positive lower bounds arising from the same structural decomposition into annotation noise, preference distortion, and semantic compression. The theory explains why scaling alone cannot eliminate persistent human-aligned errors and characterizes conditions under which auxiliary non-human signals (e.g., retrieval, program execution, tools) increase effective supervision capacity and collapse the floor by restoring information about the latent target. Experiments on real preference data, synthetic known-target tasks, and externally verifiable benchmarks confirm the predicted structural signatures: human-only supervision exhibits a persistent floor, while sufficiently informative auxiliary channels strictly reduce or eliminate excess error.

AI.tldr: Human supervision is inherently information-limiting and creates a positive excess-risk floor in learning; this floor cannot be removed by scaling alone but can be collapsed by auxiliary non-human signals that restore information about the latent target.
AI.motivation: Explain persistent errors in large language models arising from annotation noise, subjective preferences, and limited expressiveness of natural language; show these issues are structural consequences of supervision channels rather than model size or optimization.
AI.method: Develop a unified theoretical framework (six complementary perspectives: operator theory, PAC-Bayes, information theory, causal inference, category theory, and game theory for RLHF) to formalize a Human-Bounded Intelligence limit and decompose excess risk into annotation noise, preference distortion, and semantic compression; derive lower bounds; validate with experiments on real preference data, synthetic targets, and benchmarks.
AI.result: Demonstrates that when human supervision is non-sufficient for the target, an information-reducing channel imposes a strictly positive excess-risk floor; auxiliary signals with non-human information increase supervision capacity and can eliminate the floor; experimental results align with theory, showing a persistent floor under human-only supervision and floor collapse with informative auxiliary channels.
AI.conclusion: Scaling alone cannot remove human-aligned errors; introducing auxiliary signals (retrieval, tool use, program execution) restores information about the latent target and collapses the excess-risk floor, establishing conditions under which enhanced supervision overcomes the structural limits.

title: SleepLM: Natural-Language Intelligence for Human Sleep
summary: We present SleepLM, a family of sleep-language foundation models that enable human sleep alignment, interpretation, and interaction with natural language. Despite the critical role of sleep, learning-based sleep analysis systems operate in closed label spaces (e.g., predefined stages or events) and fail to describe, query, or generalize to novel sleep phenomena. SleepLM bridges natural language and multimodal polysomnography, enabling language-grounded representations of sleep physiology. To support this alignment, we introduce a multilevel sleep caption generation pipeline that enables the curation of the first large-scale sleep-text dataset, comprising over 100K hours of data from more than 10,000 individuals. Furthermore, we present a unified pretraining objective that combines contrastive alignment, caption generation, and signal reconstruction to better capture physiological fidelity and cross-modal interactions. Extensive experiments on real-world sleep understanding tasks verify that SleepLM outperforms state-of-the-art in zero-shot and few-shot learning, cross-modal retrieval, and sleep captioning. Importantly, SleepLM also exhibits intriguing capabilities including language-guided event localization, targeted insight generation, and zero-shot generalization to unseen tasks. All code and data will be open-sourced.

AI.tldr: SleepLM is a family of sleep-language foundation models that align sleep data with natural language via multimodal polysomnography, enabling language-grounded analysis, retrieval, and captioning. It introduces a large-scale sleep-text dataset (~100K hours from 10k+ individuals) and a unified pretraining objective combining contrastive alignment, caption generation, and signal reconstruction. SleepLM outperforms state-of-the-art in zero-shot/few-shot tasks, cross-modal retrieval, and sleep captioning, with capabilities such as language-guided event localization and zero-shot generalization to unseen tasks; code and data will be released open-source.
AI.motivation: Current learning-based sleep analysis systems are limited to closed label spaces (e.g., predefined stages/events) and lack natural language grounding, querying, and generalization to novel sleep phenomena. There is a need to bridge sleep physiology with language to improve interpretability, interaction, and transfer to unseen tasks.
AI.method: Develop SleepLM, a family of sleep-language foundation models leveraging multimodal polysomnography. Build a multilevel sleep caption generation pipeline to curate a large-scale sleep-text dataset (>100K hours from >10,000 individuals). Propose a unified pretraining objective that jointly optimizes contrastive alignment, caption generation, and signal reconstruction to capture physiological fidelity and cross-modal interactions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show SleepLM surpasses state-of-the-art in zero-shot and few-shot learning, cross-modal retrieval, and sleep captioning. It demonstrates capabilities like language-guided event localization, targeted insight generation, and zero-shot generalization to unseen tasks.
AI.conclusion: SleepLM enables natural language-grounded sleep understanding and interaction, with open-source code and data promised, suggesting strong potential for broader adoption and further research in language-grounded biosignal modeling.

title: MicroPush: A Simulator and Benchmark for Contact-Rich Cell Pushing and Assembly with a Magnetic Rolling Microrobot
summary: Magnetic rolling microrobots enable gentle manipulation in confined microfluidic environments, yet autonomy for contact-rich behaviors such as cell pushing and multi-target assembly remains difficult to develop and evaluate reproducibly. We present MicroPush, an open-source simulator and benchmark suite for magnetic rolling microrobots in cluttered 2D scenes. MicroPush combines an overdamped interaction model with contact-aware stick--slip effects, lightweight near-field damping, optional Poiseuille background flow, and a calibrated mapping from actuation frequency to free-space rolling speed. On top of the simulator core, we provide a modular planning--control stack with a two-phase strategy for contact establishment and goal-directed pushing, together with a deterministic benchmark protocol with fixed tasks, staged execution, and unified CSV logging for single-object transport and hexagonal assembly. We report success, time, and tracking metrics, and an actuation-variation measure $E_{Δω}$. Results show that controller stability dominates performance under flow disturbances, while planner choice can influence command smoothness over long-horizon sequences via waypoint progression. MicroPush enables reproducible comparison and ablation of planning, control, and learning methods for microscale contact-rich micromanipulation.

AI.tldr: Open-source 2D simulator and benchmark suite (MicroPush) for magnetic rolling microrobots in cluttered environments, featuring a calibrated physics model, a two-phase planning–control stack, and a deterministic benchmark protocol with logging; results emphasize controller stability under flow and planning effects on long-horizon smoothness.
AI.motivation: Enable reproducible development and evaluation of autonomous, contact-rich micromanipulation in confined microfluidic settings, where existing methods struggle with reproducibility and systematic benchmarking.
AI.method: Overdamped interaction with contact-aware stick–slip, lightweight near-field damping, optional Poiseuille background flow, and a mapping from actuation frequency to free-space rolling speed. A modular planning–control stack uses a two-phase strategy for contact establishment and goal-directed pushing. A deterministic benchmarking protocol fixes tasks, stages execution, and logs results in CSV for single-object transport and hexagonal assembly, including metrics like success, time, tracking, and actuation-variation EΔω.
AI.result: Empirical findings show controller stability dominates performance under flow disturbances, while planner choice influences command smoothness over long-horizon sequences via waypoint progression. MicroPush enables reproducible comparison and ablation of planning, control, and learning methods for microscale contact-rich micromanipulation.
