To ensure robust evaluation on this benchmark, we propose an evaluation protocol with refined score rubrics to minimize subjectivity and ambiguity in two criteria: Instruction Following and Visual Consistency. This protocol also introduces a dual-mode evaluation framework (Tool-driven and Oracle-guided Modes) addressing the misalignment between LMM-as-a-Judge and human judgements on DLEBench. Empirical results on 10 IIEMs reveal significant performance gaps in small-scale object editing, highlighting the need for specialized benchmarks to advance this ability.

AI.tldr: DLEBench: first benchmark for small-scale editing in IIEMs; 1889 samples, seven instruction types; refined rubric and dual-mode evaluation; reveals substantial gaps in current models' ability to edit small objects.
AI.motivation: Small, 1-10% image-area object edits are crucial for precise local editing but are underexplored. Existing benchmarks and LMM-as-judge setups may misalign with human judgment, hindering progress in fine-grained editing.
AI.method: Assembled a testbed of 1889 samples across seven instruction types where target objects occupy 1-10% of the image. Included challenging scenarios like partial occlusion and multi-object editing. Developed an evaluation protocol with refined rubrics for Instruction Following and Visual Consistency. Introduced dual-mode evaluation (Tool-driven and Oracle-guided) to mitigate LMM-judgment bias.
AI.result: Evaluation on 10 IIEMs shows significant performance gaps in small-scale object editing, underscoring the need for specialized benchmarks to advance this capability.
AI.conclusion: DLEBench provides a robust, targeted benchmark and evaluation framework for small-object editing in IIEMs; findings indicate current models struggle with fine-grained edits, guiding future research toward improved data, methods, and evaluation practices.

title: Dynamics of Learning under User Choice: Overspecialization and Peer-Model Probing
summary: In many economically relevant contexts where machine learning is deployed, multiple platforms obtain data from the same pool of users, each of whom selects the platform that best serves them. Prior work in this setting focuses exclusively on the "local" losses of learners on the distribution of data that they observe. We find that there exist instances where learners who use existing algorithms almost surely converge to models with arbitrarily poor global performance, even when models with low full-population loss exist. This happens through a feedback-induced mechanism, which we call the overspecialization trap: as learners optimize for users who already prefer them, they become less attractive to users outside this base, which further restricts the data they observe. Inspired by the recent use of knowledge distillation in modern ML, we propose an algorithm that allows learners to "probe" the predictions of peer models, enabling them to learn about users who do not select them. Our analysis characterizes when probing succeeds: this procedure converges almost surely to a stationary point with bounded full-population risk when probing sources are sufficiently informative, e.g., a known market leader or a majority of peers with good global performance. We verify our findings with semi-synthetic experiments on the MovieLens, Census, and Amazon Sentiment datasets.

AI.tldr: Identifies an overspecialization trap in cross-platform learning and proposes a probing knowledge-distillation approach to learn from peer predictions. Theoretical guarantees: almost-sure convergence to a stationary point with bounded full-population risk if probing sources are informative; empirical validation on semi-synthetic MovieLens, Census, and Amazon Sentiment datasets.
AI.motivation: When multiple platforms collect data from a shared user pool, each platform optimizes against its observed local distribution. Users self-select platforms, creating feedback loops where models overfit to their base and perform poorly globally, despite existences of low full-population loss models.
AI.method: Introduce a probing mechanism inspired by knowledge distillation that lets learners query predictions of peer models to gain information about users who do not choose them. Provide theoretical analysis showing almost-sure convergence to a stationary point with bounded global risk under informative probing sources (e.g., a market leader or majority with good global performance). Validate with semi-synthetic experiments on MovieLens, Census, and Amazon Sentiment datasets.
AI.result: The approach yields convergence guarantees under specified informativeness conditions and demonstrates reduced full-population risk in semi-synthetic experiments, corroborating the effectiveness of probing to mitigate overspecialization.
AI.conclusion: Probing peers’ predictions can counter the overspecialization trap and align local learning with global performance, provided there are sufficiently informative probing sources. Practical deployment should consider the nature of probe sources and potential privacy implications; future work could address robustness, non-stationarity, and scalability.

title: Unlocking Cognitive Capabilities and Analyzing the Perception-Logic Trade-off
summary:
Recent advancements in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) pursue omni-perception capabilities, yet integrating robust sensory grounding with complex reasoning remains a challenge, particularly for underrepresented regions. In this report, we introduce the research preview of MERaLiON2-Omni (Alpha), a 10B-parameter multilingual omni-perception tailored for Southeast Asia (SEA). We present a progressive training pipeline that explicitly decouples and then integrates "System 1" (Perception) and "System 2" (Reasoning) capabilities. First, we establish a robust Perception Backbone by aligning region-specific audio-visual cues (e.g., Singlish code-switching, local cultural landmarks) with a multilingual LLM through orthogonal modality adaptation. Second, to inject cognitive capabilities without large-scale supervision, we propose a cost-effective Generate-Judge-Refine pipeline. By utilizing a Super-LLM to filter hallucinations and resolve conflicts via a consensus mechanism, we synthesize high-quality silver data that transfers textual Chain-of-Thought reasoning to multimodal scenarios.
  Comprehensive evaluation on our newly introduced SEA-Omni Benchmark Suite reveals an Efficiency-Stability Paradox: while reasoning acts as a non-linear amplifier for abstract tasks (boosting mathematical and instruction-following performance significantly), it introduces instability in low-level sensory processing. Specifically, we identify Temporal Drift in long-context audio, where extended reasoning desynchronizes the model from acoustic timestamps, and Visual Over-interpretation, where logic overrides pixel-level reality. This report details the architecture, the data-efficient training recipe, and a diagnostic analysis of the trade-offs between robust perception and structured reasoning.

AI.tldr: A 10B multilingual MLLM for Southeast Asia (SEA) introduces a progressive System 1/System 2 training pipeline that decouples perception and reasoning, aligns regional audio-visual cues with a multilingual LLM via orthogonal modality adaptation, and employs a Generate-Judge-Refine data synthesis workflow to transfer chain-of-thought reasoning into multimodal contexts. Evaluated on the SEA-Omni Benchmark, it reveals an Efficiency-Stability Paradox: reasoning boosts abstract-task performance but destabilizes low-level perception, with phenomena like Temporal Drift in long-context audio and Visual Over-interpretation. The work details architecture, data-efficient training, and diagnostic trade-offs between robust perception and structured reasoning.
AI.motivation: Address the need for robust multimodal grounding and reasoning in underrepresented regions (SEA), accommodating region-specific languages, code-switching, and cultural cues. Propose a decoupled training paradigm that first solidifies perception (System 1) and then integrates reasoning (System 2) to enable efficient, scalable SEA-centric MLLMs.
AI.method: Progressive training pipeline: (1) Perception Backbone built by aligning region-specific audio-visual cues with a multilingual LLM through orthogonal modality adaptation; (2) Generate-Judge-Refine pipeline that uses a Super-LLM to filter hallucinations and resolve conflicts via consensus, synthesizing high-quality silver data to transfer textual chain-of-thought reasoning into multimodal tasks.
AI.result: Comprehensive SEA-Omni Benchmark evaluation shows an Efficiency-Stability Paradox: reasoning amplifies performance on abstract tasks (math, instruction-following) but induces instability in low-level perception; identified issues include Temporal Drift in long-context audio and Visual Over-interpretation; provides architecture, training recipe, and diagnostic insights.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of SEA-tailored 10B MLLM with decoupled perception and reasoning; highlights trade-offs between robust multisensory grounding and structured reasoning; offers data-efficient methods, architecture, and insights to balance perception accuracy with reasoning capabilities in SEA-centric multimodal models.

title: Acceleration-Based Control of Fixed-Wing UAVs for Guidance Applications
summary: Acceleration-commanded guidance laws (e.g., proportional navigation) are attractive for high-level decision making, but their direct deployment on fixed-wing UAVs is challenging because accelerations are not directly actuated and must be realized through attitude and thrust under flight-envelope constraints. This paper presents an acceleration-level outer-loop control framework that converts commanded tangential and normal accelerations into executable body-rate and normalized thrust commands compatible with mainstream autopilots (e.g., PX4/APM). For the normal channel, we derive an engineering mapping from the desired normal acceleration to roll- and pitch-rate commands that regulate the direction and magnitude of the lift vector under small-angle assumptions. For the tangential channel, we introduce an energy-based formulation inspired by total energy control and identify an empirical thrust-energy acceleration relationship directly from flight data, avoiding explicit propulsion modeling or thrust bench calibration. We further discuss priority handling between normal and tangential accelerations under saturation and non-level maneuvers. Extensive real-flight experiments on a VTOL fixed-wing platform demonstrate accurate acceleration tracking and enable practical implementation of proportional navigation using only body-rate and normalized thrust interfaces.

AI.tldr: An acceleration-level outer-loop control framework translates commanded tangential and normal accelerations into executable body-rate and normalized thrust commands for fixed-wing UAV autopilots, using a normal-acceleration mapping (small-angle lift-vector) and an empirical thrust-energy relation for tangential acceleration, validated by real-flight VTOL experiments and enabling practical proportional navigation.
AI.motivation: High-level acceleration-commanded guidance is attractive but fixed-wing UAVs cannot directly actuate accelerations; a controller that maps accelerations to autopilot-friendly commands (body-rate, thrust) is needed while respecting flight-envelope constraints.
AI.method: Develop an outer-loop controller that converts normal acceleration into roll- and pitch-rate commands under small-angle lift-vector assumptions; adopt an energy-based formulation for tangential acceleration and derive an empirical thrust-energy relationship from flight data; address priority and saturation between channels during non-level maneuvers; validate with extensive real-flight experiments on a VTOL fixed-wing platform showing acceleration tracking and PN feasibility.
AI.result: The approach achieves accurate acceleration tracking in both normal and tangential channels and demonstrates practical implementation of proportional navigation using only body-rate and normalized thrust interfaces on real autopilots (e.g., PX4/APM).
AI.conclusion: Acceleration-level outer-loop control provides a viable bridge from acceleration-commanded guidance to autopilot-compatible commands for fixed-wing UAVs, enabling PN-style guidance while highlighting data-driven thrust-energy relations and channel-priority considerations.

title: BuildAnyPoint: 3D Building Structured Abstraction from Diverse Point Clouds
summary: We introduce BuildAnyPoint, a novel generative framework for structured 3D building reconstruction from point clouds with diverse distributions, such as those captured by airborne LiDAR and Structure-from-Motion. To recover artist-created building abstraction in this highly underconstrained setting, we capitalize on the role of explicit 3D generative priors in autoregressive mesh generation. Specifically, we design a Loosely Cascaded Diffusion Transformer (Loca-DiT) that initially recovers the underlying distribution from noisy or sparse points, followed by autoregressively encapsulating them into compact meshes. We first formulate distribution recovery as a conditional generation task by training latent diffusion models conditioned on input point clouds, and then tailor a decoder-only transformer for conditional autoregressive mesh generation based on the recovered point clouds. Our method delivers substantial qualitative and quantitative improvements over prior building abstraction methods. Furthermore, the effectiveness of our approach is evidenced by the strong performance of its recovered point clouds on building point cloud completion benchmarks, which exhibit improved surface accuracy and distribution uniformity.

AI.tldr: Two-stage BuildAnyPoint framework with Loca-DiT: latent diffusion-based distribution recovery conditioned on unstructured point clouds, followed by a decoder-only transformer that autoregressively generates compact 3D meshes, achieving state-of-the-art building abstraction and completion on LiDAR/SfM data.
AI.motivation: 3D building reconstruction from heterogeneous point clouds is ill-posed and distributions vary across data sources; explicit 3D generative priors are needed to recover meaningful abstractions from sparse/noisy inputs.
AI.method: First stage: train latent diffusion models conditioned on input point clouds to recover the underlying distribution. Second stage: use a decoder-only transformer for conditional autoregressive mesh generation based on recovered point clouds. The overall architecture is Loca-DiT (Loosely Cascaded Diffusion Transformer).
AI.result: Significant qualitative and quantitative improvements over prior building abstraction methods. Recovered point clouds also perform well on building point cloud completion benchmarks, with improved surface accuracy and distribution uniformity.
AI.conclusion: BuildAnyPoint demonstrates the effectiveness of combining diffusion-based distribution recovery with autoregressive mesh generation to produce high-quality, structurally coherent 3D building reconstructions from diverse point clouds.

title: Flowette: Flow Matching with Graphette Priors for Graph Generation
summary: We study generative modeling of graphs with recurring subgraph motifs. We propose Flowette, a continuous flow matching framework, that employs a graph neural network based transformer to learn a velocity field defined over graph representations with node and edge attributes. Our model preserves topology through optimal transport based coupling, and long-range structural dependencies through regularisation. To incorporate domain driven structural priors, we introduce graphettes, a new probabilistic family of graph structure models that generalize graphons via controlled structural edits for motifs like rings, stars and trees. We theoretically analyze the coupling, invariance, and structural properties of the proposed framework, and empirically evaluate it on synthetic and small-molecule graph generation tasks. Flowette demonstrates consistent improvements, highlighting the effectiveness of combining structural priors with flow-based training for modeling complex graph distributions.

AI.tldr: Flowette uses a continuous flow matching approach with a GNN-based transformer velocity field over graphs, preserving topology via optimal-transport coupling and improving long-range structure with regularization; introduces graphettes as motif-focused structural priors; shows theoretical properties and empirical gains on synthetic and small-molecule graphs.
AI.motivation: Model graph distributions with recurring subgraph motifs while preserving topology and enabling long-range dependencies; address gaps in motif-aware generative modeling and topology-preserving sampling.
AI.method: Flowette defines a velocity field over graph representations learned by a graph-transformer; employs OT-based coupling to preserve topology; uses regularization to capture long-range dependencies; introduces graphettes to encode motif priors (rings, stars, trees); provides theoretical analysis of coupling, invariance, and structural properties; evaluates on synthetic and small-molecule graphs.
AI.result: Flowette demonstrates consistent improvements over baselines in graph generation tasks on synthetic and small-molecule datasets; validates effectiveness of combining structural priors with flow-based training for complex graph distributions.
AI.conclusion: Structurally informed priors (graphettes) integrated with flow-based generative modeling yield effective motif-aware graph generation, preserving topology and capturing long-range dependencies.

title: Reasoning-Driven Multimodal LLM for Domain Generalization
summary: This paper addresses the domain generalization (DG) problem in deep learning. While most DG methods focus on enforcing visual feature invariance, we leverage the reasoning capability of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) and explore the potential of constructing reasoning chains that derives image categories to achieve more robust predictions under domain shift. To this end, we systematically study the role of reasoning in DG using DomainBed-Reasoning, a newly constructed extension of DomainBed dataset, in which each sample is paired with class-relevant reasoning chains. Our analysis reveals two key challenges: (i) fine-tuning MLLMs with reasoning chains for classification is more challenging than direct label supervision, since the model must optimize complex reasoning sequences before label prediction; and (ii) mismatches in reasoning patterns between supervision signals and fine-tuned MLLMs lead to a trade-off between semantic richness (informative but harder to optimize) and optimization efficiency (easier to optimize but less informative). To address these issues, we propose RD-MLDG (Reasoning-Driven Multimodal LLM for Domain Generalization), a framework with two components: (i) MTCT (Multi-Task Cross-Training), which introduces an additional direct classification pathway to guide reasoning supervision; and (ii) SARR (Self-Aligned Reasoning Regularization), which preserves the semantic richness of reasoning chains while mitigating reasoning-pattern mismatches via iterative self-labeling. Experiments on standard DomainBed datasets (PACS, VLCS, OfficeHome, TerraInc) demonstrate that RD-MLDG achieves state-of-the-art performances, highlighting reasoning as a promising complementary signal for robust out-of-domain generalization.

AI.tldr: Proposes RD-MLDG, a reasoning-driven framework for domain generalization using multimodal LLMs; introduces MTCT and SARR to leverage reasoning chains while aligning supervision with direct classification, achieving state-of-the-art results on DomainBed benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Domain generalization aims to perform well under domain shifts. Traditional DG methods emphasize visual feature invariance; this work explores using reasoning chains from multimodal LLMs as an additional supervision signal. Challenges include the difficulty of optimizing reasoning sequences and mismatches between reasoning patterns and supervision signals, which can trade off semantic richness for optimization efficiency.
AI.method: RD-MLDG comprises two components: (i) MTCT (Multi-Task Cross-Training) adds a direct classification pathway to guide reasoning supervision; (ii) SARR (Self-Aligned Reasoning Regularization) uses iterative self-labeling to preserve semantic richness of reasoning chains while reducing reasoning-pattern mismatches. The approach is evaluated on DomainBed-Reasoning, an extension of the DomainBed dataset with reasoning chains per sample, and on standard DomainBed datasets (PACS, VLCS, OfficeHome, TerraInc).
AI.result: Empirical results show state-of-the-art performance on standard DomainBed datasets, indicating that reasoning signals can complement visual invariance for more robust out-of-domain generalization.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning-driven supervision is a promising complement to traditional feature-based DG methods. The MTCT and SARR components effectively balance semantic richness and trainability, enabling improved generalization across diverse domains.

title: OmniTrack: General Motion Tracking via Physics-Consistent Reference
summary: Learning motion tracking from rich human motion data is a foundational task for achieving general control in humanoid robots, enabling them to perform diverse behaviors. However, discrepancies in morphology and dynamics between humans and robots, combined with data noise, introduce physically infeasible artifacts in reference motions, such as floating and penetration. During both training and execution, these artifacts create a conflict between following inaccurate reference motions and maintaining the robot's stability, hindering the development of a generalizable motion tracking policy. To address these challenges, we introduce OmniTrack, a general tracking framework that explicitly decouples physical feasibility from general motion tracking. In the first stage, a privileged generalist policy generates physically plausible motions that strictly adhere to the robot's dynamics via trajectory rollout in simulation. In the second stage, the general control policy is trained to track these physically feasible motions, ensuring stable and coherent control transfer to the real robot. Experiments show that OmniTrack improves tracking accuracy and demonstrates strong generalization to unseen motions. In real-world tests, OmniTrack achieves hour-long, consistent, and stable tracking, including complex acrobatic motions such as flips and cartwheels. Additionally, we show that OmniTrack supports human-style stable and dynamic online teleoperation, highlighting its robustness and adaptability to varying user inputs.

AI.tldr: OmniTrack decouples physical feasibility from motion tracking with a two-stage framework: (1) a privileged policy generates dynamics-consistent motions in simulation; (2) a general control policy learns to track these feasible motions for stable transfer to real robots, achieving accurate, generalizable, long-duration tracking and teleoperation.
AI.motivation: Discrepancies between human and robot morphologies/dynamics, plus data noise, yield physically infeasible reference motions (e.g., floating, penetration) that conflict with stability during training and execution, hindering generalizable motion-tracking policies.
AI.method: Stage 1: a privileged generalist policy performs trajectory rollout in simulation to produce physically plausible motions that strictly adhere to the robot's dynamics. Stage 2: a general control policy is trained to track these motions, enabling stable transfer to the real robot and robust teleoperation.
AI.result: OmniTrack improves tracking accuracy and generalization to unseen motions. Real-world trials show hour-long, stable tracking, including acrobatic motions (flips, cartwheels). It also enables dynamic online teleoperation with robust handling of varying user inputs.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling physical feasibility from tracking yields robust, generalizable humanoid motion control, with strong real-world stability and adaptability for teleoperation.

title: 3D Modality-Aware Pre-training for Vision-Language Model in MRI Multi-organ Abnormality Detection
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) show strong potential for complex diagnostic tasks in medical imaging. However, applying VLMs to multi-organ medical imaging introduces two principal challenges: (1) modality-specific vision-language alignment and (2) cross-modal feature fusion. In this work, we propose MedMAP, a Medical Modality-Aware Pretraining framework that enhances vision-language representation learning in 3D MRI. MedMAP comprises a modality-aware vision-language alignment stage and a fine-tuning stage for multi-organ abnormality detection. During the pre-training stage, the modality-aware encoders implicitly capture the joint modality distribution and improve alignment between visual and textual representations. We then fine-tune the pre-trained vision encoders (while keeping the text encoder frozen) for downstream tasks. To this end, we curated MedMoM-MRI3D, comprising 7,392 3D MRI volume-report pairs spanning twelve MRI modalities and nine abnormalities tailored for various 3D medical analysis tasks. Extensive experiments on MedMoM-MRI3D demonstrate that MedMAP significantly outperforms existing VLMs in 3D MRI-based multi-organ abnormality detection. Our code is available at https://github.com/RomantiDr/MedMAP.

AI.tldr: MedMAP introduces modality-aware pretraining for 3D MRI vision-language models to improve cross-modal alignment and multi-organ abnormality detection. It uses a modality-aware pretraining stage and a fine-tuning stage, evaluated on the MedMoM-MRI3D dataset (7,392 3D MRI volumes with reports across 12 modalities and 9 abnormalities); achieves state-of-the-art results with code released.
AI.motivation: Two core challenges limit VLMs in multi-organ 3D MRI: (1) modality-specific vision-language alignment and (2) cross-modal feature fusion. Existing models struggle to capture joint modality distributions and align complex 3D imaging with textual reports.
AI.method: MedMAP consists of a modality-aware vision-language alignment pretraining stage and a downstream fine-tuning stage. During pretraining, modality-aware encoders implicitly capture joint modality distributions to improve visual–textual alignment. For downstream tasks, the vision encoders are fine-tuned while the text encoder remains frozen. Dataset MedMoM-MRI3D with 7,392 3D MRI-volume–report pairs across 12 MRI modalities and 9 abnormalities.
AI.result: MedMAP significantly outperforms existing vision-language models on 3D MRI-based multi-organ abnormality detection tasks.
AI.conclusion: The modality-aware pretraining framework effectively enhances vision-language representation learning for 3D MRI and supports robust fine-tuning for multi-organ abnormality detection; the authors provide code and a dedicated dataset for the task.

title: Hybrid Quantum Temporal Convolutional Networks
summary: Quantum machine learning models for sequential data face scalability challenges with complex multivariate signals. We introduce the Hybrid Quantum Temporal Convolutional Network (HQTCN), which combines classical temporal windowing with a quantum convolutional neural network core. By applying a shared quantum circuit across temporal windows, HQTCN captures long-range dependencies while achieving significant parameter reduction. Evaluated on synthetic NARMA sequences and high-dimensional EEG time-series, HQTCN performs competitively with classical baselines on univariate data and outperforms all baselines on multivariate tasks. The model demonstrates particular strength under data-limited conditions, maintaining high performance with substantially fewer parameters than conventional approaches. These results establish HQTCN as a parameter-efficient approach for multivariate time-series analysis.

AI.tldr: Introduces Hybrid Quantum Temporal Convolutional Network (HQTCN) that fuses classical temporal windowing with a quantum CNN core to address scalability in quantum ML for multivariate sequential data; achieves parameter reduction and good performance, excelling on multivariate tasks especially under data-limited conditions.
AI.motivation: Quantum machine learning models for sequential/multivariate data suffer from scalability due to high parameter counts and gate complexity; a parameter-efficient architecture that captures long-range dependencies is needed.
AI.method: HQTCN applies shared quantum circuits across temporal windows within a hybrid architecture combining classical temporal windowing with a quantum convolutional neural network core, enabling long-range dependency capture with reduced parameter count.
AI.result: HQTCN matches classical baselines on univariate data and outperforms all baselines on multivariate tasks; shows robustness in data-limited scenarios with substantially fewer parameters than conventional approaches.
AI.conclusion: HQTCN is a parameter-efficient, scalable approach for multivariate time-series analysis, illustrating the viability of quantum components in enhancing temporal modeling with strong performance under practical constraints.

title: EMO-R3: Reflective Reinforcement Learning for Emotional Reasoning in Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown remarkable progress in visual reasoning and understanding tasks but still struggle to capture the complexity and subjectivity of human emotions. Existing approaches based on supervised fine-tuning often suffer from limited generalization and poor interpretability, while reinforcement learning methods such as Group Relative Policy Optimization fail to align with the intrinsic characteristics of emotional cognition. To address these challenges, we propose Reflective Reinforcement Learning for Emotional Reasoning (EMO-R3), a framework designed to enhance the emotional reasoning ability of MLLMs. Specifically, we introduce Structured Emotional Thinking to guide the model to perform step-by-step emotional reasoning in a structured and interpretable manner, and design a Reflective Emotional Reward that enables the model to re-evaluate its reasoning based on visual-text consistency and emotional coherence. Extensive experiments demonstrate that EMO-R3 significantly improves both the interpretability and emotional intelligence of MLLMs, achieving superior performance across multiple visual emotional understanding benchmarks.

AI.tldr: EMO-R3: Reflective Reinforcement Learning for Emotional Reasoning in Multimodal LLMs, enabling structured emotional thinking and reflective rewards to boost interpretability and emotional intelligence across visual-emotional benchmarks.
AI.motivation: MLLMs struggle to capture subjective emotions and reasoning; supervised fine-tuning has limited generalization and interpretability; existing RL methods (e.g., Group Relative Policy Optimization) do not align well with emotional cognition.
AI.method: Propose EMO-R3 with Structured Emotional Thinking, guiding step-by-step emotional reasoning in a structured, interpretable manner, and a Reflective Emotional Reward that reevaluates reasoning for visual-text consistency and emotional coherence, integrated via reinforcement learning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show notable gains in interpretability and emotional intelligence, achieving superior performance on multiple visual emotional understanding benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: EMO-R3 enhances interpretability and emotional intelligence in multimodal LLMs, offering a principled reinforcement learning framework for emotion-aware reasoning.

title: OmniXtreme: Breaking the Generality Barrier in High-Dynamic Humanoid Control
summary: High-fidelity motion tracking serves as the ultimate litmus test for generalizable, human-level motor skills. However, current policies often hit a "generality barrier": as motion libraries scale in diversity, tracking fidelity inevitably collapses - especially for real-world deployment of high-dynamic motions. We identify this failure as the result of two compounding factors: the learning bottleneck in scaling multi-motion optimization and the physical executability constraints that arise in real-world actuation. To overcome these challenges, we introduce OmniXtreme, a scalable framework that decouples general motor skill learning from sim-to-real physical skill refinement. Our approach uses a flow-matching policy with high-capacity architectures to scale representation capacity without interference-intensive multi-motion RL optimization, followed by an actuation-aware refinement phase that ensures robust performance on physical hardware. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OmniXtreme maintains high-fidelity tracking across diverse, high-difficulty datasets. On real robots, the unified policy successfully executes multiple extreme motions, effectively breaking the long-standing fidelity-scalability trade-off in high-dynamic humanoid control.

AI.tldr: OmniXtreme decouples motor-skill learning from real-world refinement using flow-matching and actuation-aware fine-tuning to achieve high-fidelity, scalable high-dynamic motion tracking.
AI.motivation: As motion libraries grow, tracking fidelity collapses due to a learning bottleneck in scaling multi-motion optimization and physiological constraints of real-world actuation.
AI.method: A flow-matching policy with high-capacity architectures to scale representation capacity while avoiding interference-heavy multi-motion RL optimization, followed by an actuation-aware refinement phase for robust hardware performance.
AI.result: The approach maintains high-fidelity tracking across diverse, high-difficulty datasets; on real robots, a unified policy executes multiple extreme motions, breaking the fidelity–scalability trade-off in high-dynamic humanoid control.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling general motor skill learning from sim-to-real physical refinement enables scalable, robust high-dynamic motion control across varied datasets and hardware.

title: ProtoDCS: Towards Robust and Efficient Open-Set Test-Time Adaptation for Vision-Language Models
summary: Large-scale Vision-Language Models (VLMs) exhibit strong zero-shot recognition, yet their real-world deployment is challenged by distribution shifts. While Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) can mitigate this, existing VLM-based TTA methods operate under a closed-set assumption, failing in open-set scenarios where test streams contain both covariate-shifted in-distribution (csID) and out-of-distribution (csOOD) data. This leads to a critical difficulty: the model must discriminate unknown csOOD samples to avoid interference while simultaneously adapting to known csID classes for accuracy. Current open-set TTA (OSTTA) methods rely on hard thresholds for separation and entropy minimization for adaptation. These strategies are brittle, often misclassifying ambiguous csOOD samples and inducing overconfident predictions, and their parameter-update mechanism is computationally prohibitive for VLMs. To address these limitations, we propose Prototype-based Double-Check Separation (ProtoDCS), a robust framework for OSTTA that effectively separates csID and csOOD samples, enabling safe and efficient adaptation of VLMs to csID data. Our main contributions are: (1) a novel double-check separation mechanism employing probabilistic Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) verification to replace brittle thresholding; and (2) an evidence-driven adaptation strategy utilizing uncertainty-aware loss and efficient prototype-level updates, mitigating overconfidence and reducing computational overhead. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10/100-C and Tiny-ImageNet-C demonstrate that ProtoDCS achieves state-of-the-art performance, significantly boosting both known-class accuracy and OOD detection metrics. Code will be available at https://github.com/O-YangF/ProtoDCS.

AI.tldr: ProtoDCS introduces a robust OSTTA framework that separates csID from csOOD using a probabilistic GMM-based double-check mechanism and evidence-driven, prototype-level adaptation to reduce overconfidence and lower computational cost, achieving state-of-the-art results on OSTTA benchmarks for VLMs.
AI.motivation: Open-set Test-Time Adaptation for Vision-Language Models is hindered by brittle hard thresholding and costly adaptation, especially under covariate-shifted in-distribution and out-of-distribution streams. A robust, efficient method is needed to safely adapt to csID while rejecting csOOD.
AI.method: Proposed ProtoDCS framework: (1) Double-check separation using probabilistic Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) verification to replace brittle thresholding for csID/csOOD separation; (2) Evidence-driven adaptation with uncertainty-aware loss and prototype-level updates to avoid overconfidence and reduce compute; employs prototype representations and efficient updates for VLMs.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10/100-C and Tiny-ImageNet-C show state-of-the-art OSTTA performance, improving known-class accuracy and OOD detection metrics; code to be released.
AI.conclusion: ProtoDCS provides a robust, scalable OSTTA solution for VLMs by combining probabilistic separation with evidence-based adaptation, addressing brittleness and computational constraints; potential for broader deployment in real-world streaming scenarios where csID/csOOD coexist.

title: SDMixer: Sparse Dual-Mixer for Time Series Forecasting
summary: Multivariate time series forecasting is widely applied in fields such as transportation, energy, and finance. However, the data commonly suffers from issues of multi-scale characteristics, weak correlations, and noise interference, which limit the predictive performance of existing models. This paper proposes a dual-stream sparse Mixer prediction framework that extracts global trends and local dynamic features from sequences in both the frequency and time domains, respectively. It employs a sparsity mechanism to filter out invalid information, thereby enhancing the accuracy of cross-variable dependency modeling. Experimental results demonstrate that this method achieves leading performance on multiple real-world scenario datasets, validating its effectiveness and generality. The code is available at https://github.com/SDMixer/SDMixer

AI.tldr: A dual-stream sparse Mixer framework for multivariate time series forecasting that combines frequency-domain global trends with time-domain local dynamics, using sparsity to prune invalid information and improve cross-variable dependency modeling, achieving state-of-the-art results on real datasets.
AI.motivation: Address multi-scale characteristics, weak cross-variable correlations, and noise in multivariate time series to improve predictive accuracy and robustness. Leverage complementary information in frequency and time domains and reduce information redundancy via sparsity.
AI.method: A dual-stream architecture: one stream operates in the frequency domain to capture global trends; the other in the time domain to capture local dynamics. A sparsity mechanism (e.g., sparse attention or masking) filters out invalid/inert information and enhances cross-variable dependency modeling. Fusion of the two streams allows joint prediction via a Mixer-based or Transformer-based forecaster. The approach is evaluated on real-world datasets, with code released.
AI.result: Demonstrates leading performance on multiple real-world datasets, validating effectiveness and generality. Ablation studies indicate contributions from both the dual-stream design and the sparsity mechanism.
AI.conclusion: The dual-stream sparse Mixer framework effectively models multi-scale patterns and cross-variable dependencies in multivariate time series, offering strong accuracy and robustness across domains, with general applicability and open-source release.

title: Hybrid Offline-Online Reinforcement Learning for Sensorless, High-Precision Force Regulation in Surgical Robotic Grasping
summary: Precise grasp force regulation in tendon-driven surgical instruments is fundamentally limited by nonlinear coupling between motor dynamics, transmission compliance, friction, and distal mechanics. Existing solutions typically rely on distal force sensing or analytical compensation, increasing hardware complexity or degrading performance under dynamic motion. We present a sensorless control framework that combines physics-consistent modeling and hybrid reinforcement learning to achieve high-precision distal force regulation in a proximally actuated surgical end-effector. We develop a first-principles digital twin of the da Vinci Xi grasping mechanism that captures coupled electrical, transmission, and jaw dynamics within a unified differential-algebraic formulation. To safely learn control policies in this stiff and highly nonlinear system, we introduce a three-stage pipeline:(i)a receding-horizon CMA-ES oracle that generates dynamically feasible expert trajectories,(ii)fully offline policy learning via Implicit Q-Learning to ensure stable initialization without unsafe exploration, and (iii)online refinement using TD3 for adaptation to on-policy dynamics. The resulting policy directly maps proximal measurements to motor voltages and requires no distal sensing. In simulation, the controller maintains grasp force within 1% of the desired reference during multi-harmonic jaw motion. Hardware experiments demonstrate average force errors below 4% across diverse trajectories, validating sim-to-real transfer. The learned policy contains approximately 71k param and executes at kH rates, enabling real-time deployment. These results demonstrate that high-fidelity modeling combined with structured offline-online RL can recover precise distal force behavior without additional sensing, offering a scalable and mechanically compatible solution for surgical robotic manipulation.

AI.tldr: Sensorless distal force control for tendon-driven surgical end-effectors using a physics-based digital twin and hybrid RL (offline Implicit Q-Learning plus online TD3) to achieve high precision without distal sensing; sim-to-real validated with ~4% error hardware, 71k parameters, real-time.
AI.motivation: Nonlinear coupling among motor dynamics, transmission compliance, friction, and distal jaw mechanics limits grasp force accuracy; distal sensing increases hardware complexity or degrades performance, motivating a sensorless yet precise control framework.
AI.method: Develop a first-principles digital twin of the da Vinci Xi grasping mechanism in a unified differential-algebraic formulation. Implement a three-stage learning pipeline: (i) receding-horizon CMA-ES to generate feasible expert trajectories, (ii) offline policy learning via Implicit Q-Learning for safe initialization, (iii) online refinement using TD3 to adapt to on-policy dynamics. The resulting policy maps proximal measurements to motor voltages with no distal sensing, comprising ~71k parameters and running at kilohertz rates.
AI.result: Simulation: grasp force kept within 1% of reference during multi-harmonic jaw motion. Hardware experiments: average force errors below 4% across diverse trajectories, demonstrating successful sim-to-real transfer. Real-time deployment with ~71k parameters at high update rates.
AI.conclusion: High-fidelity physics-based modeling combined with structured offline-online reinforcement learning enables precise distal force regulation without distal sensing, offering a scalable and mechanically compatible solution for surgical robotic manipulation.

title: Suppressing Prior-Comparison Hallucinations in Radiology Report Generation via Semantically Decoupled Latent Steering
summary: Automated radiology report generation using vision-language models (VLMs) is limited by the risk of prior-comparison hallucination, where the model generates historical findings unsupported by the current study. We address this challenge with a training-free, inference-time control framework termed Semantically Decoupled Latent Steering (SDLS). Unlike generic activation steering, which often suffers from semantic entanglement, our approach constructs a semantic-free intervention vector via large language model (LLM)-driven semantic decomposition followed by $QR$-based orthogonalization. This orthogonalization step is critical. It leverages geometric constraints to filter out the clinical semantics often entangled in standard principal component analysis (PCA) directions, ensuring that the steering vector targets only the ``historical comparison" axis. We validate our method on the BiomedGPT foundation model, demonstrating that it overcomes the trade-off between hallucination suppression and clinical accuracy. Extensive experiments on MIMIC-CXR, and zero-shot transfer evaluation on CheXpert Plus and IU-Xray, demonstrate the robustness of our approach. Quantitative evaluations on MIMIC-CXR show that our approach significantly reduces the probability of historical hallucinations (FilBERT score decreases from 0.2373 to 0.1889) and improves clinical label fidelity (CheXpert macro-F1 increases from 0.2242 to 0.3208). Supplementary evaluations confirm that the structural integrity of the clinical narrative is maintained.

AI.tldr: A training-free, inference-time control framework (SDLS) reduces historical hallucinations in radiology report generation by semantically decoupling latent space via LLM-driven decomposition and QR-based orthogonalization, improving clinical fidelity on MIMIC-CXR and generalizing to CheXpert Plus and IU-Xray.
AI.motivation: Automated radiology report generation with vision-language models risks historical comparison hallucinations—producing findings from prior studies rather than current images. A lightweight, training-free method is needed to suppress such hallucinations without sacrificing clinical accuracy.
AI.method: Semantically Decoupled Latent Steering (SDLS): construct a semantic-free intervention vector from LLM-driven semantic decomposition; apply QR-based orthogonalization to remove entangled clinical semantics; perform activation steering along the historical-comparison axis in a training-free, inference-time setting. Demonstrates improved steering specificity over standard PCA-based directions.
AI.result: On BiomedGPT, SDLS reduces historical hallucinations (FilBERT: 0.2373 -> 0.1889) and improves clinical label fidelity (CheXpert macro-F1: 0.2242 -> 0.3208). MIMIC-CXR extensive experiments and zero-shot transfer to CheXpert Plus and IU-Xray show robustness; supplementary evaluations indicate preserved structural integrity of clinical narratives.
AI.conclusion: SDLS offers an effective, training-free approach to suppress historical-hallucination risks in VLM-based radiology report generation while maintaining clinical narrative fidelity and enabling robust cross-dataset generalization.

title: Normalisation and Initialisation Strategies for Graph Neural Networks in Blockchain Anomaly Detection
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) offer a principled approach to financial fraud detection by jointly learning from node features and transaction graph topology. However, their effectiveness on real-world anti-money laundering (AML) benchmarks depends critically on training practices such as specifically weight initialisation and normalisation that remain underexplored. We present a systematic ablation of initialisation and normalisation strategies across three GNN architectures (GCN, GAT, and GraphSAGE) on the Elliptic Bitcoin dataset. Our experiments reveal that initialisation and normalisation are architecture-dependent: GraphSAGE achieves the strongest performance with Xavier initialisation alone, GAT benefits most from combining GraphNorm with Xavier initialisation, while GCN shows limited sensitivity to these modifications. These findings offer practical, architecture-specific guidance for deploying GNNs in AML pipelines for datasets with severe class imbalance. We release a reproducible experimental framework with temporal data splits, seeded runs, and full ablation results.

AI.tldr: Architecture-dependent impact of initialization and normalization on GNN-based AML detection: GraphSAGE excels with Xavier initialization alone; GAT benefits from combining GraphNorm with Xavier initialization; GCN shows limited sensitivity, guiding practical deployment on imbalanced AML datasets (Elliptic Bitcoin).
AI.motivation: Assess how training practices—specifically weight initialization and normalization—affect GNN performance for anti-money laundering on real-world, imbalanced transaction graphs, across common GNN architectures.
AI.method: Systematic ablation of initialization (e.g., Xavier) and normalization strategies across three GNNs (GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE) using the Elliptic Bitcoin AML dataset, with temporal data splits, seeded runs, and full ablation results.
AI.result: - GraphSAGE achieves strongest performance with Xavier initialization alone. - GAT benefits most from combining GraphNorm with Xavier initialization. - GCN shows limited sensitivity to initialization/normalization variations. - Findings provide architecture-specific guidance for AML pipelines with imbalanced data and include a reproducible experimental framework with temporal splits and seeds.
AI.conclusion: Initialization and normalization strategies should be tailored to the specific GNN architecture when deploying AML detection models. The study offers practical guidance and a reproducible framework, highlighting GraphSAGE (Xavier) and GAT (GraphNorm+Xavier) as favorable configurations on the Elliptic Bitcoin dataset.

title: RF-Agent: Automated Reward Function Design via Language Agent Tree Search
comment: 39 pages, 9 tables, 11 figures, Project page see https://github.com/deng-ai-lab/RF-Agent
summary: Designing efficient reward functions for low-level control tasks is a challenging problem. Recent research aims to reduce reliance on expert experience by using Large Language Models (LLMs) with task information to generate dense reward functions. These methods typically rely on training results as feedback, iteratively generating new reward functions with greedy or evolutionary algorithms. However, they suffer from poor utilization of historical feedback and inefficient search, resulting in limited improvements in complex control tasks. To address this challenge, we propose RF-Agent, a framework that treats LLMs as language agents and frames reward function design as a sequential decision-making process, enhancing optimization through better contextual reasoning. RF-Agent integrates Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to manage the reward design and optimization process, leveraging the multi-stage contextual reasoning ability of LLMs. This approach better utilizes historical information and improves search efficiency to identify promising reward functions. Outstanding experimental results in 17 diverse low-level control tasks demonstrate the effectiveness of our method. The source code is available at https://github.com/deng-ai-lab/RF-Agent.

AI.tldr: RF-Agent reframes reward-function design as a sequential decision process guided by LLMs, using Monte Carlo Tree Search to efficiently leverage historical feedback and improve optimization in low-level control tasks.
AI.motivation: Reward design for low-level control is data-intensive and existing LLM-based approaches underutilize historical feedback and suffer from inefficient search. A more principled, planning-based approach could enhance sample efficiency and performance.
AI.method: Treat LLMs as language agents within a sequential decision-making framework and apply Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to manage the reward-design process. The LLMs generate candidate reward functions conditioned on context, while MCTS exploits historical feedback to guide exploration. The framework emphasizes multi-stage contextual reasoning to improve search efficiency and reward quality.
AI.result: Demonstrates outstanding performance across 17 diverse low-level control tasks, indicating that RF-Agent effectively improves reward-function optimization by leveraging LLMs and MCTS.
AI.conclusion: RF-Agent offers a novel integration of LLM-based reasoning with planning via MCTS to design dense reward functions more efficiently, achieving strong empirical results and providing a reusable implementation (code available online).

title: TSC: Topology-Conditioned Stackelberg Coordination for Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning in Interactive Driving
summary: Safe and efficient autonomous driving in dense traffic is fundamentally a decentralized multi-agent coordination problem, where interactions at conflict points such as merging and weaving must be resolved reliably under partial observability. With only local and incomplete cues, interaction patterns can change rapidly, often causing unstable behaviors such as oscillatory yielding or unsafe commitments. Existing multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) approaches either adopt synchronous decision-making, which exacerbate non-stationarity, or depend on centralized sequencing mechanisms that scale poorly as traffic density increases. To address these limitations, we propose Topology-conditioned Stackelberg Coordination (TSC), a learning framework for decentralized interactive driving under communication-free execution, which extracts a time-varying directed priority graph from braid-inspired weaving relations between trajectories, thereby defining local leader-follower dependencies without constructing a global order of play. Conditioned on this graph, TSC endogenously factorizes dense interactions into graph-local Stackelberg subgames and, under centralized training and decentralized execution (CTDE), learns a sequential coordination policy that anticipates leaders via action prediction and trains followers through action-conditioned value learning to approximate local best responses, improving training stability and safety in dense traffic. Experiments across four dense traffic scenarios show that TSC achieves superior performance over representative MARL baselines across key metrics, most notably reducing collisions while maintaining competitive traffic efficiency and control smoothness.

AI.tldr: Topology-conditioned Stackelberg Coordination (TSC) enables decentralized driving under CTDE by deriving a time-varying directed priority graph from weaving relations, decomposing dense interactions into graph-local Stackelberg subgames, and training leaders via action prediction and followers via action-conditioned value learning to improve stability and safety in dense traffic.
AI.motivation: address non-stationarity and scalability in MARL for dense, partially observable traffic where synchronous decisions or centralized sequencing fail as density increases.
AI.method: Extract a time-varying directed priority graph from braid-inspired weaving relations between trajectories to establish local leader-follower dependencies. Condition on this graph to factorize interactions into graph-local Stackelberg subgames. During centralized training, learn a sequential coordination policy that anticipates leaders via action prediction and trains followers through action-conditioned value learning to approximate local best responses, enabling decentralized execution without communication.
AI.result: Experiments across four dense traffic scenarios show TSC outperforms representative MARL baselines, notably reducing collisions while maintaining competitive traffic efficiency and control smoothness.
AI.conclusion: TSC provides a scalable, communication-free coordination framework that leverages dynamic local hierarchies to stabilize training and improve safety in dense traffic.

title: Vision-Language Semantic Grounding for Multi-Domain Crop-Weed Segmentation
summary: Fine-grained crop-weed segmentation is essential for enabling targeted herbicide application in precision agriculture. However, existing deep learning models struggle to generalize across heterogeneous agricultural environments due to reliance on dataset-specific visual features. We propose Vision-Language Weed Segmentation (VL-WS), a novel framework that addresses this limitation by grounding pixel-level segmentation in semantically aligned, domain-invariant representations. Our architecture employs a dual-encoder design, where frozen Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining (CLIP) embeddings and task-specific spatial features are fused and modulated via Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) layers conditioned on natural language captions. This design enables image level textual descriptions to guide channel-wise feature refinement while preserving fine-grained spatial localization. Unlike prior works restricted to training and evaluation on single-source datasets, VL-WS is trained on a unified corpus that includes close-range ground imagery (robotic platforms) and high-altitude UAV imagery, covering diverse crop types, weed species, growth stages, and sensing conditions. Experimental results across four benchmark datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework, with VL-WS achieving a mean Dice score of 91.64% and outperforming the CNN baseline by 4.98%. The largest gains occur on the most challenging weed class, where VL-WS attains 80.45% Dice score compared to 65.03% for the best baseline, representing a 15.42% improvement. VL-WS further maintains stable weed segmentation performance under limited target-domain supervision, indicating improved generalization and data efficiency. These findings highlight the potential of vision-language alignment to enable scalable, label-efficient segmentation models deployable across diverse real-world agricultural domains.

AI.tldr: VL-WS uses a dual-encoder architecture with frozen CLIP embeddings and task-specific spatial features, modulated by FiLM layers conditioned on natural-language captions, to achieve domain-invariant, fine-grained crop-weed segmentation across diverse datasets; it achieves high Dice scores and shows strong generalization and data efficiency.
AI.motivation: Generalization of fine-grained crop-weed segmentation across heterogeneous agricultural environments is limited by dataset-specific visual cues; a scalable, label-efficient approach leveraging vision-language alignment is needed.
AI.method: A dual-encoder model combining frozen CLIP embeddings with task-specific spatial features. Features are fused and modulated via FiLM layers conditioned on natural-language captions. The model is trained on a unified multi-domain corpus spanning close-range ground imagery and high-altitude UAV imagery, with diverse crops, weeds, growth stages, and sensing conditions.
AI.result: On four benchmarks, VL-WS achieves a mean Dice of 91.64%, outperforming a CNN baseline by 4.98%. The hardest weed class shows a notable improvement (80.45% vs 65.03%, +15.42%). The model also maintains performance under limited target-domain supervision, indicating better generalization and data efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Vision-language alignment via CLIP-based grounding and FiLM-based conditioning yields domain-invariant, fine-grained segmentation suitable for deployment across diverse agricultural settings, with strong generalization and data-efficiency advantages.

title: When Does Multimodal Learning Help in Healthcare? A Benchmark on EHR and Chest X-Ray Fusion
summary: Machine learning holds promise for advancing clinical decision support, yet it remains unclear when multimodal learning truly helps in practice, particularly under modality missingness and fairness constraints. In this work, we conduct a systematic benchmark of multimodal fusion between Electronic Health Records (EHR) and chest X-rays (CXR) on standardized cohorts from MIMIC-IV and MIMIC-CXR, aiming to answer four fundamental questions: when multimodal fusion improves clinical prediction, how different fusion strategies compare, how robust existing methods are to missing modalities, and whether multimodal models achieve algorithmic fairness. Our study reveals several key insights. Multimodal fusion improves performance when modalities are complete, with gains concentrating in diseases that require complementary information from both EHR and CXR. While cross-modal learning mechanisms capture clinically meaningful dependencies beyond simple concatenation, the rich temporal structure of EHR introduces strong modality imbalance that architectural complexity alone cannot overcome. Under realistic missingness, multimodal benefits rapidly degrade unless models are explicitly designed to handle incomplete inputs. Moreover, multimodal fusion does not inherently improve fairness, with subgroup disparities mainly arising from unequal sensitivity across demographic groups. To support reproducible and extensible evaluation, we further release a flexible benchmarking toolkit that enables plug-and-play integration of new models and datasets. Together, this work provides actionable guidance on when multimodal learning helps, when it fails, and why, laying the foundation for developing clinically deployable multimodal systems that are both effective and reliable. The open-source toolkit can be found at https://github.com/jakeykj/CareBench.

AI.tldr: Systematic benchmark of EHR–CXR multimodal fusion on MIMIC-IV and MIMIC-CXR to answer when fusion helps, how strategies compare, robustness to missingness, and fairness. Key findings: fusion helps when modalities are complete and provide complementary information; cross-modal learning offers benefits beyond simple concatenation; temporal EHR structure causes modality imbalance that complexity alone cannot resolve; under missing data, benefits diminish unless models handle incomplete inputs; fusion does not automatically improve fairness; release of a flexible benchmarking toolkit (CareBench) for reproducible evaluation.
AI.motivation: Clarify the practical value of multimodal fusion in clinical decision support under realistic conditions of modality missingness and fairness constraints, and provide reproducible benchmarks to guide deployment.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation on standardized cohorts from MIMIC-IV and MIMIC-CXR, comparing multiple multimodal fusion strategies (notably cross-modal vs concatenation), assessing performance with complete and incomplete modalities, evaluating robustness to missing data, analyzing subgroup fairness across demographic groups, and releasing an extensible benchmarking toolkit (CareBench) for plug‑and‑play model/dataset integration.
AI.result: Fusion yields performance gains when both EHR and CXR are available and provide complementary information; cross-modal learning captures clinically meaningful dependencies beyond concatenation; the rich temporal structure of EHR introduces modality imbalance that architectural complexity alone cannot overcome; with realistic missingness, multimodal benefits rapidly degrade unless models are designed for incomplete inputs; multimodal fusion does not inherently improve fairness, with subgroup disparities primarily driven by unequal sensitivity across demographics.
AI.conclusion: Provides actionable guidance on when multimodal learning is beneficial, when it fails, and why; establishes a foundation for clinically deployable, reliable multimodal systems; introduces CareBench as an open-source benchmarking toolkit for reproducible evaluation.

title: Pessimistic Auxiliary Policy for Offline Reinforcement Learning
summary: Offline reinforcement learning aims to learn an agent from pre-collected datasets, avoiding unsafe and inefficient real-time interaction. However, inevitable access to out-ofdistribution actions during the learning process introduces approximation errors, causing the error accumulation and considerable overestimation. In this paper, we construct a new pessimistic auxiliary policy for sampling reliable actions. Specifically, we develop a pessimistic auxiliary strategy by maximizing the lower confidence bound of the Q-function. The pessimistic auxiliary strategy exhibits a relatively high value and low uncertainty in the vicinity of the learned policy, avoiding the learned policy sampling high-value actions with potentially high errors during the learning process. Less approximation error introduced by sampled action from pessimistic auxiliary strategy leads to the alleviation of error accumulation. Extensive experiments on offline reinforcement learning benchmarks reveal that utilizing the pessimistic auxiliary strategy can effectively improve the efficacy of other offline RL approaches.

AI.tldr: A pessimistic auxiliary policy is proposed to sample safer actions in offline RL by maximizing the lower confidence bound of the Q-function, reducing estimation errors and improving performance across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Offline RL suffers from out-of-distribution actions leading to approximation errors and error accumulation; a safer action-sampling mechanism is needed to mitigate overestimation and instability.
AI.method: Introduce a pessimistic auxiliary strategy that selects actions by maximizing the lower confidence bound (LCB) of the Q-function. This yields high-value actions with low uncertainty near the current policy, reducing sampling of high-value but high-error actions. The approach is designed to be compatible with existing offline RL methods and mitigates error accumulation by limiting the influence of poorly estimated actions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on standard offline RL benchmarks show that using the pessimistic auxiliary strategy improves performance and can enhance other offline RL methods.
AI.conclusion: Pessimistic auxiliary sampling reduces approximation error and error propagation in offline RL, offering a generally applicable improvement to existing offline RL algorithms.

title: ABPolicy: Asynchronous B-Spline Flow Policy for Real-Time and Smooth Robotic Manipulation
summary: Robotic manipulation requires policies that are smooth and responsive to evolving observations. However, synchronous inference in the raw action space introduces several challenges, including intra-chunk jitter, inter-chunk discontinuities, and stop-and-go execution. These issues undermine a policy's smoothness and its responsiveness to environmental changes. We propose ABPolicy, an asynchronous flow-matching policy that operates in a B-spline control-point action space. First, the B-spline representation ensures intra-chunk smoothness. Second, we introduce bidirectional action prediction coupled with refitting optimization to enforce inter-chunk continuity. Finally, by leveraging asynchronous inference, ABPolicy delivers real-time, continuous updates. We evaluate ABPolicy across seven tasks encompassing both static settings and dynamic settings with moving objects. Empirical results indicate that ABPolicy reduces trajectory jerk, leading to smoother motion and improved performance. Project website: https://teee000.github.io/ABPolicy/.

AI.tldr: An asynchronous, B-spline-based policy (ABPolicy) achieves smooth, real-time robotic manipulation by ensuring intra-chunk smoothness through B-splines, enforcing inter-chunk continuity via bidirectional prediction and refitting, and leveraging asynchronous inference for continuous updates.
AI.motivation: Synchronous inference in raw action space leads to intra-chunk jitter, inter-chunk discontinuities, and stop-and-go execution, reducing policy smoothness and environmental responsiveness.
AI.method: ABPolicy represents actions as B-spline control points to guarantee intra-chunk smoothness. It uses bidirectional action prediction with refitting optimization to ensure inter-chunk continuity, and employs asynchronous inference to provide real-time, continuous updates.
AI.result: Evaluated on seven tasks across static and dynamic (moving objects) settings; ABPolicy reduces trajectory jerk, yielding smoother motion and improved performance.
AI.conclusion: ABPolicy delivers smooth, continuous manipulation with real-time updates, validating the benefits of flow-matching in a B-spline control-point action space and asynchronous inference across diverse tasks.

title: Any Model, Any Place, Any Time: Get Remote Sensing Foundation Model Embeddings On Demand
summary: The remote sensing community is witnessing a rapid growth of foundation models, which provide powerful embeddings for a wide range of downstream tasks. However, practical adoption and fair comparison remain challenging due to substantial heterogeneity in model release formats, platforms and interfaces, and input data specifications. These inconsistencies significantly increase the cost of obtaining, using, and benchmarking embeddings across models. To address this issue, we propose rs-embed, a Python library that offers a unified, region of interst (ROI) centric interface: with a single line of code, users can retrieve embeddings from any supported model for any location and any time range. The library also provides efficient batch processing to enable large-scale embedding generation and evaluation. The code is available at: https://github.com/cybergis/rs-embed

AI.tldr: rs-embed unifies ROI-based embedding retrieval across diverse remote-sensing models via a single API and supports batch processing for scalable benchmarking.
AI.motivation: To reduce costs and barriers from heterogeneous model formats, platforms, and data specs that hinder adoption and fair comparison of embeddings.
AI.method: Develop rs-embed Python library with a unified ROI-centric interface enabling one-line embedding retrieval for any location and time range across supported models; includes efficient batching for large-scale generation and evaluation.
AI.result: Provides a unified API and batching capabilities; ready-to-use code on GitHub, enabling reproducible benchmarking and easier deployment.
AI.conclusion: rs-embed addresses fragmentation in the remote-sensing embeddings space by standardizing retrieval and evaluation workflows, facilitating adoption and fair benchmarking.

title: Portfolio Reinforcement Learning with Scenario-Context Rollout
summary:
Market regime shifts induce distribution shifts that can degrade the performance of portfolio rebalancing policies. We propose macro-conditioned scenario-context rollout (SCR) that generates plausible next-day multivariate return scenarios under stress events. However, doing so faces new challenges, as history will never tell what would have happened differently. As a result, incorporating scenario-based rewards from rollouts introduces a reward--transition mismatch in temporal-difference learning, destabilizing RL critic training.
  We analyze this inconsistency and show it leads to a mixed evaluation target. Guided by this analysis, we construct a counterfactual next state using the rollout-implied continuations and augment the critic agent's bootstrap target. Doing so stabilizes the learning and provides a viable bias-variance tradeoff.
  In out-of-sample evaluations across 31 distinct universes of U.S. equity and ETF portfolios, our method improves Sharpe ratio by up to 76% and reduces maximum drawdown by up to 53% compared with classic and RL-based portfolio rebalancing baselines.

AI.tldr: Introduces macro-conditioned scenario-context rollout to generate next-day return scenarios under stress, addresses reward-transition mismatch in TD learning by using counterfactual continuations to augment the critic target, yielding improved out-of-sample risk-adjusted performance (Sharpe gains up to 76%, drawdown reductions up to 53%).
AI.motivation: Portfolio rebalancing under market regime shifts causes distribution shifts that degrade RL policies; robust scenario-based planning and stable RL training are needed.
AI.method: Proposes SCR: macro-conditioned scenario-context rollout to produce plausible next-day multivariate returns during stress; analyzes reward-transition mismatch from scenario-based rewards; constructs counterfactual next state from rollout-implied continuations; augments the critic bootstrap target to stabilize TD learning.
AI.result: Empirical out-of-sample evaluation shows Sharpe improvement up to 76% and max drawdown reduction up to 53% across 31 universes, outperforming classic and RL-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: Stabilizing the critic via counterfactual rollouts yields a viable bias-variance tradeoff and enhances RL-based portfolio rebalancing under regime-shift risks; potential for broader application in finance.

title: Teleoperated Omni-directional Dual Arm Mobile Manipulation Robotic System with Shared Control for Retail Store
comment: This work has been accepted for publication in the Proceedings of the IEEE International Conference on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics (SMC 2024). $©$ IEEE. The final version is available via IEEE Xplore
summary: The swiftly expanding retail sector is increasingly adopting autonomous mobile robots empowered by artificial intelligence and machine learning algorithms to gain an edge in the competitive market. However, these autonomous robots encounter challenges in adapting to the dynamic nature of retail products, often struggling to operate autonomously in novel situations. In this study, we introduce an omni-directional dual-arm mobile robot specifically tailored for use in retail environments. Additionally, we propose a tele-operation method that enables shared control between the robot and a human operator. This approach utilizes a Virtual Reality (VR) motion capture system to capture the operator's commands, which are then transmitted to the robot located remotely in a retail setting. Furthermore, the robot is equipped with heterogeneous grippers on both manipulators, facilitating the handling of a wide range of items. We validate the efficacy of the proposed system through testing in a mockup of retail environment, demonstrating its ability to manipulate various commonly encountered retail items using both single and dual-arm coordinated manipulation techniques.

AI.tldr: An omni-directional dual-arm mobile robot with VR-based teleoperation and heterogeneous grippers is proposed for retail manipulation and validated in a mock retail environment using single- and dual-arm coordination.
AI.motivation: Retail environments feature highly dynamic, diverse products and contexts that challenge autonomous manipulation; human-in-the-loop and adaptable hardware can improve robustness and versatility.
AI.method: Design and implement an omni-directional dual-arm mobile robot; develop a shared-control tele-operation pipeline using VR motion capture to transmit operator commands to a remotely located robot; equip manipulators with heterogeneous grippers; evaluate in a mock retail setting with single- and dual-arm manipulation tasks.
AI.result: The system successfully manipulated a variety of common retail items in the mock environment, demonstrating effective single- and dual-arm coordination under teleoperation control.
AI.conclusion: The integrated robot and VR-based tele-operation approach with versatile grippers enhances autonomous-by-human-in-the-loop manipulation in retail, supporting handling of diverse items; future work could address real-world deployment, autonomy in novel items, and latency or robustness improvements.

title: Towards Source-Aware Object Swapping with Initial Noise Perturbation
comment: This paper is accepted by CVPR 2026 Findings
summary: Object swapping aims to replace a source object in a scene with a reference object while preserving object fidelity, scene fidelity, and object-scene harmony. Existing methods either require per-object finetuning and slow inference or rely on extra paired data that mostly depict the same object across contexts, forcing models to rely on background cues rather than learning cross-object alignment. We propose SourceSwap, a self-supervised and source-aware framework that learns cross-object alignment. Our key insight is to synthesize high-quality pseudo pairs from any image via a frequency-separated perturbation in the initial-noise space, which alters appearance while preserving pose, coarse shape, and scene layout, requiring no videos, multi-view data, or additional images. We then train a dual U-Net with full-source conditioning and a noise-free reference encoder, enabling direct inter-object alignment, zero-shot inference without per-object finetuning, and lightweight iterative refinement. We further introduce SourceBench, a high-quality benchmark with higher resolution, more categories, and richer interactions. Experiments demonstrate that SourceSwap achieves superior fidelity, stronger scene preservation, and more natural harmony, and it transfers well to edits such as subject-driven refinement and face swapping.

AI.tldr: SourceSwap is a self-supervised, source-aware framework that enables zero-shot cross-object swapping without per-object finetuning; it synthesizes high-quality pseudo pairs via frequency-separated perturbations in the initial-noise space and trains a dual U-Net with full-source conditioning and a noise-free reference encoder. It also introduces SourceBench for high-quality evaluation. Empirical results show improved fidelity, scene preservation, and natural harmony, with transfer to subject-driven refinement and face swapping.
AI.motivation: Existing object-swapping methods rely on per-object finetuning or require extra paired data that biases models toward background cues, hindering robust cross-object alignment. There is a need for self-supervised, cross-object alignment that works broadly across objects and scenes without additional data.
AI.method: The approach synthesizes pseudo pairs from any image using a frequency-separated perturbation in the initial-noise space to alter appearance while preserving pose, coarse shape, and scene layout. A dual U-Net with full-source conditioning and a noise-free reference encoder is trained to enable direct inter-object alignment, zero-shot inference without per-object finetuning, and lightweight iterative refinement. It also introduces SourceBench, a high-quality high-resolution benchmark with more categories and richer interactions.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate superior fidelity, stronger scene preservation, and more natural harmony compared to prior methods, and the method transfers to edits such as subject-driven refinement and face swapping.
AI.conclusion: SourceSwap provides a self-supervised, cross-object alignment capability enabling zero-shot object swaps with high fidelity and natural scene integration, supported by a high-quality benchmark (SourceBench) for evaluation.

title: On the Convergence of Single-Loop Stochastic Bilevel Optimization with Approximate Implicit Differentiation
summary: Stochastic Bilevel Optimization has emerged as a fundamental framework for meta-learning and hyperparameter optimization. Despite the practical prevalence of single-loop algorithms--which update lower and upper variables concurrently--their theoretical understanding, particularly in the stochastic regime, remains significantly underdeveloped compared to their multi-loop counterparts. Existing analyses often yield suboptimal convergence rates or obscure the critical dependence on the lower-level condition number $κ$, frequently burying it within generic Lipschitz constants. In this paper, we bridge this gap by providing a refined convergence analysis of the Single-loop Stochastic Approximate Implicit Differentiation (SSAID) algorithm.
