We prove that SSAID achieves an $ε$-stationary point with an oracle complexity of $\mathcal{O}(κ^7 ε^{-2})$. Our result is noteworthy in two aspects: (i) it matches the optimal $\mathcal{O}(ε^{-2})$ rate of state-of-the-art multi-loop methods (e.g., stocBiO) while maintaining the computational efficiency of a single-loop update; and (ii) it provides the first explicit, fine-grained characterization of the $κ$-dependence for stochastic AID-based single-loop methods. This work demonstrates that SSAID is not merely a heuristic approach, but admits a rigorous theoretical foundation with convergence guarantees competitive with mainstream multi-loop frameworks.

AI.tldr: SSAID achieves an ε-stationary point for stochastic bilevel optimization with oracle complexity O(κ^7 ε^{-2}), matching multi-loop rates while preserving single-loop updates; provides explicit κ-dependence in the stochastic AID setting.
AI.motivation: There is a gap in theoretical understanding of single-loop stochastic AID methods for bilevel optimization, with suboptimal rates and opaque κ-dependence.
AI.method: Refined convergence analysis of the SSAID algorithm in the stochastic bilevel setting, deriving explicit O(κ^7 ε^{-2}) complexity by isolating lower-level condition number κ in the analysis and comparing against multi-loop methods (e.g., stocBiO).
AI.result: The analysis yields an oracle complexity bound of O(κ^7 ε^{-2}) for attaining an ε-stationary point. This matches the ε^{-2} rate of state-of-the-art multi-loop methods while maintaining the efficiency of single-loop updates, and it is the first to give a fine-grained κ-dependence for stochastic AID-based single-loop methods.
AI.conclusion: SSAID provides a rigorous theoretical foundation with convergence guarantees competitive with mainstream multi-loop bilevel optimization frameworks, demonstrating that single-loop stochastic AID can achieve optimal rates with explicit κ-dependence.

title: CIRCLE: A Framework for Evaluating AI from a Real-World Lens
summary: This paper proposes CIRCLE, a six-stage, lifecycle-based framework to bridge the reality gap between model-centric performance metrics and AI's materialized outcomes in deployment. While existing frameworks like MLOps focus on system stability and benchmarks measure abstract capabilities, decision-makers outside the AI stack lack systematic evidence about the behavior of AI technologies under real-world user variability and constraints. CIRCLE operationalizes the Validation phase of TEVV (Test, Evaluation, Verification, and Validation) by formalizing the translation of stakeholder concerns outside the stack into measurable signals. Unlike participatory design, which often remains localized, or algorithmic audits, which are often retrospective, CIRCLE provides a structured, prospective protocol for linking context-sensitive qualitative insights to scalable quantitative metrics. By integrating methods such as field testing, red teaming, and longitudinal studies into a coordinated pipeline, CIRCLE produces systematic knowledge: evidence that is comparable across sites yet sensitive to local context. This can enable governance based on materialized downstream effects rather than theoretical capabilities.

AI.tldr: CIRCLE is a six-stage lifecycle framework that links model-centric metrics to real-world deployment outcomes by translating stakeholder concerns into measurable signals within TEVV Validation, using field testing, red teaming, and longitudinal studies to create comparable, context-sensitive evidence for governance.
AI.motivation: To close the gap between abstract AI capabilities and materialized downstream effects, addressing the reality that traditional benchmarks and MLOps focus on internal or theoretical aspects and may overlook deployment-context variability.
AI.method: A six-stage lifecycle framework (CIRCLE) that formalizes Validation in TEVV, translating stakeholder concerns outside the AI stack into measurable signals. It combines field testing, red teaming, and longitudinal studies in a coordinated pipeline to yield evidence that is comparable across sites yet locally contextualized.
AI.result: Produces systematic knowledge: evidence that is both comparable across sites and sensitive to local context, enabling governance based on materialized downstream effects rather than solely on theoretical capabilities.
AI.conclusion: CIRCLE provides a proactive, structured protocol to link qualitative, context-sensitive insights with scalable quantitative metrics, enabling governance and risk assessment of AI deployments by validating behavior under real-world conditions.

title: Learning to Build: Autonomous Robotic Assembly of Stable Structures Without Predefined Plans
summary: This paper presents a novel autonomous robotic assembly framework for constructing stable structures without relying on predefined architectural blueprints. Instead of following fixed plans, construction tasks are defined through targets and obstacles, allowing the system to adapt more flexibly to environmental uncertainty and variations during the building process. A reinforcement learning (RL) policy, trained using deep Q-learning with successor features, serves as the decision-making component. As a proof of concept, we evaluate the approach on a benchmark of 15 2D robotic assembly tasks of discrete block construction. Experiments using a real-world closed-loop robotic setup demonstrate the feasibility of the method and its ability to handle construction noise. The results suggest that our framework offers a promising direction for more adaptable and robust robotic construction in real-world environments.

AI.tldr: An autonomous robotic assembly framework using reinforcement learning with successor features to construct stable structures without predefined blueprints, validated on a 15-task 2D block-assembly benchmark in real-world closed-loop conditions.
AI.motivation: Address environmental uncertainty and variability in robotic construction by replacing fixed architectural plans with target/obstacle-based task definitions, enabling flexible adaptation.
AI.method: Utilizes deep Q-learning with successor features as the decision-making backbone; tasks defined by targets and obstacles; 15 discrete 2D block assembly tasks; real-world closed-loop robot setup; evaluates robustness to construction noise.
AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility and robustness: can handle construction noise, passes 15-task benchmark, real-world validation supports adaptability of the framework.
AI.conclusion: Suggests a promising direction for adaptive and robust robotic construction in real environments; future work may extend to 3D, more complex assemblies, and generalization across tasks.

title: HiDrop: Hierarchical Vision Token Reduction in MLLMs via Late Injection, Concave Pyramid Pruning, and Early Exit
summary: The quadratic computational cost of processing vision tokens in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) hinders their widespread adoption. While progressive vision token pruning offers a promising solution, current methods misinterpret shallow layer functions and use rigid schedules, which fail to unlock the full efficiency potential. To address these issues, we propose HiDrop, a framework that aligns token pruning with the true hierarchical function of MLLM layers. HiDrop features two key innovations: (1) Late Injection, which bypasses passive shallow layers to introduce visual tokens exactly where active fusion begins; and (2) Concave Pyramid Pruning with an Early Exit mechanism to dynamically adjust pruning rates across middle and deep layers. This process is optimized via an inter-layer similarity measure and a differentiable top-k operator. To ensure practical efficiency, HiDrop further incorporates persistent positional encoding, FlashAttention-compatible token selection, and parallel decoupling of vision computation to eliminate hidden overhead associated with dynamic token reduction. Extensive experiments show that HiDrop compresses about 90% visual tokens while matching the original performance and accelerating training by 1.72 times. Our work not only sets a new state-of-the-art for efficient MLLM training and inference but also provides valuable insights into the hierarchical nature of multimodal fusion. The code is released at https://github.com/EIT-NLP/HiDrop.

AI.tldr: HiDrop introduces Late Injection and Concave Pyramid Pruning with Early Exit to align token pruning with the true hierarchical fusion of vision tokens in MLLMs, enabling aggressive pruning (~90%) while preserving performance and achieving 1.72x training speedup; supported by differentiable top-k, inter-layer similarity, and practical optimizations; code released.
AI.motivation: MLLMs incur quadratic costs from processing vision tokens. Existing progressive pruning methods misinterpret shallow layer roles and rely on fixed schedules, limiting efficiency gains. There is a need to align pruning with the actual hierarchical fusion behavior across model layers to unlock deeper efficiency.
AI.method: Two core innovations: (1) Late Injection: bypasses passive shallow layers and injects visual tokens exactly where active fusion begins; (2) Concave Pyramid Pruning with an Early Exit: dynamically adjusts pruning rates across middle and deep layers, guided by an inter-layer similarity measure and a differentiable top-k operator. Supporting techniques include persistent positional encoding, FlashAttention-compatible token selection, and parallel decoupling of vision computation to remove hidden overhead of dynamic token reduction.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show ~90% reduction of visual tokens with no loss in original performance, and a training speedup of about 1.72x. Claims include state-of-the-art efficiency for efficient MLLM training and inference.
AI.conclusion: HiDrop provides a principled alignment between token pruning and layer function in MLLMs, yielding substantial efficiency gains and offering insights into hierarchical multimodal fusion. Code is released for reproducibility.

title: FlexGuard: Continuous Risk Scoring for Strictness-Adaptive LLM Content Moderation
summary: Ensuring the safety of LLM-generated content is essential for real-world deployment. Most existing guardrail models formulate moderation as a fixed binary classification task, implicitly assuming a fixed definition of harmfulness. In practice, enforcement strictness - how conservatively harmfulness is defined and enforced - varies across platforms and evolves over time, making binary moderators brittle under shifting requirements. We first introduce FlexBench, a strictness-adaptive LLM moderation benchmark that enables controlled evaluation under multiple strictness regimes. Experiments on FlexBench reveal substantial cross-strictness inconsistency in existing moderators: models that perform well under one regime can degrade substantially under others, limiting their practical usability. To address this, we propose FlexGuard, an LLM-based moderator that outputs a calibrated continuous risk score reflecting risk severity and supports strictness-specific decisions via thresholding. We train FlexGuard via risk-alignment optimization to improve score-severity consistency and provide practical threshold selection strategies to adapt to target strictness at deployment. Experiments on FlexBench and public benchmarks demonstrate that FlexGuard achieves higher moderation accuracy and substantially improved robustness under varying strictness. We release the source code and data to support reproducibility.

AI.tldr: Introduces FlexBench, a strictness-adaptive LLM moderation benchmark, and FlexGuard, an LLM-based moderator that outputs a calibrated continuous risk score for varying enforcement strictness; demonstrates improved accuracy and robustness across strictness regimes; provides open-source code/data.
AI.motivation: Binary moderation is brittle under shifting enforcement strictness across platforms and over time; a continuous risk score with thresholding can adapt to different strictness requirements.
AI.method: 1) FlexBench: a benchmark enabling evaluation across multiple strictness regimes. 2) FlexGuard: an LLM moderator producing a calibrated risk score; strictness-specific decisions via thresholding. 3) Risk-alignment optimization to improve score-severity consistency; practical threshold selection strategies for deployment.
AI.result: FlexGuard achieves higher moderation accuracy and substantially improved robustness under varying strictness on FlexBench and public benchmarks; cross-regressions between regimes are mitigated; code/data released.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, risk-scored moderation is effective for real-world deployments with evolving enforcement standards; FlexGuard provides practical deployment guidance and reproducibility.

title: Human or Machine? A Preliminary Turing Test for Speech-to-Speech Interaction
summary: The pursuit of human-like conversational agents has long been guided by the Turing test. For modern speech-to-speech (S2S) systems, a critical yet unanswered question is whether they can converse like humans. To tackle this, we conduct the first Turing test for S2S systems, collecting 2,968 human judgments on dialogues between 9 state-of-the-art S2S systems and 28 human participants. Our results deliver a clear finding: no existing evaluated S2S system passes the test, revealing a significant gap in human-likeness. To diagnose this failure, we develop a fine-grained taxonomy of 18 human-likeness dimensions and crowd-annotate our collected dialogues accordingly. Our analysis shows that the bottleneck is not semantic understanding but stems from paralinguistic features, emotional expressivity, and conversational persona. Furthermore, we find that off-the-shelf AI models perform unreliably as Turing test judges. In response, we propose an interpretable model that leverages the fine-grained human-likeness ratings and delivers accurate and transparent human-vs-machine discrimination, offering a powerful tool for automatic human-likeness evaluation. Our work establishes the first human-likeness evaluation for S2S systems and moves beyond binary outcomes to enable detailed diagnostic insights, paving the way for human-like improvements in conversational AI systems.

AI.tldr: First human-likeness Turing test for speech-to-speech systems; no system passes; identifies 18-dimension bottlenecks in paralinguistics, emotion, and persona; unreliable AI judges; introduces an interpretable diagnostic model for human-vs-machine discrimination and diagnostic evaluation.
AI.motivation: To move beyond binary pass/fail in evaluating S2S systems and establish a diagnostic, human-likeness-centric evaluation framework for conversational AI.
AI.method: 1) Collect 2,968 judgments on dialogues between 9 state-of-the-art S2S systems and 28 human participants; 2) develop a fine-grained 18-dimension taxonomy of human-likeness; 3) crowd-annotate dialogues; 4) analyze bottlenecks; 5) assess off-the-shelf AI models as judges; 6) propose an interpretable model leveraging ratings for accurate human-vs-machine discrimination.
AI.result: No evaluated S2S system passes the Turing test; bottlenecks lie in paralinguistic features, emotional expressivity, and conversational persona; off-the-shelf AI judges are unreliable; an interpretable model using fine-grained ratings achieves accurate discrimination between human and machine.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes the first human-likeness evaluation for S2S systems, provides diagnostic insights beyond binary outcomes, and offers a practical tool to guide improvements toward more human-like conversational AI.

title: Enhancing Vision-Language Navigation with Multimodal Event Knowledge from Real-World Indoor Tour Videos
summary: Vision-Language Navigation (VLN) agents often struggle with long-horizon reasoning in unseen environments, particularly when facing ambiguous, coarse-grained instructions. While recent advances use knowledge graph to enhance reasoning, the potential of multimodal event knowledge inspired by human episodic memory remains underexplored. In this work, we propose an event-centric knowledge enhancement strategy for automated process knowledge mining and feature fusion to solve coarse-grained instruction and long-horizon reasoning in VLN task. First, we construct YE-KG, the first large-scale multimodal spatiotemporal knowledge graph, with over 86k nodes and 83k edges, derived from real-world indoor videos. By leveraging multimodal large language models (i.e., LLaVa, GPT4), we extract unstructured video streams into structured semantic-action-effect events to serve as explicit episodic memory. Second, we introduce STE-VLN, which integrates the above graph into VLN models via a Coarse-to-Fine Hierarchical Retrieval mechanism. This allows agents to retrieve causal event sequences and dynamically fuse them with egocentric visual observations. Experiments on REVERIE, R2R, and R2R-CE benchmarks demonstrate the efficiency of our event-centric strategy, outperforming state-of-the-art approaches across diverse action spaces. Our data and code are available on the project website https://sites.google.com/view/y-event-kg/.

AI.tldr: Proposes YE-KG, a large multimodal spatiotemporal knowledge graph, and STE-VLN, a coarse-to-fine retrieval-based VLN framework that fuses episodic event memory with egocentric perception; achieves SOTA on REVERIE, R2R, R2R-CE; code/data released.
AI.motivation: VLN agents struggle with long-horizon reasoning and coarse instructions in unseen environments; leveraging multimodal episodic memory can provide explicit causal event context to guide navigation.
AI.method: Construct YE-KG from real indoor videos: 86k nodes, 83k edges; convert video streams into semantic-action-effect events via LLaVa, GPT-4; integrate into VLN via STE-VLN using a Coarse-to-Fine Hierarchical Retrieval to fetch event sequences and fuse with visual observations.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods on REVERIE, R2R, R2R-CE across varying action spaces; demonstrates efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Event-centric multimodal knowledge enhances long-horizon VLN reasoning; the proposed knowledge graph and retrieval framework open new avenues for memory-informed navigation; dataset and code provided.

title: EgoGraph: Temporal Knowledge Graph for Egocentric Video Understanding
summary: Ultra-long egocentric videos spanning multiple days present significant challenges for video understanding. Existing approaches still rely on fragmented local processing and limited temporal modeling, restricting their ability to reason over such extended sequences. To address these limitations, we introduce EgoGraph, a training-free and dynamic knowledge-graph construction framework that explicitly encodes long-term, cross-entity dependencies in egocentric video streams. EgoGraph employs a novel egocentric schema that unifies the extraction and abstraction of core entities, such as people, objects, locations, and events, and structurally reasons about their attributes and interactions, yielding a significantly richer and more coherent semantic representation than traditional clip-based video models. Crucially, we develop a temporal relational modeling strategy that captures temporal dependencies across entities and accumulates stable long-term memory over multiple days, enabling complex temporal reasoning. Extensive experiments on the EgoLifeQA and EgoR1-bench benchmarks demonstrate that EgoGraph achieves state-of-the-art performance on long-term video question answering, validating its effectiveness as a new paradigm for ultra-long egocentric video understanding.

AI.tldr: Introduces EgoGraph, a training-free dynamic knowledge-graph framework for ultra-long egocentric video understanding, enabling long-term cross-entity reasoning and memory across days; achieves state-of-the-art on EgoLifeQA and EgoR1-bench.
AI.motivation: Overcome fragmentation and limited temporal modeling in day-to-day egocentric videos to support long-term reasoning.
AI.method: Constructs an egocentric schema unifying extraction/abstraction of core entities (people, objects, locations, events); builds a dynamic knowledge graph without training; performs structural reasoning over attributes/interactions; employs temporal relational modeling to capture cross-entity dependencies and accumulate long-term memory across days.
AI.result: Experiments on EgoLifeQA and EgoR1-bench showing state-of-the-art performance on long-term video QA.
AI.conclusion: Presents a new paradigm for ultra-long egocentric video understanding with potential for robust long-term reasoning; demonstrates effectiveness through empirical results.

title: FedRot-LoRA: Mitigating Rotational Misalignment in Federated LoRA
summary: Federated LoRA provides a communication-efficient mechanism for fine-tuning large language models on decentralized data. In practice, however, a discrepancy between the factor-wise averaging used to preserve low rank and the mathematically correct aggregation of local updates can cause significant aggregation error and unstable training. We argue that a major source of this problem is rotational misalignment, arising from the rotational invariance of low-rank factorizations -- semantically equivalent updates can be represented in different latent subspaces across clients since $(B_i R_i)(R_i^\top A_i) = B_i A_i$. When such misaligned factors are averaged directly, they interfere destructively and degrade the global update. To address this issue, we propose FedRot-LoRA, a federated LoRA framework that aligns client updates via orthogonal transformations prior to aggregation. This alignment preserves the semantic update while reducing cross-client subspace mismatch, without increasing communication cost or restricting model expressivity. We provide a convergence analysis that examines the aggregation error induced by factor-wise averaging and shows how rotational alignment yields a tighter upper bound on this error. Extensive experiments on natural language understanding and generative tasks demonstrate that FedRot-LoRA consistently outperforms existing federated LoRA baselines across a range of heterogeneity levels and LoRA ranks.

AI.tldr: FedRot-LoRA aligns per-client low-rank updates using orthogonal rotations to reduce subspace misalignment, improving aggregation and convergence in federated LoRA without extra communication.
AI.motivation: Rotational invariance in low-rank factorizations causes semantically equivalent updates to project into different latent subspaces across clients, making direct factor-wise averaging error-prone and destabilizing training.
AI.method: Introduce orthogonal rotations for each client's LoRA factors before aggregation; align B_i and A_i factors via a rotation R_i so that (B_i R_i)(R_i^T A_i) matches across clients; justify that this preserves expressivity and does not increase communication; provide convergence analysis showing tighter aggregation-error bounds; empirical evaluation across NLP tasks under varying heterogeneity and LoRA ranks.
AI.result: FedRot-LoRA consistently outperforms baselines on natural language understanding and generative tasks across heterogeneity levels and LoRA ranks.
AI.conclusion: Orthogonal rotational alignment mitigates cross-client subspace mismatch in federated LoRA, enabling more stable and accurate aggregation without extra cost, with theoretical convergence guarantees and strong empirical gains.

title: Bi-level RL-Heuristic Optimization for Real-world Winter Road Maintenance
summary: Winter road maintenance is critical for ensuring public safety and reducing environmental impacts, yet existing methods struggle to manage large-scale routing problems effectively and mostly reply on human decision. This study presents a novel, scalable bi-level optimization framework, validated on real operational data on UK strategic road networks (M25, M6, A1), including interconnected local road networks in surrounding areas for vehicle traversing, as part of the highway operator's efforts to solve existing planning challenges. At the upper level, a reinforcement learning (RL) agent strategically partitions the road network into manageable clusters and optimally allocates resources from multiple depots. At the lower level, a multi-objective vehicle routing problem (VRP) is solved within each cluster, minimizing the maximum vehicle travel time and total carbon emissions. Unlike existing approaches, our method handles large-scale, real-world networks efficiently, explicitly incorporating vehicle-specific constraints, depot capacities, and road segment requirements. Results demonstrate significant improvements, including balanced workloads, reduced maximum travel times below the targeted two-hour threshold, lower emissions, and substantial cost savings. This study illustrates how advanced AI-driven bi-level optimization can directly enhance operational decision-making in real-world transportation and logistics.

AI.tldr: A scalable bi-level optimization for winter road maintenance using RL-based network partitioning and multi-objective VRP within clusters; demonstrates real-world scalability on UK road networks with results showing balanced workloads, max travel time below 2 hours, lower emissions, and cost savings.
AI.motivation: Address large-scale, realistic winter road maintenance routing challenges that current methods struggle to scale and often rely on human decision making; need an automated, efficient, and environmentally-conscious approach.
AI.method: A bi-level framework: an upper-level reinforcement learning (RL) agent partitions the road network into clusters and allocates resources from multiple depots; a lower-level multi-objective vehicle routing problem (VRP) is solved within each cluster, optimizing for the maximum vehicle travel time and total carbon emissions, while incorporating vehicle constraints, depot capacities, and road segment requirements. Validated on real UK networks (M25, M6, A1) and surrounding local roads.
AI.result: Claims significant improvements: balanced workloads; maximum travel time reduced to below a two-hour threshold; lower emissions; substantial cost savings; scalable to large real-world networks with explicit constraints.
AI.conclusion: AI-driven bi-level optimization can directly enhance operational decision-making in transportation/logistics, with demonstrated potential for real-world applicability and environmental benefits; invites further investigation into deployment, robustness, and generalizability.

title: Learning Robust Control Policies for Inverted Pose on Miniature Blimp Robots
summary: The ability to achieve and maintain inverted poses is essential for unlocking the full agility of miniature blimp robots (MBRs). However, developing reliable control methods for MBRs remains challenging due to their complex and underactuated dynamics. To address this challenge, we propose a novel framework that enables robust control policy learning for inverted pose on MBRs. The proposed framework operates through three core stages: First, a high-fidelity three-dimensional (3D) simulation environment was constructed, which was calibrated against real-world MBR motion data to ensure accurate replication of inverted-state dynamics. Second, a robust policy for MBR inverted control was trained within the simulation environment via a domain randomization strategy and a modified Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3) algorithm. Third, a mapping layer was designed to bridge the sim-to-real gap for the learned policy deployment. Comprehensive evaluations in the simulation environment demonstrate that the learned policy achieves a higher success rate compared to the energy-shaping controller. Furthermore, experimental results confirm that the learned policy with a mapping layer enables an MBR to achieve and maintain a fully upside-down pose in real-world settings.

AI.tldr: Proposes a sim-to-real framework for inverted pose control in miniature blimp robots, using calibrated 3D simulation, domain randomization with a modified TD3 algorithm, and a mapping layer; outperforms an energy-shaping controller in simulation and achieves real-world inverted pose.
AI.motivation: Invert and maintain poses to unlock greater agility in MBRs, addressing the challenges of underactuated and complex dynamics with a robust learning-based control approach.
AI.method: A three-stage framework: (1) build and calibrate a high-fidelity 3D simulator using real MBR motion data; (2) train a robust inverted-control policy in simulation using domain randomization and a modified Twin Delayed DDPG; (3) design a mapping layer to bridge sim-to-real for deploying the policy.
AI.result: Simulation shows higher success rate for the learned policy compared to an energy-shaping controller; real-world experiments demonstrate that the policy with the mapping layer can achieve and maintain a fully upside-down pose.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables robust inverted-pose control for MBRs and successfully transfers from simulation to reality via a mapping layer; the approach improves control robustness over traditional energy-based methods.

title: Can Unified Generation and Understanding Models Maintain Semantic Equivalence Across Different Output Modalities?
summary: Unified Multimodal Large Language Models (U-MLLMs) integrate understanding and generation within a single architecture. However, existing evaluations typically assess these capabilities separately, overlooking semantic equivalence, i.e., the ability to manifest consistent reasoning results regardless of the output modality. In this work, we investigate whether current U-MLLMs satisfy this premise. We observe that while models demonstrate robust textual reasoning, they fail to maintain semantic equivalence when required to render the same results in the image modality. To rigorously diagnose this discrepancy, we introduce VGUBench, a framework to decouple reasoning logic from generation fidelity. VGUBench comprises three diagnostic tasks: (1)Textual Generative Understanding, establishing a baseline for reasoning accuracy in textual response; (2)Visual Generative Understanding, evaluating the ability to generate visual responses that represent the correct answer; and (3)a Visual Rendering control task, which assesses the ability to directly render explicit visual descriptions into images without complex reasoning. Our evaluation reveals a significant disparity: despite strong performance in textual understanding and visual rendering, U-MLLMs exhibit a marked performance collapse when required to generate visual answers to questions. Furthermore, we find a negligible correlation between visual answering performance and basic rendering quality. These results suggest that the failure stems not from insufficient generation fidelity, but from a breakdown in cross-modal semantic alignment. We provide diagnostic insights to address this challenge in future Unified Generation and Understanding Models.

AI.tldr: VGUBench introduces diagnostic tasks to evaluate semantic equivalence in Unified Multimodal LLMs, revealing cross-modal semantic alignment gaps despite strong textual reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing U-MLLM evaluations focus on individual modalities, neglecting whether the same reasoning outputs are semantically equivalent across text and image modalities. This gap undermines true cross-modal understanding.
AI.method: VGUBench decouples reasoning from rendering fidelity via three diagnostic tasks: (1) Textual Generative Understanding (text-based reasoning accuracy), (2) Visual Generative Understanding (ability to generate visuals that reflect correct answers), and (3) Visual Rendering control (directly rendering explicit visual descriptions in images).
AI.result: Models exhibit strong textual reasoning and rendering capabilities but show a significant collapse when required to generate visual answers; rendering quality poorly predicts visual answering performance, indicating a breakdown in cross-modal semantic alignment rather than generation fidelity.
AI.conclusion: Address cross-modal semantic alignment in Unified Generation and Understanding Models; use VGUBench insights to guide future architecture and training strategies for robust cross-modal reasoning.

title: Selective Denoising Diffusion Model for Time Series Anomaly Detection
summary: Time series anomaly detection (TSAD) has been an important area of research for decades, with reconstruction-based methods, mostly based on generative models, gaining popularity and demonstrating success. Diffusion models have recently attracted attention due to their advanced generative capabilities. Existing diffusion-based methods for TSAD rely on a conditional strategy, which reconstructs input instances from white noise with the aid of the conditioner. However, this poses challenges in accurately reconstructing the normal parts, resulting in suboptimal detection performance. In response, we propose a novel diffusion-based method, named AnomalyFilter, which acts as a selective filter that only denoises anomaly parts in the instance while retaining normal parts. To build such a filter, we mask Gaussian noise during the training phase and conduct the denoising process without adding noise to the instances. The synergy of the two simple components greatly enhances the performance of naive diffusion models. Extensive experiments on five datasets demonstrate that AnomalyFilter achieves notably low reconstruction error on normal parts, providing empirical support for its effectiveness in anomaly detection. AnomalyFilter represents a pioneering approach that focuses on the noise design of diffusion models specifically tailored for TSAD.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based TSAD method, AnomalyFilter, selectively denoises anomaly parts by masking noise during training and preventing noise addition during denoising, yielding low reconstruction error on normal parts and improved anomaly detection across five datasets.
AI.motivation: TSAD using generative diffusion struggles to accurately reconstruct normal regions with conditional diffusion. There is a need to tailor the diffusion noise design to emphasize anomalies while preserving normals.
AI.method: Introduce a selective denoising mechanism (AnomalyFilter) that acts as a filter. Train by masking Gaussian noise; perform denoising without adding noise to the inputs, so only anomalous parts are denoised while normal parts remain largely intact.
AI.result: On five datasets, AnomalyFilter achieves notably lower reconstruction error on normal parts and demonstrates empirical effectiveness, outperforming naive diffusion baselines.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion models for TSAD benefit from noise design tailored to anomaly emphasis. AnomalyFilter represents a pioneering approach by focusing on selective noise treatment to improve detection performance.

title: Artificial Agency Program: Curiosity, compression, and communication in agents
comment: This is a working draft. Feedback and criticism is most welcome
summary: This paper presents the Artificial Agency Program (AAP), a position and research agenda for building AI systems as reality embedded, resource-bounded agents whose development is driven by curiosity-as-learning-progress under physical and computational constraints. The central thesis is that AI is most useful when treated as part of an extended human--tool system that increases sensing, understanding, and actuation capability while reducing friction at the interface between people, tools, and environments. The agenda unifies predictive compression, intrinsic motivation, empowerment and control, interface quality (unification), and language/self-communication as selective information bottlenecks. We formulate these ideas as a falsifiable program with explicit costs, staged experiments, and a concrete multimodal tokenized testbed in which an agent allocates limited budget among observation, action, and deliberation. The aim is to provide a conceptual and experimental framework that connects intrinsic motivation, information theory, thermodynamics, bounded rationality, and modern reasoning systems

AI.tldr: AAP outlines a falsifiable research program to build reality-embedded, resource-bounded AI agents driven by curiosity, framing AI as an extended human-tool system and proposing a multimodal testbed to study allocation of limited budgets among observation, action, and deliberation.
AI.motivation: Increase AI usefulness by reducing interface friction and integrating sensing, understanding, and actuation within physical and computational constraints; unify theories (predictive compression, intrinsic motivation, empowerment, interface quality, and bottlenecks) into an actionable agenda.
AI.method: Formulates a falsifiable program with explicit costs, staged experiments, and a tokenized multimodal testbed; frames agent as allocating budget among observation, action, and deliberation; integrates information theory, thermodynamics, bounded rationality, and modern reasoning systems.
AI.result: Conceptual framework and experimental plan; no empirical results reported in the abstract; provides a structured roadmap and testbed design for future work.
AI.conclusion: Aims to connect intrinsic motivation, information theory, thermodynamics, bounded rationality, and modern reasoning systems within a unified, testable agenda for AI development.

title: Autonomous Inspection of Power Line Insulators with UAV on an Unmapped Transmission Tower
summary: This paper introduces an online inspection algorithm that enables an autonomous UAV to fly around a transmission tower and obtain detailed inspection images without a prior map of the tower. Our algorithm relies on camera-LiDAR sensor fusion for online detection and localization of insulators. In particular, the algorithm is based on insulator detection using a convolutional neural network, projection of LiDAR points onto the image, and filtering them using the bounding boxes. The detection pipeline is coupled with several proposed insulator localization methods based on DBSCAN, RANSAC, and PCA algorithms. The performance of the proposed online inspection algorithm and camera-LiDAR sensor fusion pipeline is demonstrated through simulation and real-world flights. In simulation, we showed that our single-flight inspection strategy can save up to 24 % of total inspection time, compared to the two-flight strategy of scanning the tower and afterwards visiting the inspection waypoints in the optimal way. In a real-world experiment, the best performing proposed method achieves a mean horizontal and vertical localization error for the insulator of 0.16 +- 0.08 m and 0.16 +- 0.11 m, respectively. Compared to the most relevant approach, the proposed method achieves more than an order of magnitude lower variance in horizontal insulator localization error.

AI.tldr: Online UAV inspection around transmission towers using camera–LiDAR fusion for real-time insulator detection and localization, achieving accurate insulator localization with DBSCAN/RANSAC/PCA, and a single-flight strategy that reduces inspection time.
AI.motivation: Improve autonomous inspection of transmission towers without pre-built tower maps by enabling online detection and localization of insulators to guide inspection paths.
AI.method: CNN-based insulator detection; projection of LiDAR points into the image; filtering LiDAR points using CNN-based bounding boxes; insulator localization using DBSCAN, RANSAC, and PCA; evaluation in simulation and real-world flights; single-flight inspection strategy.
AI.result: Single-flight inspection saves up to 24% of total inspection time versus a two-flight approach; real-world localization errors for insulators: mean horizontal 0.16±0.08 m and vertical 0.16±0.11 m; the method achieves over an order of magnitude lower variance in horizontal localization error compared to a relevant baseline.
AI.conclusion: Camera–LiDAR fusion with CNN-based detection and multiple localization techniques enables efficient online tower inspection with accurate insulator localization, supporting single-flight planning and robust real-world performance.

title: A Difference-in-Difference Approach to Detecting AI-Generated Images
summary: Diffusion models are able to produce AI-generated images that are almost indistinguishable from real ones. This raises concerns about their potential misuse and poses substantial challenges for detecting them. Many existing detectors rely on reconstruction error -- the difference between the input image and its reconstructed version -- as the basis for distinguishing real from fake images. However, these detectors become less effective as modern AI-generated images become increasingly similar to real ones. To address this challenge, we propose a novel difference-in-difference method. Instead of directly using the reconstruction error (a first-order difference), we compute the difference in reconstruction error -- a second-order difference -- for variance reduction and improving detection accuracy. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves strong generalization performance, enabling reliable detection of AI-generated images in the era of generative AI.

AI.tldr: Second-order difference detector for AI-generated images using the difference in reconstruction errors to improve robustness and generalization.
AI.motivation: Current detectors relying on reconstruction error degrade as generated images increasingly resemble real ones; a variance-reducing, second-order statistic may enhance detection in the diffusion-model era.
AI.method: Introduce a difference-in-difference approach: compute reconstruction error (first-order) and then compute the difference between reconstruction errors (second-order) as the detection statistic, aiming for variance reduction and improved accuracy; validated with extensive experiments showing generalization.
AI.result: Empirical results indicate strong generalization and reliable detection of AI-generated images across diverse generative models, outperforming baseline first-order reconstruction-error detectors.
AI.conclusion: The second-order difference statistic offers a robust detector for AI-generated images in modern generative AI; future work should explore broader datasets, adversarial robustness, and theoretical analysis of the variance-reduction mechanism.

title: Disentangled Mode-Specific Representations for Tensor Time Series via Contrastive Learning
summary: Multi-mode tensor time series (TTS) can be found in many domains, such as search engines and environmental monitoring systems. Learning representations of a TTS benefits various applications, but it is also challenging since the complexities inherent in the tensor hinder the realization of rich representations. In this paper, we propose a novel representation learning method designed specifically for TTS, namely MoST. Specifically, MoST uses a tensor slicing approach to reduce the complexity of the TTS structure and learns representations that can be disentangled into individual non-temporal modes. Each representation captures mode-specific features, which are the relationship between variables within the same mode, and mode-invariant features, which are in common in representations of different modes. We employ a contrastive learning framework to learn parameters; the loss function comprises two parts intended to learn representation in a mode-specific way and mode-invariant way, effectively exploiting disentangled representations as augmentations. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets show that MoST consistently outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in terms of classification and forecasting accuracy. Code is available at https://github.com/KoheiObata/MoST.

AI.tldr: MoST proposes tensor-slicing with disentangled, mode-specific and mode-invariant representations for multi-mode tensor time series, learned via contrastive learning, achieving improved classification and forecasting over SOTA.
AI.motivation: Multi-mode tensor time series (TTS) exhibit complex, high-dimensional structures. Existing methods struggle to efficiently capture both mode-specific relationships and shared, mode-invariant information, hindering rich representations for downstream tasks.
AI.method: MoST uses tensor slicing to reduce TTS complexity and learns representations that can be disentangled into mode-specific and mode-invariant components. It employs a contrastive learning framework with a two-part loss to encourage mode-specific and mode-invariant representations, treating disentangled representations as augmentations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real-world datasets show MoST consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in classification and forecasting accuracy. Code is available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: The proposed tensor slicing-based disentangled representation learning for TTS effectively balances capturing mode-specific relationships and shared mode-invariant features, yielding superior predictive performance and offering a scalable framework for complex TTS data.

title: Recycling Failures: Salvaging Exploration in RLVR via Fine-Grained Off-Policy Guidance
summary: Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for enhancing the complex reasoning capabilities of Large Reasoning Models. However, standard outcome-based supervision suffers from a critical limitation that penalizes trajectories that are largely correct but fail due to several missteps as heavily as completely erroneous ones. This coarse feedback signal causes the model to discard valuable largely correct rollouts, leading to a degradation in rollout diversity that prematurely narrows the exploration space. Process Reward Models have demonstrated efficacy in providing reliable step-wise verification for test-time scaling, naively integrating these signals into RLVR as dense rewards proves ineffective.Prior methods attempt to introduce off-policy guided whole-trajectory replacement that often outside the policy model's distribution, but still fail to utilize the largely correct rollouts generated by the model itself and thus do not effectively mitigate the narrowing of the exploration space. To address these issues, we propose SCOPE (Step-wise Correction for On-Policy Exploration), a novel framework that utilizes Process Reward Models to pinpoint the first erroneous step in suboptimal rollouts and applies fine-grained, step-wise off-policy rectification. By applying precise refinement on partially correct rollout, our method effectively salvages partially correct trajectories and increases diversity score by 13.5%, thereby sustaining a broad exploration space. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach establishes new state-of-the-art results, achieving an average accuracy of 46.6% on math reasoning and exhibiting robust generalization with 53.4% accuracy on out-of-distribution reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: A step-wise correction framework (SCOPE) using Process Reward Models to rectify the first erroneous step in rollouts, preserving exploration and boosting math/OOD performance.
AI.motivation: Coarse outcome-based supervision penalizes largely-correct trajectories, reducing rollout diversity and exploration. Reliable step-wise verification via Process Reward Models is needed to sustain exploration and improve reasoning.
AI.method: Identify the first erroneous step in suboptimal rollouts with Process Reward Models and apply fine-grained, off-policy corrections at that step. Salvage partially correct trajectories to maintain diversity, instead of replacing entire trajectories. Evaluate on math reasoning and out-of-distribution reasoning tasks.
AI.result: Diversity score increases by 13.5%. Achieves state-of-the-art results with an average math accuracy of 46.6% and 53.4% accuracy on out-of-distribution reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Step-wise correction via Process Reward Models effectively preserves exploration and improves both in-distribution math reasoning and generalization without requiring full-trajectory replacement.

title: Curriculum Reinforcement Learning for Quadrotor Racing with Random Obstacles
summary: Autonomous drone racing has attracted increasing interest as a research topic for exploring the limits of agile flight. However, existing studies primarily focus on obstacle-free racetracks, while the perception and dynamic challenges introduced by obstacles remain underexplored, often resulting in low success rates and limited robustness in real-world flight. To this end, we propose a novel vision-based curriculum reinforcement learning framework for training a robust controller capable of addressing unseen obstacles in drone racing. We combine multi-stage cu rriculum learning, domain randomization, and a multi-scene updating strategy to address the conflicting challenges of obstacle avoidance and gate traversal. Our end-to-end control policy is implemented as a single network, allowing high-speed flight of quadrotors in environments with variable obstacles. Both hardware-in-the-loop and real-world experiments demonstrate that our method achieves faster lap times and higher success rates than existing approaches, effectively advancing drone racing in obstacle-rich environments. The video and code are available at: https://github.com/SJTU-ViSYS-team/CRL-Drone-Racing.

AI.tldr: A vision-based curriculum RL framework for obstacle-rich drone racing that uses multi-stage curriculum, domain randomization, and multi-scene updating to train an end-to-end controller capable of handling unseen obstacles, achieving faster laps and higher success rates in hardware-in-the-loop and real-world tests.
AI.motivation: To address perception and dynamic challenges introduced by obstacles in drone racing, which are not well covered by obstacle-free racing methods, and to improve robustness and success rates in real-world scenarios.
AI.method: Proposes a vision-based curriculum reinforcement learning framework. Key components include multi-stage curriculum learning, domain randomization, and a multi-scene updating strategy. The controller is an end-to-end network trained to both avoid obstacles and traverse gates, enabling high-speed flight in obstacle-rich environments. Evaluation includes hardware-in-the-loop and real-world experiments, with a link to code and video.
AI.result: Outperforms existing approaches by delivering faster lap times and higher success rates in obstacle-rich drone racing. Demonstrates robustness to unseen obstacles through hardware-in-the-loop and real-world experiments.
AI.conclusion: The work advances obstacle-rich drone racing by integrating curriculum RL, domain randomization, and multi-scene updating into a single end-to-end policy, improving robustness and performance. Public release of code and video enhances reproducibility and potential adaptation to related autonomous flight tasks.

title: UTPTrack: Towards Simple and Unified Token Pruning for Visual Tracking
summary: One-stream Transformer-based trackers achieve advanced performance in visual object tracking but suffer from significant computational overhead that hinders real-time deployment. While token pruning offers a path to efficiency, existing methods are fragmented. They typically prune the search region, dynamic template, and static template in isolation, overlooking critical inter-component dependencies, which yields suboptimal pruning and degraded accuracy. To address this, we introduce UTPTrack, a simple and Unified Token Pruning framework that, for the first time, jointly compresses all three components. UTPTrack employs an attention-guided, token type-aware strategy to holistically model redundancy, a design that seamlessly supports unified tracking across multimodal and language-guided tasks within a single model. Extensive evaluations on 10 benchmarks demonstrate that UTPTrack achieves a new state-of-the-art in the accuracy-efficiency trade-off for pruning-based trackers, pruning 65.4% of vision tokens in RGB-based tracking and 67.5% in unified tracking while preserving 99.7% and 100.5% of baseline performance, respectively. This strong performance across both RGB and multimodal scenarios underlines its potential as a robust foundation for future research in efficient visual tracking. Code will be released at https://github.com/EIT-NLP/UTPTrack.

AI.tldr: Unified token pruning for one-stream Transformer trackers that jointly compresses search region, dynamic template, and static template. It achieves state-of-the-art accuracy–efficiency trade-off by pruning ~65–68% of tokens while preserving near-baseline or slightly above baseline performance, applicable to RGB and multimodal tracking; code to be released.
AI.motivation: Address the computational overhead of one-stream Transformer trackers and the suboptimal, component-wise pruning approaches that ignore inter-component dependencies; aim to realize holistic efficiency gains without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.method: Introduce UTPTrack: an attention-guided, token type–aware framework that jointly prunes vision tokens, dynamic templates, and static templates. Supports unified tracking across multimodal and language-guided tasks in a single model, with pruning rates of 65.4% (RGB tracking) and 67.5% (unified tracking); evaluated on 10 benchmarks.
AI.result: Achieves a new state-of-the-art accuracy–efficiency trade-off among pruning-based trackers. Prunes 65.4% of vision tokens in RGB-based tracking and 67.5% in unified tracking while preserving 99.7% and 100.5% of baseline performance, respectively.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates strong performance across RGB and multimodal scenarios, underscoring UTPTrack as a robust foundation for future research in efficient visual tracking; code will be released.

title: Optimizer-Induced Low-Dimensional Drift and Transverse Dynamics in Transformer Training
summary: We study the geometry of training trajectories in small transformer models and find that parameter updates organize into a dominant drift direction with transverse residual dynamics. Using uncentered, row-normalized trajectory PCA, we show that a single direction captures a large fraction of cumulative parameter movement early in training, while remaining components encode oscillatory behavior in auxiliary probe performance. Instantaneous gradients exhibit little alignment with this dominant direction, indicating that it arises from accumulated optimizer updates rather than per-batch gradient structure. Comparing AdamW with SGD variants at matched loss levels reveals substantial differences in trajectory geometry: AdamW develops multi-dimensional drift structure, whereas SGD-family optimizers produce nearly colinear parameter evolution and weaker probe dynamics. Reheating selectively perturbs transverse components with minimal effect on the dominant drift coordinate. These findings suggest that optimizer choice shapes the effective dimensionality and structure of learning trajectories beyond what is apparent from loss values alone.

AI.tldr: Dominant drift direction governs early training updates in small transformers, with transverse residual dynamics; optimizer choice (AdamW vs SGD) shapes the trajectory dimensionality and probe-related behavior beyond loss values; reheating perturbs transverse modes with minimal impact on the main drift.
AI.motivation: To understand how optimizer choice influences the geometry of learning trajectories in transformer training, and how this geometry relates to performance, beyond what is captured by loss curves.
AI.method: Analyze parameter trajectories via uncentered, row-normalized trajectory PCA on small transformer training runs. Compare AdamW and SGD-family optimizers at matched loss levels. Examine alignment between instantaneous gradients and the dominant drift, and assess transverse probe dynamics. Apply reheating perturbations to study robustness of the drift vs transverse components.
AI.result: A single dominant drift direction captures a large fraction of cumulative parameter movement early in training; transverse components encode oscillatory behavior in auxiliary probe performance. Instantaneous gradients show little alignment with the dominant direction, indicating accumulation of optimizer updates, not per-batch gradients, drives the drift. AdamW exhibits multi-dimensional drift structure, while SGD-family optimizers produce near-colinear parameter evolution and weaker probe dynamics. Reheating selectively perturbs transverse components with minimal effect on the dominant drift coordinate. Overall, optimizer choice shapes the effective dimensionality and structure of learning trajectories beyond loss values alone.
AI.conclusion: Optimizer choice meaningfully reshapes learning dynamics: learning trajectories are low-dimensional along a dominant drift coordinate with richer transverse modes tied to probe tasks; these geometric patterns offer insight into how optimizers influence training beyond what is reported by loss alone and may inform optimizer selection and training interventions.

title: LemmaBench: A Live, Research-Level Benchmark to Evaluate LLM Capabilities in Mathematics
summary: We present a new approach for benchmarking Large Language Model (LLM) capabilities on research-level mathematics. Existing benchmarks largely rely on static, hand-curated sets of contest or textbook-style problems as proxies for mathematical research. Instead, we establish an updatable benchmark evaluating models directly on the latest research results in mathematics. This consists of an automatic pipeline that extracts lemmas from arXiv and rewrites them into self-contained statements by making all assumptions and required definitions explicit. It results in a benchmark that can be updated regularly with new problems taken directly from human mathematical research, while previous instances can be used for training without compromising future evaluations. We benchmark current state-of-the-art LLMs, which obtain around 10-15$\%$ accuracy in theorem proving (pass@1) depending on the model, showing that there is currently a large margin of progression for LLMs to reach human-level proving capabilities in a research context.

AI.tldr: Presents an updatable, research-level mathematics benchmark for LLMs by automatically extracting and rewriting lemmas from arXiv into explicit, self-contained statements; current state-of-the-art LLMs reach ~10-15% pass@1, indicating substantial room for progress toward human-level mathematical proofing.
AI.motivation: There is a need for benchmarks that reflect research-level mathematical reasoning rather than static contest problems, enabling ongoing assessment as the field advances and tracking progress toward human-level proving capabilities.
AI.method: An automatic pipeline extracts lemmas from arXiv and rewrites them into self-contained statements by making all assumptions and definitions explicit, producing a regularly updatable benchmark; previous instances can be reused for training without compromising future evaluations.
AI.result: State-of-the-art LLMs achieve around 10-15% pass@1 on theorem proving tasks, illustrating a large gap to human-level performance in a research context.
AI.conclusion: An updatable, research-grounded benchmark can drive progress in LLM mathematical reasoning and provide a robust framework to measure improvements over time, while enabling reuse of past instances for training without leakage into evaluation.

title: Geometry-based pneumatic actuators for soft robotics
summary: Soft pneumatic actuators enable safe human-machine interaction with lightweight and powerful applied parts. On the other side, they suffer design limitations as regards complex actuation patterns, including minimum bending radii, multi-states capabilities and structural stability. We present geometry-based pneumatic actuators (GPAs), a design and implementation approach that introduces constraint layers with configurable CNC heat-sealed chambers. The approach achieves predictable deformation, near-zero bending radii, multi-states actuation, and enables customizable and repeatable complex actuated geometries. Mathematical modeling reveals predictable linear angle transformations and validates nonlinear torque-angle relationships across diverse configurations. We demonstrate versatility of the GPAs approach through three applications: a 49 g wrist exoskeleton reducing muscle activity by up to 51%, a 30.8 g haptic interface delivering 8 N force feedback with fast response, and a 208 g bipedal robot achieving multi-gait locomotion. GPAs establish a configurable platform for next-generation wearable robotics, haptic systems, and soft locomotion devices.

AI.tldr: Geometry-based pneumatic actuators (GPAs) use constraint layers with CNC heat-sealed chambers to achieve near-zero bending radii, multi-state actuation, and predictable deformation for soft robotics; validated by three applications: a lightweight wrist exoskeleton, a compact haptic interface, and a small bipedal robot.
AI.motivation: Soft pneumatic actuators are inherently safe and lightweight but face design bottlenecks: minimum bending radii, limited multi-state actuation, and structural stability. A configurable, repeatable architecture is needed to realize complex actuated geometries.
AI.method: Introduce constraint-layer GPAs with CNC heat-sealed chambers to enable programmable, geometry-driven deformation. Develop mathematical models capturing linear angle transformations and nonlinear torque–angle relationships across configurations. Validate the approach across diverse geometries and demonstrate three applications: a wrist exoskeleton, a haptic interface, and a bipedal robot.
AI.result: GPAs achieve near-zero bending radii and multi-state actuation with predictable deformation. The math shows linear angle transformations and nonlinear torque–angle behavior across configurations. Demonstrations include a 49 g wrist exoskeleton reducing muscle activity by up to 51%, a 30.8 g haptic interface delivering 8 N force feedback with fast response, and a 208 g bipedal robot enabling multi-gait locomotion.
AI.conclusion: GPAs provide a configurable platform for next-generation wearable robotics, haptic systems, and soft locomotion devices, enabling complex actuated geometries with repeatable performance.

title: U-Mind: A Unified Framework for Real-Time Multimodal Interaction with Audiovisual Generation
summary: Full-stack multimodal interaction in real-time is a central goal in building intelligent embodied agents capable of natural, dynamic communication. However, existing systems are either limited to unimodal generation or suffer from degraded reasoning and poor cross-modal alignment, preventing coherent and perceptually grounded interactions. In this work, we introduce U-Mind, the first unified system for high-intelligence multimodal dialogue that supports real-time generation and jointly models language, speech, motion, and video synthesis within a single interactive loop. At its core, U-Mind implements a Unified Alignment and Reasoning Framework that addresses two key challenges: enhancing cross-modal synchronization via a segment-wise alignment strategy, and preserving reasoning abilities through Rehearsal-Driven Learning. During inference, U-Mind adopts a text-first decoding pipeline that performs internal chain-of-thought planning followed by temporally synchronized generation across modalities. To close the loop, we implement a real-time video rendering framework conditioned on pose and speech, enabling expressive and synchronized visual feedback. Extensive experiments demonstrate that U-Mind achieves state-of-the-art performance on a range of multimodal interaction tasks, including question answering, instruction following, and motion generation, paving the way toward intelligent, immersive conversational agents.

AI.tldr: U-Mind is a real-time, unified multimodal dialogue system that jointly models language, speech, motion, and video, using segment-wise alignment and rehearsal-driven learning to enable synchronized, coherent generation in an interactive loop.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal systems are often unimodal in generation or suffer from weak cross-modal alignment and reasoning, impairing natural, embodied dialogue.
AI.method: Unified Alignment and Reasoning Framework with segment-wise alignment; Rehearsal-Driven Learning to preserve reasoning; text-first decoding with internal chain-of-thought planning; temporally synchronized generation across modalities; real-time video rendering conditioned on pose and speech.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows state-of-the-art performance on multimodal interaction tasks such as question answering, instruction following, and motion generation.
AI.conclusion: U-Mind advances toward intelligent, immersive conversational agents by delivering unified, real-time multimodal generation and reasoning in a single interactive loop.

title: Bridging Dynamics Gaps via Diffusion Schrödinger Bridge for Cross-Domain Reinforcement Learning
summary: Cross-domain reinforcement learning (RL) aims to learn transferable policies under dynamics shifts between source and target domains. A key challenge lies in the lack of target-domain environment interaction and reward supervision, which prevents direct policy learning. To address this challenge, we propose Bridging Dynamics Gaps for Cross-Domain Reinforcement Learning (BDGxRL), a novel framework that leverages Diffusion Schrödinger Bridge (DSB) to align source transitions with target-domain dynamics encoded in offline demonstrations. Moreover, we introduce a reward modulation mechanism that estimates rewards based on state transitions, applying to DSB-aligned samples to ensure consistency between rewards and target-domain dynamics. BDGxRL performs target-oriented policy learning entirely within the source domain, without access to the target environment or its rewards. Experiments on MuJoCo cross-domain benchmarks demonstrate that BDGxRL outperforms state-of-the-art baselines and shows strong adaptability under transition dynamics shifts.

AI.tldr: BDGxRL uses Diffusion Schrödinger Bridge to bridge dynamics gaps between source and target domains, with a reward modulation step, enabling target-oriented policy learning entirely in the source domain and achieving strong cross-domain performance on MuJoCo benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Cross-domain RL suffers from lack of target-domain interaction and rewards; aligning source transitions to target dynamics encoded in offline demonstrations is essential for transferable policies.
AI.method: Apply Diffusion Schrödinger Bridge to transform source-domain transitions to match target-domain dynamics encoded in offline demonstrations; incorporate a reward modulation mechanism that estimates rewards from state transitions to maintain consistency with target dynamics; train the policy entirely in the source domain without access to the target environment or rewards.
AI.result: BDGxRL outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on MuJoCo cross-domain benchmarks and shows robust adaptability under dynamics shifts.
AI.conclusion: BDGxRL provides a principled framework for cross-domain RL with dynamic shifts, enabling target-oriented learning using source-domain data by bridging dynamics via DSB and aligning rewards accordingly.

title: Planning from Observation and Interaction
summary: Observational learning requires an agent to learn to perform a task by referencing only observations of the performed task. This work investigates the equivalent setting in real-world robot learning where access to hand-designed rewards and demonstrator actions are not assumed. To address this data-constrained setting, this work presents a planning-based Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) algorithm for world modeling from observation and interaction alone. Experiments conducted entirely in the real-world demonstrate that this paradigm is effective for learning image-based manipulation tasks from scratch in under an hour, without assuming prior knowledge, pre-training, or data of any kind beyond task observations. Moreover, this work demonstrates that the learned world model representation is capable of online transfer learning in the real-world from scratch. In comparison to existing approaches, including IRL, RL, and Behavior Cloning (BC), which have more restrictive assumptions, the proposed approach demonstrates significantly greater sample efficiency and success rates, enabling a practical path forward for online world modeling and planning from observation and interaction. Videos and more at: https://uwrobotlearning.github.io/mpail2/.

AI.tldr: A planning-based inverse reinforcement learning framework that builds a world model from observation and interaction alone, enabling rapid, data-efficient learning of image-based robotic manipulation from scratch in the real world, with online transfer capabilities and superior performance over conventional IRL/RL/BC under restrictive assumptions.
AI.motivation: Address the data-constrained setting of observational learning in real-world robotics where hand-designed rewards and expert actions are unavailable; seek sample-efficient world modeling that can plan from observations without pretraining.
AI.method: Proposes a planning-based IRL algorithm that learns a latent world-model from pure observations and real interaction data, without reward supervision or demonstration data, and uses planning within this learned model for control. Demonstrates real-world, image-based manipulation tasks learned from scratch within under an hour and enables online transfer learning from scratch.
AI.result: Real-world experiments show effective from-observation learning of image-based manipulation tasks in under an hour without prior knowledge or data beyond task observations; the learned world model supports online transfer learning; outperforms existing approaches (IRL, RL, BC) with more restrictive assumptions in sample efficiency and success rates.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers a practical path for online world modeling and planning from observation and interaction, reducing data requirements and enabling effective real-world robotic manipulation through planning-based IRL, with demonstrated sample-efficient learning and transferable world representations.

title: Learning Accurate Segmentation Purely from Self-Supervision
summary: Accurately segmenting objects without any manual annotations remains one of the core challenges in computer vision. In this work, we introduce Selfment, a fully self-supervised framework that segments foreground objects directly from raw images without human labels, pretrained segmentation models, or any post-processing. Selfment first constructs patch-level affinity graphs from self-supervised features and applies NCut to obtain an initial coarse foreground--background separation. We then introduce Iterative Patch Optimization (IPO), a feature-space refinement procedure that progressively enforces spatial coherence and semantic consistency through iterative patch clustering. The refined masks are subsequently used as supervisory signals to train a lightweight segmentation head with contrastive and region-consistency objectives, allowing the model to learn stable and transferable object representations. Despite its simplicity and complete absence of manual supervision, Selfment sets new state-of-the-art (SoTA) results across multiple benchmarks. It achieves substantial improvements on $F_{\max}$ over previous unsupervised saliency detection methods on ECSSD ($+4.0\%$), HKUIS ($+4.6\%$), and PASCAL-S ($+5.7\%$). Moreover, without any additional fine-tuning, Selfment demonstrates remarkable zero-shot generalization to camouflaged object detection tasks (e.g., $0.910$ $S_m$ on CHAMELEON and $0.792$ $F_β^ω$ on CAMO), outperforming all existing unsupervised approaches and even rivaling the SoTA fully supervised methods.

AI.tldr: Fully self-supervised framework Selfment segments foreground objects from raw images without labels or post-processing, using patch-level affinity graphs, NCut, Iterative Patch Optimization, and a lightweight segmentation head with contrastive and region-consistency objectives; achieves SoTA results on unsupervised saliency benchmarks and strong zero-shot camouflaged object detection.
AI.motivation: Overcome the reliance on manual annotations or pretrained segmentation models for object segmentation by developing a purely self-supervised approach that yields transferable, stable object representations.
AI.method: 1) Build patch-level affinity graphs from self-supervised features and apply normalized cuts (NCut) for initial coarse FG/BG separation. 2) Employ Iterative Patch Optimization (IPO) to refine features and enforce spatial coherence and semantic consistency via iterative patch clustering. 3) Use the refined masks to supervise a lightweight segmentation head trained with contrastive and region-consistency objectives to learn stable, transferable representations.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art (SoTA) results on multiple unsupervised saliency benchmarks: ECSSD (+4.0% F_max), HKUIS (+4.6% F_max), PASCAL-S (+5.7% F_max). Demonstrates strong zero-shot generalization to camouflaged object detection (S_m = 0.910 on CHAMELEON; Fβ^ω = 0.792 on CAMO), outperforming existing unsupervised methods and rivaling fully supervised methods.
AI.conclusion: Selfment shows that fully self-supervised segmentation without human labels, pretrained models, or post-processing is feasible and yields competitive, transferable object representations, setting new SoTA on unsupervised datasets and offering strong zero-shot generalization.

title: OPTIAGENT: A Physics-Driven Agentic Framework for Automated Optical Design
summary: Optical design is the process of configuring optical elements to precisely manipulate light for high-fidelity imaging. It is inherently a highly non-convex optimization problem that relies heavily on human heuristic expertise and domain-specific knowledge. While Large Language Models (LLMs) possess extensive optical knowledge, their capabilities in leveraging the knowledge in designing lens system remain significantly constrained. This work represents the first attempt to employ LLMs in the field of optical design. We bridge the expertise gap by enabling users without formal optical training to successfully develop functional lens systems. Concretely, we curate a comprehensive dataset, named OptiDesignQA, which encompasses both classical lens systems sourced from standard optical textbooks and novel configurations generated by automated design algorithms for training and evaluation. Furthermore, we inject domain-specific optical expertise into the LLM through a hybrid objective of full-system synthesis and lens completion. To align the model with optical principles, we employ Group Relative Policy Optimization Done Right (DrGRPO) guided by Optical Lexicographic Reward for physics-driven policy alignment. This reward system incorporates structural format rewards, physical feasibility rewards, light-manipulation accuracy, and LLM-based heuristics. Finally, our model integrates with specialized optical optimization routines for end-to-end fine-tuning and precision refinement. We benchmark our proposed method against both traditional optimization-based automated design algorithms and LLM counterparts, and experimental results show the superiority of our method.

AI.tldr: LLM-assisted optical design using OptiDesignQA dataset and physics-guided policy optimization (DrGRPO with Optical Lexicographic Reward) to outperform traditional optimization and LLM baselines.
AI.motivation: To reduce reliance on human heuristic expertise in non-convex optical design and enable non-experts to design functional lens systems by leveraging LLMs with physics-guided training and end-to-end optimization.
AI.method: Create OptiDesignQA dataset combining classical lens systems and AI-generated configurations; train with a hybrid objective of full-system synthesis and lens completion; apply Group Relative Policy Optimization Done Right (DrGRPO) guided by Optical Lexicographic Reward, incorporating structural, physical feasibility, light-manipulation accuracy, and LLM-based heuristic rewards; integrate with specialized optical optimization routines for end-to-end fine-tuning and refinement.
AI.result: Benchmarking against traditional optimization algorithms and LLM counterparts shows superior performance of the proposed method.
AI.conclusion: LLM-driven optical design with physics-aligned policy optimization and end-to-end integration is feasible and superior to existing approaches, enabling non-experts to develop functional lens systems.

title: Uncertainty Quantification for Multimodal Large Language Models with Incoherence-adjusted Semantic Volume
comment: Earlier versions presented at ICLR 2025 QUESTION workshop and ICML 2025 R2-FM workshop
summary: Despite their capabilities, Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) may produce plausible but erroneous outputs, hindering reliable deployment. Accurate uncertainty metrics could enable escalation of unreliable queries to human experts or larger models for improved performance. However, existing uncertainty metrics have practical constraints, such as being designed only for specific modalities, reliant on external tools, or computationally expensive. We introduce UMPIRE, a training-free uncertainty quantification framework for MLLMs that works efficiently across various input and output modalities without external tools, relying only on the models' own internal modality features. UMPIRE computes the incoherence-adjusted semantic volume of sampled MLLM responses for a given task instance, effectively capturing both the global semantic diversity of samples and the local incoherence of responses based on internal model confidence. We propose uncertainty desiderata for MLLMs and provide theoretical analysis motivating UMPIRE's design. Extensive experiments show that UMPIRE consistently outperforms baseline metrics in error detection and uncertainty calibration across image, audio, and video-text benchmarks, including adversarial and out-of-distribution settings. We also demonstrate UMPIRE's generalization to non-text output tasks, including image and audio generation.

AI.tldr: UMPIRE: a training-free uncertainty quantification framework for Multimodal LLMs that uses incoherence-adjusted semantic volume of sampled responses, leveraging internal model features, to improve error detection and calibration across image, audio, and video-text modalities.
AI.motivation: Existing uncertainty metrics are often modality-specific, require external tools, or are computationally expensive, hindering reliable deployment of multimodal LLMs. A universal, training-free, tool-free uncertainty measure is desirable.
AI.method: Compute the incoherence-adjusted semantic volume (IASV) from multiple sampled responses for a given task instance, using the model's own internal modality features to capture global semantic diversity and local incoherence of outputs. No external tools and no training required. The authors also define uncertainty desiderata for MLLMs and provide theoretical justification for UMPIRE's design.
