AI.result: Empirical results show UMPIRE outperforms baseline uncertainty metrics in error detection and calibration across image, audio, and video-text benchmarks, including adversarial and out-of-distribution settings. UMPIRE also generalizes to non-text output tasks such as image and audio generation.
AI.conclusion: UMPIRE provides an effective, efficient, training-free uncertainty quantification framework for MLLMs with broad modality support and strong empirical performance, enabling better detection of unreliable outputs and safer escalation to human or larger-model review.

title: Robust Skills, Brittle Grounding: Diagnosing Restricted Generalization in Vision-Language Action Policies via Multi-Object Picking
summary: Vision-language action (VLA) policies often report strong manipulation benchmark performance with relatively few demonstrations, but it remains unclear whether this reflects robust language-to-object grounding or reliance on object--location correlations that do not transfer beyond the training distribution. We present a controlled multi-object picking study that progressively increases object placement variability up to full workspace randomization and evaluates held-out object--location pairings that break familiar associations without increasing spatial difficulty. Across these stress tests and data scaling, we find that for representative VLA policies, including SmolVLA and $π_{0.5}$, execution of the manipulation primitive remains substantially more reliable than instruction-conditioned task success in harder regimes, suggesting that manipulation skill acquisition is decoupled from instruction following. We recommend augmenting manipulation benchmarks with task ladders and decomposed metrics that separately measure primitive execution and instruction-conditioned success to better diagnose instruction-grounded generalization.

AI.tldr: In controlled multi-object picking, VLA policies' manipulation primitives generalize under increasing object placement variability better than instruction-conditioned success; evidence of decoupled manipulation from language-conditioned task execution; benchmarks should include task ladders and decomposed metrics.
AI.motivation: Assess whether vision-language action (VLA) policies truly ground language-to-object manipulation or rely on object-location priors, and evaluate generalization beyond training distributions.
AI.method: Progressively randomize object placements in multi-object picking, introduce held-out object-location pairings that disrupt familiar associations without raising spatial difficulty, and evaluate SmolVLA and π0.5 across data scales; compare fabrication of the manipulation primitive vs instruction-conditioned task success.
AI.result: Across stress tests and data scaling, manipulation execution remains substantially more reliable than instruction-conditioned success in harder regimes, indicating a decoupling between manipulation skill acquisition and language-conditioned instruction following.
AI.conclusion: Augment benchmarks with task ladders and decomposed metrics that separately measure primitive execution and instruction-conditioned success to better diagnose instruction-grounded generalization.

title: Diffusion Probe: Generated Image Result Prediction Using CNN Probes
summary:
Text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models lack an efficient mechanism for early quality assessment, leading to costly trial-and-error in multi-generation scenarios such as prompt iteration, agent-based generation, and flow-grpo. We reveal a strong correlation between early diffusion cross-attention distributions and final image quality. Based on this finding, we introduce Diffusion Probe, a framework that leverages internal cross-attention maps as predictive signals.
  We design a lightweight predictor that maps statistical properties of early-stage cross-attention extracted from initial denoising steps to the final image's overall quality. This enables accurate forecasting of image quality across diverse evaluation metrics long before full synthesis is complete.
  We validate Diffusion Probe across a wide range of settings. On multiple T2I models, across early denoising windows, resolutions, and quality metrics, it achieves strong correlation (PCC > 0.7) and high classification performance (AUC-ROC > 0.9).
  Its reliability translates into practical gains. By enabling early quality-aware decisions in workflows such as prompt optimization, seed selection, and accelerated RL training, the probe supports more targeted sampling and avoids computation on low-potential generations. This reduces computational overhead while improving final output quality.
  Diffusion Probe is model-agnostic, efficient, and broadly applicable, offering a practical solution for improving T2I generation efficiency through early quality prediction.

AI.tldr: Diffusion Probe forecasts final image quality from early cross-attention in T2I diffusion models, enabling early quality-aware decisions.
AI.motivation: Reduce computational cost and improve sample quality in T2I diffusion by predicting final quality before full synthesis.
AI.method: Train a lightweight predictor using statistics of early-stage cross-attention maps extracted from initial denoising steps; model-agnostic across diffusion models; evaluate with various metrics.
AI.result: Strong predictive performance: PCC > 0.7 across early windows, resolutions; AUC-ROC > 0.9 for quality classification; leads to reduced computation and better final outputs.
AI.conclusion: An efficient, model-agnostic approach that enables early quality assessment in diffusion-based text-to-image generation.

title: MAGE: Multi-scale Autoregressive Generation for Offline Reinforcement Learning
summary: Generative models have gained significant traction in offline reinforcement learning (RL) due to their ability to model complex trajectory distributions. However, existing generation-based approaches still struggle with long-horizon tasks characterized by sparse rewards. Some hierarchical generation methods have been developed to mitigate this issue by decomposing the original problem into shorter-horizon subproblems using one policy and generating detailed actions with another. While effective, these methods often overlook the multi-scale temporal structure inherent in trajectories, resulting in suboptimal performance. To overcome these limitations, we propose MAGE, a Multi-scale Autoregressive GEneration-based offline RL method. MAGE incorporates a condition-guided multi-scale autoencoder to learn hierarchical trajectory representations, along with a multi-scale transformer that autoregressively generates trajectory representations from coarse to fine temporal scales. MAGE effectively captures temporal dependencies of trajectories at multiple resolutions. Additionally, a condition-guided decoder is employed to exert precise control over short-term behaviors. Extensive experiments on five offline RL benchmarks against fifteen baseline algorithms show that MAGE successfully integrates multi-scale trajectory modeling with conditional guidance, generating coherent and controllable trajectories in long-horizon sparse-reward settings.

AI.tldr: MAGE introduces multi-scale autoregressive generation for offline RL by combining a condition-guided multi-scale autoencoder with a multi-scale transformer and a conditional decoder, achieving coherent and controllable long-horizon trajectories under sparse rewards.
AI.motivation: Offline RL with generative trajectory modeling struggles with long-horizon, sparse-reward tasks and underutilizes multi-scale temporal structure; existing hierarchical methods often operate at a single temporal scale and miss cross-scale dependencies.
AI.method: Proposes a three-component architecture: a condition-guided multi-scale autoencoder to learn hierarchical trajectory representations; a multi-scale transformer that generates trajectory representations from coarse to fine temporal scales in an autoregressive fashion; a condition-guided decoder to exert precise control over short-term actions. Training on offline data; inference uses coarse-to-fine generation; optimization encourages long-horizon consistency and alignment with rewards.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on five offline RL benchmarks against fifteen baselines shows MAGE yields coherent and controllable trajectories in long-horizon sparse-reward settings, demonstrating effective multi-scale modeling with conditional guidance.
AI.conclusion: MAGE validates that modeling temporality at multiple scales with conditioned generation improves offline RL performance in challenging settings; suggests broader applicability and opportunities for extensions to other domains requiring hierarchical sequence generation.

title: A Minimal Agent for Automated Theorem Proving
summary: We propose a minimal agentic baseline that enables systematic comparison across different AI-based theorem prover architectures. This design implements the core features shared among state-of-the-art systems: iterative proof refinement, library search and context management. We evaluate our baseline using qualitatively different benchmarks and compare various popular models and design choices, and demonstrate competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art approaches, while using a significantly simpler architecture. Our results demonstrate consistent advantages of an iterative approach over multiple single-shot generations, especially in terms of sample efficiency and cost effectiveness. The implementation is released open-source as a candidate reference for future research and as an accessible prover for the community.

AI.tldr: A minimal agentic baseline for AI-based theorem provers enabling fair cross-architecture comparisons; emphasizes iterative refinement, library search, and context management; finds iterative strategies more sample-efficient and cost-effective; released as open-source reference.
AI.motivation: Provide a simple, reusable baseline to unify evaluation across AI theorem prover designs, reducing fragmentation and enabling fair comparisons.
AI.method: Implement core features: iterative proof refinement, library search, and context management; evaluate on qualitatively diverse benchmarks; compare multiple models and design choices; analyze sample efficiency and cost.
AI.result: Competitive performance with a simpler architecture; iterative approach outperforms single-shot generation in sample efficiency and cost; baseline released open-source as candidate reference.
AI.conclusion: Iterative refinement is advantageous for theorem proving; baseline serves as accessible reference for future research; open-source release fosters community adoption and reproducibility.

title: Humanoid Robots as First Assistants in Endoscopic Surgery
summary: Humanoid robots have become a focal point of technological ambition, with claims of surgical capability within years in mainstream discourse. These projections are aspirational yet lack empirical grounding. To date, no humanoid has assisted a surgeon through an actual procedure, let alone performed one. The work described here breaks this new ground. Here we report a proof of concept in which a teleoperated Unitree G1 provided endoscopic visualization while an attending otolaryngologist performed a cadaveric sphenoidectomy. The procedure was completed successfully, with stable visualization maintained throughout. Teleoperation allowed assessment of whether the humanoid form factor could meet the physical demands of surgical assistance in terms of sustenance and precision; the cognitive demands were satisfied -- for now -- by the operator. Post-procedure analysis identified engineering targets for clinical translation, alongside near-term opportunities such as autonomous diagnostic scoping. This work establishes form-factor feasibility for humanoid surgical assistance while identifying challenges for continued development.

AI.tldr: First proof-of-concept of a humanoid robot performing teleoperated endoscopic visualization for a cadaveric sphenoidectomy; demonstrates form-factor feasibility and outlines translation path and autonomous diagnostic scoping opportunities.
AI.motivation: To ground aspirational claims of humanoid surgical capability with empirical data; evaluate whether a humanoid form can meet the physical and cognitive demands of surgical assistance and identify obstacles and translation targets.
AI.method: Teleoperation of a Unitree G1 to provide endoscopic visualization while an attending otolaryngologist performed a cadaveric sphenoidectomy; assessment of visualization stability, precision, and cognitive workload; post-procedure engineering analysis to identify clinical translation targets and near-term autonomous capabilities.
AI.result: Procedure completed successfully with stable endoscopic visualization; demonstrates form-factor feasibility for humanoid surgical assistance; identifies engineering targets and near-term opportunities, including autonomous diagnostic scoping.
AI.conclusion: Establishes form-factor feasibility for humanoid surgical assistance while outlining remaining challenges for clinical translation and continued development.

title: Fourier Angle Alignment for Oriented Object Detection in Remote Sensing
summary: In remote sensing rotated object detection, mainstream methods suffer from two bottlenecks, directional incoherence at detector neck and task conflict at detecting head. Ulitising fourier rotation equivariance, we introduce Fourier Angle Alignment, which analyses angle information through frequency spectrum and aligns the main direction to a certain orientation. Then we propose two plug and play modules : FAAFusion and FAA Head. FAAFusion works at the detector neck, aligning the main direction of higher-level features to the lower-level features and then fusing them. FAA Head serves as a new detection head, which pre-aligns RoI features to a canonical angle and adds them to the original features before classification and regression. Experiments on DOTA-v1.0, DOTA-v1.5 and HRSC2016 show that our method can greatly improve previous work. Particularly, our method achieves new state-of-the-art results of 78.72% mAP on DOTA-v1.0 and 72.28% mAP on DOTA-v1.5 datasets with single scale training and testing, validating the efficacy of our approach in remote sensing object detection. The code is made publicly available at https://github.com/gcy0423/Fourier-Angle-Alignment .

AI.tldr: Proposes Fourier Angle Alignment (FAA) to address directional incoherence at the detector neck and task conflict at the detection head in rotated object detection for remote sensing. Introduces two plug-in modules—FAAFusion (neck) and FAA Head (RoI head)—based on Fourier rotation equivariance to align angles and fuse features. Demonstrates state-of-the-art results on DOTA-v1.0, DOTA-v1.5, and HRSC2016 with single-scale training/testing (78.72% mAP on DOTA-v1.0; 72.28% mAP on DOTA-v1.5).
AI.motivation: Rotated object detection in remote sensing suffers from two bottlenecks: directional incoherence at the detector neck (misalignment of orientation-consistent features across levels) and task conflict at the detecting head (inconsistent RoI feature alignment during classification/regression). There is a need for a representation and training strategy that preserves and leverages angle information across the detector.
AI.method: Introduce Fourier Angle Alignment (FAA) leveraging Fourier rotation equivariance to analyze angle information in the frequency domain and align the main orientation to a canonical reference. Develop two plug-in modules: (1) FAAFusion, placed at the detector neck to align higher-level features with lower-level features before fusion; (2) FAA Head, a new detection head that pre-aligns RoI features to a canonical angle and adds them to the backbone features prior to classification and regression.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on DOTA-v1.0, DOTA-v1.5, and HRSC2016 shows substantial improvements, achieving state-of-the-art results with single-scale training/testing (78.72% mAP on DOTA-v1.0 and 72.28% mAP on DOTA-v1.5).
AI.conclusion: FAA effectively mitigates directional incoherence and task conflict in rotated object detection, offering a practical, plug-and-play solution that delivers strong performance gains across standard remote sensing benchmarks; code is publicly available.

title: TradeFM: A Generative Foundation Model for Trade-flow and Market Microstructure
summary: Foundation models have transformed domains from language to genomics by learning general-purpose representations from large-scale, heterogeneous data. We introduce TradeFM, a 524M-parameter generative Transformer that brings this paradigm to market microstructure, learning directly from billions of trade events across >9K equities. To enable cross-asset generalization, we develop scale-invariant features and a universal tokenization scheme that map the heterogeneous, multi-modal event stream of order flow into a unified discrete sequence -- eliminating asset-specific calibration. Integrated with a deterministic market simulator, TradeFM-generated rollouts reproduce key stylized facts of financial returns, including heavy tails, volatility clustering, and absence of return autocorrelation. Quantitatively, TradeFM achieves 2-3x lower distributional error than Compound Hawkes baselines and generalizes zero-shot to geographically out-of-distribution APAC markets with moderate perplexity degradation. Together, these results suggest that scale-invariant trade representations capture transferable structure in market microstructure, opening a path toward synthetic data generation, stress testing, and learning-based trading agents.

AI.tldr: TradeFM is a 524M-parameter Transformer model trained on billions of trade events across 9k+ equities, using scale-invariant features and a universal tokenization scheme to achieve cross-asset generalization. It integrates with a deterministic market simulator, reproduces key stylized facts, and outperforms Compound Hawkes baselines with zero-shot transfer to APAC markets, enabling synthetic data and learning-based trading agents.
AI.motivation: Extend the foundation-model paradigm to financial market microstructure, enabling transfer across assets, synthetic data generation for stress testing, and the development of learning-based trading agents.
AI.method: Train a 524M-parameter generative Transformer (TradeFM) on billions of trade events across >9K equities. Develop scale-invariant features and a universal tokenization scheme to map heterogeneous, multi-modal order-flow into a unified discrete sequence. Integrate with a deterministic market simulator for rollouts. Evaluate distributional fit and cross-market generalization, comparing to Compound Hawkes baselines and testing zero-shot transfer to APAC markets.
AI.result: TradeFM-generated rollouts reproduce key stylized facts of financial returns (heavy tails, volatility clustering, no return autocorrelation) and achieve 2–3x lower distributional error than Compound Hawkes baselines. The model generalizes zero-shot to geographically out-of-distribution APAC markets with moderate perplexity degradation.
AI.conclusion: Scale-invariant trade representations capture transferable structure in market microstructure, enabling synthetic data generation, stress testing, and learning-based trading agents across markets.

title: DARE-bench: Evaluating Modeling and Instruction Fidelity of LLMs in Data Science
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026. 10 pages plus appendix
summary: The fast-growing demands in using Large Language Models (LLMs) to tackle complex multi-step data science tasks create an emergent need for accurate benchmarking. There are two major gaps in existing benchmarks: (i) the lack of standardized, process-aware evaluation that captures instruction adherence and process fidelity, and (ii) the scarcity of accurately labeled training data. To bridge these gaps, we introduce DARE-bench, a benchmark designed for machine learning modeling and data science instruction following. Unlike many existing benchmarks that rely on human- or model-based judges, all tasks in DARE-bench have verifiable ground truth, ensuring objective and reproducible evaluation. To cover a broad range of tasks and support agentic tools, DARE-bench consists of 6,300 Kaggle-derived tasks and provides both large-scale training data and evaluation sets. Extensive evaluations show that even highly capable models such as gpt-o4-mini struggle to achieve good performance, especially in machine learning modeling tasks. Using DARE-bench training tasks for fine-tuning can substantially improve model performance. For example, supervised fine-tuning boosts Qwen3-32B's accuracy by 1.83x and reinforcement learning boosts Qwen3-4B's accuracy by more than 8x. These significant improvements verify the importance of DARE-bench both as an accurate evaluation benchmark and critical training data.

AI.tldr: DARE-bench provides a ground-truth, process-aware benchmarking suite with 6,300 Kaggle tasks for ML modeling and data science instruction following, enabling objective evaluation and effective training data, with notable performance gains observed when fine-tuned or RL-trained.
AI.motivation: Address two gaps in existing benchmarks: (i) lack of standardized, process-aware evaluation capturing instruction adherence and process fidelity; (ii) scarcity of accurately labeled training data.
AI.method: Assemble DARE-bench with verifiable ground truth across Kaggle-derived tasks; include large-scale training data and evaluation sets; avoid reliance on human- or model-based judges; supports agentic tools; 6,300 tasks; provides both data for training and evaluation sets.
AI.result: Even capable models like gpt-o4-mini struggle on machine learning modeling tasks; supervised fine-tuning boosts Qwen3-32B's accuracy by 1.83x; reinforcement learning boosts Qwen3-4B's accuracy by >8x.
AI.conclusion: DARE-bench serves as an accurate evaluation benchmark and critical training data source for data science instruction following, enabling objective benchmarking and substantial model performance gains.

title: How IMU Drift Influences Multi-Radar Inertial Odometry for Ground Robots in Subterranean Terrains
comment: Accepted in IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA), 2026
summary: Reliable radar inertial odometry (RIO) requires mitigating IMU bias drift, a challenge that intensifies in subterranean environments due to extreme temperatures and gravity-induced accelerations. Cost-effective IMUs such as the Pixhawk, when paired with FMCW TI IWR6843AOP EVM radars, suffer from drift-induced degradation compounded by sparse, noisy, and flickering radar returns, making fusion less stable than LiDAR-based odometry. Yet, LiDAR fails under smoke, dust, and aerosols, whereas FMCW radars remain compact, lightweight, cost-effective, and robust in these situations. To address these challenges, we propose a two-stage MRIO framework that combines an IMU bias estimator for resilient localization and mapping in GPS-denied subterranean environments affected by smoke. Radar-based ego-velocity estimation is formulated through a least-squares approach and incorporated into an EKF for online IMU bias correction; the corrected IMU accelerations are fused with heterogeneous measurements from multiple radars and an IMU to refine odometry. The proposed framework further supports radar-only mapping by exploiting the robot's estimated translational and rotational displacements. In subterranean field trials, MRIO delivers robust localization and mapping, outperforming EKF-RIO. It maintains accuracy across cost-efficient FMCW radar setups and different IMUs, showing resilience with Pixhawk and higher-grade units such as VectorNav. The implementation will be provided as an open-source resource to the community (code available at https://github.com/LTU-RAI/MRIO

AI.tldr: MRIO: a two-stage radar–IMU fusion framework for robust radar inertial odometry in GPS-denied subterranean environments, using an IMU bias estimator and EKF with radar ego-velocity to handle drift and sparse radar data; supports radar-only mapping and works with cheap to high-grade IMUs; open-source.
AI.motivation: IMU bias drift and sparse/noisy radar returns degrade traditional RIO, especially in subterranean settings with extreme temperatures and gravity-induced accelerations; LiDAR is unreliable in smoke/dust, so robust, cost-effective radar-based odometry is needed.
AI.method: Two-stage MRIO: (1) IMU bias estimator to correct drift; (2) radar-based ego-velocity estimation via least-squares integrated into an EKF for online bias correction; fuses corrected accelerations with heterogeneous radar and IMU measurements; enables radar-only mapping by leveraging translational/rotational displacements; validates in subterranean field trials across different FMCW radars and IMUs; code released as open-source.
AI.result: MRIO provides robust localization and mapping, outperforming EKF-RIO; maintains accuracy across cost-effective FMCW radar setups and various IMUs (e.g., Pixhawk, VectorNav).
AI.conclusion: The proposed MRIO framework effectively mitigates IMU bias drift and radar data sparsity in GPS-denied subterranean environments, enabling accurate radar-only odometry and mapping with broad hardware compatibility; open-source release facilitates adoption.

title: See, Act, Adapt: Active Perception for Unsupervised Cross-Domain Visual Adaptation via Personalized VLM-Guided Agent
summary: Pre-trained perception models excel in generic image domains but degrade significantly in novel environments like indoor scenes. The conventional remedy is fine-tuning on downstream data which incurs catastrophic forgetting of prior knowledge and demands costly, scene-specific annotations. We propose a paradigm shift through Sea$^2$ (See, Act, Adapt): rather than adapting the perception modules themselves, we adapt how they are deployed through an intelligent pose-control agent. Sea$^2$ keeps all perception modules frozen, requiring no downstream labels during training, and uses only scalar perceptual feedback to navigate the agent toward informative viewpoints. Specially, we transform a vision-language model (VLM) into a low-level pose controller through a two-stage training pipeline: first fine-tuning it on rule-based exploration trajectories that systematically probe indoor scenes, and then refining the policy via unsupervised reinforcement learning that constructs rewards from the perception module's outputs and confidence. Unlike prior active perception methods that couple exploration with specific models or collect data for retraining them, Sea$^2$ directly leverages off-the-shelf perception models for various tasks without the need for retraining. We conducted experiments on three visual perception tasks, including visual grounding, segmentation and 3D box estimation, with performance improvements of 13.54%, 15.92% and 27.68% respectively on dataset ReplicaCAD.

AI.tldr: Sea^2 freezes perception modules and learns a pose-control agent to select informative viewpoints for indoor scenes, using rule-based exploration and unsupervised RL to derive rewards from perception outputs, achieving notable gains without downstream labels.
AI.motivation: Pre-trained perception models degrade in novel indoor environments; fine-tuning risks catastrophic forgetting and requires costly scene-specific annotations. A deployment-focused adaptation seeks to maintain knowledge while improving task performance.
AI.method: Two-stage pipeline: convert a vision-language model into a low-level pose controller. Stage 1 fine-tunes on rule-based exploration trajectories probing indoor scenes. Stage 2 refines policy via unsupervised reinforcement learning, constructing rewards from the perception module outputs and confidence. Perception modules remain frozen; training uses only scalar perceptual feedback with no downstream labels.
AI.result: Three tasks—visual grounding, segmentation, and 3D box estimation—achieve improvements of 13.54%, 15.92%, and 27.68% respectively on ReplicaCAD.
AI.conclusion: Sea^2 demonstrates a deployment-first paradigm shift: adapt how models are used rather than the models themselves, enabling zero-label adaptation and effective use of off-the-shelf perception systems across multiple tasks in indoor environments.

title: Provable Subspace Identification of Nonlinear Multi-view CCA
summary: We investigate the identifiability of nonlinear Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA) in a multi-view setup, where each view is generated by an unknown nonlinear map applied to a linear mixture of shared latents and view-private noise. Rather than attempting exact unmixing, a problem proven to be ill-posed, we instead reframe multi-view CCA as a basis-invariant subspace identification problem. We prove that, under suitable latent priors and spectral separation conditions, multi-view CCA recovers the pairwise correlated signal subspaces up to view-wise orthogonal ambiguity. For $N \geq 3$ views, the objective provably isolates the jointly correlated subspaces shared across all views while eliminating view-private variations. We further establish finite-sample consistency guarantees by translating the concentration of empirical cross-covariances into explicit subspace error bounds via spectral perturbation theory. Experiments on synthetic and rendered image datasets validate our theoretical findings and confirm the necessity of the assumed conditions.

AI.tldr: Identifiability of nonlinear multi-view CCA is achieved via a basis-invariant subspace identification approach, recovering jointly correlated subspaces up to view-wise orthogonal ambiguities for N≥3, with finite-sample consistency and empirical validation on synthetic and rendered image data.
AI.motivation: Direct unmixing of nonlinear multi-view CCA is ill-posed; the paper reframes the problem to recover signal subspaces that are common across views while tolerating view-specific noise.
AI.method: Formulate multi-view CCA as a basis-invariant subspace identification problem under latent priors and spectral separation. Derive conditions under which pairwise correlated subspaces are recovered up to view-wise rotations; for N≥3 isolate jointly correlated subspaces across all views and suppress view-private variations. Translate empirical cross-covariance concentration into subspace error bounds via spectral perturbation theory; provide finite-sample guarantees.
AI.result: Under suitable latent priors and spectral separation, the method recovers the shared signal subspaces up to view-wise orthogonal ambiguities; with N≥3 it isolates joint subspaces across all views while eliminating view-private components. Finite-sample analyses yield explicit subspace error bounds. Experiments on synthetic and rendered images support the theory and demonstrate the necessity of the stated conditions.
AI.conclusion: The paper reframes identifiability in nonlinear multi-view CCA from exact unmixing to robust subspace identification, proving recoverability of shared subspaces under reasonable conditions and providing finite-sample guarantees corroborated by experiments.

title: Evaluating Accuracy of Vine Robot Shape Sensing with Distributed Inertial Measurement Units
summary: Soft, tip-extending vine robots are well suited for navigating tight, debris-filled environments, making them ideal for urban search and rescue. Sensing the full shape of a vine robot's body is helpful both for localizing information from other sensors placed along the robot body and for determining the robot's configuration within the space being explored. Prior approaches have localized vine robot tips using a single inertial measurement unit (IMU) combined with force sensing or length estimation, while one method demonstrated full-body shape sensing using distributed IMUs on a passively steered robot in controlled maze environments. However, the accuracy of distributed IMU-based shape sensing under active steering, varying robot lengths, and different sensor spacings has not been systematically quantified. In this work, we experimentally evaluate the accuracy of vine robot shape sensing using distributed IMUs along the robot body. We quantify IMU drift, measuring an average orientation drift rate of 1.33 degrees/min across 15 sensors. For passive steering, mean tip position error was 11% of robot length. For active steering, mean tip position error increased to 16%. During growth experiments across lengths from 30-175 cm, mean tip error was 8%, with a positive trend with increasing length. We also analyze the influence of sensor spacing and observe that intermediate spacings can minimize error for single-curvature shapes. These results demonstrate the feasibility of distributed IMU-based shape sensing for vine robots while highlighting key limitations and opportunities for improved modeling and algorithmic integration for field deployment.

AI.tldr: Distributed IMU-based full-body shape sensing for vine robots was experimentally evaluated. IMU drift (~1.33°/min across 15 sensors) and tip-position errors (11% of length for passive steering, 16% for active steering) were quantified, with 8% mean error across 30–175 cm growth, and sensor-spacing effects showing intermediate spacings reduce error for single-curvature shapes. The study demonstrates feasibility and highlights limitations and avenues for improved modeling for field deployment.
AI.motivation: To enable accurate full-body shape sensing of soft, tip-extending vine robots in cluttered environments, enabling better localization and configuration estimation, and to quantify how active steering, length variation, and sensor spacing affect distributed-IMU-based sensing.
AI.method: Experimentally place distributed IMUs along the vine robot body, measure orientation drift, compare passive vs. active steering, conduct growth experiments across lengths 30–175 cm, evaluate tip-position error, and analyze the effect of sensor spacing on shape estimation, particularly for single-curvature shapes.
AI.result: IMU drift ~1.33°/min across 15 sensors. Passive steering tip error ~11% of robot length; active steering tip error ~16%. Growth experiments across 30–175 cm length show mean tip error ~8% with longer lengths increasing error. Sensor spacing analysis indicates intermediate spacings minimize error for single-curvature shapes.
AI.conclusion: Distributed IMU-based full-body shape sensing for vine robots is feasible but limited by drift, increased error under active steering, and sensor-spacing effects; suggests modeling and algorithmic integration improvements for practical field deployment.

title: Action-Geometry Prediction with 3D Geometric Prior for Bimanual Manipulation
summary: Bimanual manipulation requires policies that can reason about 3D geometry, anticipate how it evolves under action, and generate smooth, coordinated motions. However, existing methods typically rely on 2D features with limited spatial awareness, or require explicit point clouds that are difficult to obtain reliably in real-world settings. At the same time, recent 3D geometric foundation models show that accurate and diverse 3D structure can be reconstructed directly from RGB images in a fast and robust manner. We leverage this opportunity and propose a framework that builds bimanual manipulation directly on a pre-trained 3D geometric foundation model. Our policy fuses geometry-aware latents, 2D semantic features, and proprioception into a unified state representation, and uses diffusion model to jointly predict a future action chunk and a future 3D latent that decodes into a dense pointmap. By explicitly predicting how the 3D scene will evolve together with the action sequence, the policy gains strong spatial understanding and predictive capability using only RGB observations. We evaluate our method both in simulation on the RoboTwin benchmark and in real-world robot executions. Our approach consistently outperforms 2D-based and point-cloud-based baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance in manipulation success, inter-arm coordination, and 3D spatial prediction accuracy. Code is available at https://github.com/Chongyang-99/GAP.git.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based policy for bimanual manipulation built on a pre-trained 3D geometric foundation model, fusing geometry-aware latents, 2D semantics, and proprioception from RGB inputs to predict future actions and a 3D latent that decodes into a dense point map; achieves state-of-the-art results on RoboTwin and real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches rely on 2D features or explicit point clouds with limited spatial awareness. Modern 3D geometric foundation models can reconstruct accurate 3D structure from RGB, enabling robust spatial reasoning for manipulation.
AI.method: A policy that fuses geometry-aware latents from a pre-trained 3D foundation model with 2D semantic features and proprioception into a unified state representation. It uses a diffusion model to jointly predict a future action chunk and a future 3D latent that decodes into a dense point cloud, operating directly from RGB observations.
AI.result: The method outperforms 2D-based and point-cloud-based baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance in manipulation success, inter-arm coordination, and 3D spatial prediction accuracy. Evaluations conducted in RoboTwin simulation and real-world robot executions; code released at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the viability and benefits of building bimanual manipulation policies on 3D geometric foundation models, achieving enhanced spatial understanding and predictive capability from RGB data, with strong real-world applicability.

title: UPath: Universal Planner Across Topological Heterogeneity For Grid-Based Pathfinding
summary: The performance of search algorithms for grid-based pathfinding, e.g. A*, critically depends on the heuristic function that is used to focus the search. Recent studies have shown that informed heuristics that take the positions/shapes of the obstacles into account can be approximated with the deep neural networks. Unfortunately, the existing learning-based approaches mostly rely on the assumption that training and test grid maps are drawn from the same distribution (e.g., city maps, indoor maps, etc.) and perform poorly on out-of-distribution tasks. This naturally limits their application in practice when often a universal solver is needed that is capable of efficiently handling any problem instance. In this work, we close this gap by designing an universal heuristic predictor: a model trained once, but capable of generalizing across a full spectrum of unseen tasks. Our extensive empirical evaluation shows that the suggested approach halves the computational effort of A* by up to a factor of 2.2, while still providing solutions within 3% of the optimal cost on average altogether on the tasks that are completely different from the ones used for training $\unicode{x2013}$ a milestone reached for the first time by a learnable solver.

AI.tldr: A universal, learning-based heuristic for A* generalizes to unseen grid maps, providing substantial speedups (up to 2.2x) with near-optimal solutions (within ~3%).
AI.motivation: Address the poor generalization of existing learning-based heuristics that are trained on specific map distributions and fail on out-of-distribution instances; develop a universal solver that works across diverse tasks.
AI.method: Train a single deep neural network to predict A* heuristics by incorporating obstacle positions/shapes; evaluate on task distributions different from training to test out-of-distribution generalization and universal applicability.
AI.result: Significant computational savings for A* (up to 2.2x faster) with solutions within 3% of optimal cost on average on unseen task distributions; demonstrates first milestone for a learnable, universal planner.
AI.conclusion: A single-trained universal heuristic predictor can generalize across diverse grid-based planning tasks, effectively reducing search effort while maintaining near-optimality, enabling practical deployment of learning-based solvers.

title: SafeGen-LLM: Enhancing Safety Generalization in Task Planning for Robotic Systems
summary: Safety-critical task planning in robotic systems remains challenging: classical planners suffer from poor scalability, Reinforcement Learning (RL)-based methods generalize poorly, and base Large Language Models (LLMs) cannot guarantee safety. To address this gap, we propose safety-generalizable large language models, named SafeGen-LLM. SafeGen-LLM can not only enhance the safety satisfaction of task plans but also generalize well to novel safety properties in various domains. We first construct a multi-domain Planning Domain Definition Language 3 (PDDL3) benchmark with explicit safety constraints. Then, we introduce a two-stage post-training framework: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on a constraint-compliant planning dataset to learn planning syntax and semantics, and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) guided by fine-grained reward machines derived from formal verification to enforce safety alignment and by curriculum learning to better handle complex tasks. Extensive experiments show that SafeGen-LLM achieves strong safety generalization and outperforms frontier proprietary baselines across multi-domain planning tasks and multiple input formats (e.g., PDDLs and natural language).

AI.tldr: SafeGen-LLM—a safety-generalizable LLM for planning trained with SFT plus GRPO using reward machines; introduces a multi-domain PDDL3 safety benchmark; shows superior safety-generalization and performance over strong proprietary baselines across PDDL and natural language inputs.
AI.motivation: Safety-critical robotic planning suffers from poor scalability (classical planners), weak generalization (RL), and unsafe assumptions of base LLMs. A system that generalizes to novel safety properties while maintaining planning competence is needed.
AI.method: Two-stage post-training: (1) Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on a constraint-compliant planning dataset to learn planning syntax and semantics; (2) Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) guided by fine-grained reward machines derived from formal verification to enforce safety alignment, plus curriculum learning to handle complex tasks. Supportive steps include constructing a multi-domain PDDL3 benchmark with explicit safety constraints.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show SafeGen-LLM achieves strong safety generalization and outperforms frontier proprietary baselines across multi-domain planning tasks and multiple input formats (PDDLs and natural language).
AI.conclusion: SafeGen-LLM demonstrates that safety-generalizable LLMs can learn constraint-compliant planning and generalize to novel safety properties, providing a viable path toward safe, scalable planning in robotics with LLM-based systems.

title: Footprint-Guided Exemplar-Free Continual Histopathology Report Generation
summary: Rapid progress in vision-language modeling has enabled pathology report generation from gigapixel whole-slide images, but most approaches assume static training with simultaneous access to all data. In clinical deployment, however, new organs, institutions, and reporting conventions emerge over time, and sequential fine-tuning can cause catastrophic forgetting. We introduce an exemplar-free continual learning framework for WSI-to-report generation that avoids storing raw slides or patch exemplars. The core idea is a compact domain footprint built in a frozen patch-embedding space: a small codebook of representative morphology tokens together with slide-level co-occurrence summaries and lightweight patch-count priors. These footprints support generative replay by synthesizing pseudo-WSI representations that reflect domain-specific morphological mixtures, while a teacher snapshot provides pseudo-reports to supervise the updated model without retaining past data. To address shifting reporting conventions, we distill domain-specific linguistic characteristics into a compact style descriptor and use it to steer generation. At inference, the model identifies the most compatible descriptor directly from the slide signal, enabling domain-agnostic setup without requiring explicit domain identifiers. Evaluated across multiple public continual learning benchmarks, our approach outperforms exemplar-free and limited-buffer rehearsal baselines, highlighting footprint-based generative replay as a practical solution for deployment in evolving clinical settings.

AI.tldr: Exemplar-free continual learning for WSI-to-report generation using footprint-based generative replay to prevent forgetting without storing raw slides or patches.
AI.motivation: Clinical pathology faces continual emergence of new organs, institutions, and reporting conventions. Sequential fine-tuning causes catastrophic forgetting and raw data cannot be retained. Need privacy-preserving, exemplar-free continual learning for WSI-to-report generation.
AI.method: Propose a compact domain footprint in frozen patch-embedding space: a small codebook of morphology tokens, slide-level co-occurrence summaries, and lightweight patch-count priors. Footprints enable generative replay by synthesizing pseudo-WSI representations reflecting domain mixtures; a teacher snapshot provides pseudo-reports for supervision without past data. Distill domain-specific linguistic traits into a compact style descriptor and steer generation. At inference, select the most compatible descriptor from the slide signal for domain-agnostic setup without explicit domain identifiers.
AI.result: Evaluated on multiple public continual learning benchmarks; the footprint-based generative replay approach outperforms exemplar-free and limited-buffer rehearsal baselines.
AI.conclusion: Footprint-based generative replay offers a practical exemplar-free continual learning solution for evolving clinical WSI-to-report generation, enabling domain adaptation without data storage and supporting domain-agnostic inference.

title: GRAIL: Post-hoc Compensation by Linear Reconstruction for Compressed Networks
summary: Structured deep model compression methods are hardware-friendly and substantially reduce memory and inference costs. However, under aggressive compression, the resulting accuracy degradation often necessitates post-compression finetuning, which can be impractical due to missing labeled data or high training cost. We propose post-hoc blockwise compensation, called GRAIL, a simple zero-finetuning step applied after model compression that restores each block's input-output behavior using a small calibration set. The method summarizes hidden activations via a Gram matrix and applies ridge regression to linearly reconstruct the original hidden representation from the reduced one. The resulting reconstruction map is absorbed into the downstream projection weights, while the upstream layer is compressed. The approach is selector-agnostic (Magnitude, Wanda, Gram-based selection, or folding), data-aware (requiring only a few forward passes without gradients or labels), and recovers classic pruning or folding when the Gram matrix is near identity, indicating weak inter-channel correlations. Across ResNets, ViTs, and decoder-only LLMs, GRAIL consistently improves accuracy or perplexity over data-free and data-aware pruning or folding baselines in practical compression regimes, with manageable overhead and no backpropagation. The code is available at https://github.com/TWWinde/GRAIL.

AI.tldr: GRAIL is a zero-finetuning, post-hoc blockwise compensation for structured model compression that uses a Gram-matrix-based reconstruction with ridge regression to recover original hidden representations after pruning/folding, requiring only a small calibration set and no backpropagation.
AI.motivation: Aggressive structured compression degrades accuracy and often needs costly post-training finetuning with labeled data. There is a need for a practical, data-efficient, post-hoc correction that avoids gradient-based retraining.
AI.method: After compression, GRAIL computes a Gram matrix of hidden activations from the reduced block and learns a ridge regression map to reconstruct the original hidden representation. This reconstruction map is absorbed into downstream projection weights; upstream layers remain compressed. The approach is selector-agnostic (supports Magnitude, Wanda, Gram-based selection, or folding) and data-aware (requires only a few forward passes, no gradients or labels). When the Gram matrix is near identity (weak inter-channel correlations), it reduces to classic pruning or folding.
AI.result: GRAIL consistently improves accuracy or perplexity across ResNets, Vision Transformers, and decoder-only LLMs in practical compression regimes, outperforming data-free and data-aware pruning or folding baselines with modest overhead and no backpropagation.
AI.conclusion: A simple, calibration-set–driven post-hoc correction can restore the input-output behavior of compressed blocks without finetuning, broadening the practicality of structured compression for diverse architectures. The method is broadly selector-agnostic, data-efficient, and aligns with regimes where channel correlations are weak (Gram near identity).

title: Altitude-Aware Visual Place Recognition in Top-Down View
summary: To address the challenge of aerial visual place recognition (VPR) problem under significant altitude variations, this study proposes an altitude-adaptive VPR approach that integrates ground feature density analysis with image classification techniques. The proposed method estimates airborne platforms' relative altitude by analyzing the density of ground features in images, then applies relative altitude-based cropping to generate canonical query images, which are subsequently used in a classification-based VPR strategy for localization. Extensive experiments across diverse terrains and altitude conditions demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves high accuracy and robustness in both altitude estimation and VPR under significant altitude changes. Compared to conventional methods relying on barometric altimeters or Time-of-Flight (ToF) sensors, this solution requires no additional hardware and offers a plug-and-play solution for downstream applications, {making it suitable for small- and medium-sized airborne platforms operating in diverse environments, including rural and urban areas.} Under significant altitude variations, incorporating our relative altitude estimation module into the VPR retrieval pipeline boosts average R@1 and R@5 by 29.85\% and 60.20\%, respectively, compared with applying VPR retrieval alone. Furthermore, compared to traditional {Monocular Metric Depth Estimation (MMDE) methods}, the proposed method reduces the mean error by 202.1 m, yielding average additional improvements of 31.4\% in R@1 and 44\% in R@5. These results demonstrate that our method establishes a robust, vision-only framework for three-dimensional visual place recognition, offering a practical and scalable solution for accurate airborne platforms localization under large altitude variations and limited sensor availability.

AI.tldr: Altitude-adaptive visual place recognition (VPR) for aerial platforms using ground-feature-density to infer relative altitude, followed by altitude-based image cropping and a classification-based VPR. Shows strong gains in R@1 and R@5 under large altitude variations, with a fully vision-only, hardware-free solution.
AI.motivation: To enable robust aerial VPR under large altitude variations without reliance on barometric or ToF sensors; ground feature density in images can serve as a proxy for altitude and facilitate canonical views for VPR.
AI.method: Estimate relative altitude from ground-feature density; apply relative-altitude-based cropping to generate canonical query images; perform classification-based VPR for localization; compare against barometer/ToF-based methods and monocular metric depth estimation (MMDE); test across terrains and altitudes.
AI.result: Significant gains in retrieval accuracy: average R@1 +29.85% and R@5 +60.20% when incorporating the altitude-estimation module versus VPR alone. Outperforms MMDE by reducing mean depth error by 202.1 m, with average improvements of 31.4% in R@1 and 44% in R@5. Demonstrates high accuracy and robustness across diverse terrains and altitude conditions; no extra hardware required.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a robust, vision-only framework for 3D VPR enabling accurate localization of airborne platforms under large altitude variations, with practical plug-and-play applicability for small- to medium-sized UAVs in rural and urban environments.

title: Denoising-Enhanced YOLO for Robust SAR Ship Detection
summary: With the rapid advancement of deep learning, synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery has become a key modality for ship detection. However, robust performance remains challenging in complex scenes, where clutter and speckle noise can induce false alarms and small targets are easily missed. To address these issues, we propose CPN-YOLO, a high-precision ship detection framework built upon YOLOv8 with three targeted improvements. First, we introduce a learnable large-kernel denoising module for input pre-processing, producing cleaner representations and more discriminative features across diverse ship types. Second, we design a feature extraction enhancement strategy based on the PPA attention mechanism to strengthen multi-scale modeling and improve sensitivity to small ships. Third, we incorporate a Gaussian similarity loss derived from the normalized Wasserstein distance (NWD) to better measure similarity under complex bounding-box distributions and improve generalization. Extensive experiments on HRSID and SSDD demonstrate the effectiveness of our method. On SSDD, CPN-YOLO surpasses the YOLOv8 baseline, achieving 97.0% precision, 95.1% recall, and 98.9% mAP, and consistently outperforms other representative deep-learning detectors in overall performance.

AI.tldr: CPN-YOLO enhances YOLOv8 for SAR ship detection via three targeted improvements: (1) a learnable large-kernel denoising module for input pre-processing, (2) a PPA attention-based strategy for improved multi-scale feature extraction and small-ship sensitivity, and (3) a Gaussian similarity loss based on normalized Wasserstein distance to better measure bounding-box similarity and enhance generalization. It achieves state-of-the-art results on SSDD (97.0% precision, 95.1% recall, 98.9% mAP) and shows strong performance on HRSID.
AI.motivation: SAR imagery presents clutter and speckle noise, leading to false alarms and missed small ships. Robust, high-precision ship detection in complex scenes remains challenging. Existing detectors (e.g., YOLOv8) may underperform in SAR contexts, motivating a specialized, integrated approach combining denoising, enhanced multi-scale features, and a geometry-aware loss.
AI.method: Three contributions integrated into YOLOv8: (i) a learnable large-kernel denoising module for input pre-processing to produce cleaner representations; (ii) a PPA (presumably Pyramid Path Attention) based feature extraction enhancement to strengthen multi-scale modeling and improve small-ship sensitivity; (iii) a Gaussian similarity loss derived from the normalized Wasserstein distance (NWD) to better capture bounding-box distributional similarity and improve generalization. Evaluated on HRSID and SSDD datasets, with SSDD results showing clear gains over the YOLOv8 baseline and other detectors.
AI.result: On SSDD, CPN-YOLO achieves 97.0% precision, 95.1% recall, and 98.9% mAP, surpassing the YOLOv8 baseline and consistently outperforming representative deep-learning detectors in overall performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed triples of denoising, multi-scale attention, and distribution-aware loss address key SAR-specific challenges (clutter, speckle, and small targets) and yield strong generalization. The approach is competitive with, and in several metrics superior to, current state-of-the-art detectors on SAR ship detection benchmarks; future work could include ablation studies, cross-dataset generalization, and deployment considerations.

title: MPU: Towards Secure and Privacy-Preserving Knowledge Unlearning for Large Language Models
summary: Machine unlearning for large language models often faces a privacy dilemma in which strict constraints prohibit sharing either the server's parameters or the client's forget set. To address this dual non-disclosure constraint, we propose MPU, an algorithm-agnostic privacy-preserving Multiple Perturbed Copies Unlearning framework that primarily introduces two server-side modules: Pre-Process for randomized copy generation and Post-Process for update aggregation. In Pre-Process, the server distributes multiple perturbed and reparameterized model instances, allowing the client to execute unlearning locally on its private forget set without accessing the server's exact original parameters. After local unlearning, the server performs Post-Process by inverting the reparameterization and aggregating updates with a harmonic denoising procedure to alleviate the impact of perturbation. Experiments with seven unlearning algorithms show that MPU achieves comparable unlearning performance to noise-free baselines, with most algorithms' average degradation well below 1% under 10% noise, and can even outperform the noise-free baseline for some algorithms under 1% noise. Code is available at https://github.com/Tristan-SHU/MPU.

AI.tldr: MPU enables privacy-preserving, algorithm-agnostic unlearning for LLMs by distributing multiple perturbed copies for client-side forget-set processing and then inverting reparameterization with harmonic denoising during aggregation; it achieves near-noiseless performance under modest perturbations and outperforms noise-free baselines in some cases, with code available.
AI.motivation: Addresses a dual privacy constraint: server parameters and client forget sets cannot be disclosed. The goal is to enable effective unlearning in LLMs under strict privacy constraints while maintaining performance.
AI.method: Pre-Process: generate multiple perturbed and reparameterized model copies and distribute them to the client, enabling local unlearning on the private forget set without server-origin parameters. Post-Process: invert the reparameterization and aggregate updates using a harmonic denoising procedure to mitigate perturbation impact. The framework is algorithm-agnostic and designed to work with various unlearning algorithms.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across seven unlearning algorithms shows MPU achieves unlearning performance comparable to noise-free baselines. Under typical 10% perturbation noise, average degradation is below 1% for most algorithms, and some even surpass the noise-free baseline at 1% noise. Code is available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: MPU offers a practical, privacy-preserving, algorithm-agnostic unlearning framework that maintains near-baseline performance under modest perturbations. It shows strong empirical promise and provides an open-source implementation for adoption and further research.

title: AoE: Always-on Egocentric Human Video Collection for Embodied AI
summary: Embodied foundation models require large-scale, high-quality real-world interaction data for pre-training and scaling. However, existing data collection methods suffer from high infrastructure costs, complex hardware dependencies, and limited interaction scope, making scalable expansion challenging. In fact, humans themselves are ideal physically embodied agents. Therefore, obtaining egocentric real-world interaction data from globally distributed "human agents" offers advantages of low cost and sustainability. To this end, we propose the Always-on Egocentric (AoE) data collection system, which aims to simplify hardware dependencies by leveraging humans themselves and their smartphones, enabling low-cost, highly efficient, and scene-agnostic real-world interaction data collection to address the challenge of data scarcity. Specifically, we first employ an ergonomic neck-mounted smartphone holder to enable low-barrier, large-scale egocentric data collection through a cloud-edge collaborative architecture. Second, we develop a cross-platform mobile APP that leverages on-device compute for real-time processing, while the cloud hosts automated labeling and filtering pipelines that transform raw videos into high-quality training data. Finally, the AoE system supports distributed Ego video data collection by anyone, anytime, and anywhere. We evaluate AoE on data preprocessing quality and downstream tasks, demonstrating that high-quality egocentric data significantly boosts real-world generalization.

AI.tldr: AoE uses neck-mounted smartphones and a cloud-edge system to collect and process egocentric real-world data from distributed human agents, enabling low-cost, scalable data collection for embodied foundation models.
AI.motivation: Overcome data scarcity, high infrastructure costs, and hardware dependencies in pretraining embodied foundation models by leveraging humans as embodied agents and ubiquitous smartphones.
AI.method: Ergonomic neck-mounted phone holder for large-scale data capture; cross-platform mobile app for on-device processing; cloud pipelines for automated labeling and filtering; cloud-edge collaboration for scalable, distributed data collection.
AI.result: Demonstrates improved preprocessing quality and downstream task performance, indicating that high-quality egocentric data enhances real-world generalization.
AI.conclusion: AoE provides a practical, scalable path to obtain diverse egocentric data at low cost, potentially accelerating the development and generalization of embodied foundation models.

title: APPO: Attention-guided Perception Policy Optimization for Video Reasoning
summary: Complex video reasoning, actually, relies excessively on fine-grained perception rather than on expert (e.g., Ph.D, Science)-level reasoning. Through extensive empirical observation, we have recognized the critical impact of perception. In particular, when perception ability is almost fixed, enhancing reasoning from Qwen3-8B to OpenAI-o3 yields only 0.7% performance improvement. Conversely, even minimal change in perception model scale (from 7B to 32B) boosts performance by 1.4%, indicating enhancing perception, rather than reasoning, is more critical to improve performance. Therefore, exploring how to enhance perception ability through reasoning without the need for expensive fine-grained annotation information is worthwhile. To achieve this goal, we specially propose APPO, the Attention-guided Perception Policy Optimization algorithm that leverages token-level dense rewards to improve model's fine-grained perception. The core idea behind APPO is to optimize those tokens from different responses that primarily focus on the same crucial video frame (called intra-group perception tokens). Experimental results on diverse video benchmarks and models with different scales (3/7B) demonstrate APPO consistently outperforms GRPO and DAPO (0.5%~4%). We hope our work provides a promising approach to effectively enhance model's perception abilities through reasoning in a low-cost manner, serving diverse scenarios and demands.

AI.tldr: APPO trains a perception-focused policy with token-level rewards to improve fine-grained video perception, outperforming GRPO/DAPO by 0.5%–4% across 3/7B models, suggesting perception enhancement is key for video reasoning at lower cost.
AI.motivation: To address the finding that fine-grained perception, not higher-level reasoning, limits performance in complex video reasoning, and to enable perception improvements without expensive fine-grained annotations.
AI.method: APPO (Attention-guided Perception Policy Optimization) uses token-level dense rewards to optimize intra-group perception tokens—tokens from different responses that focus on the same crucial video frame—across multiple model scales (3B/7B).
AI.result: APPO consistently outperforms GRPO and DAPO across evaluated benchmarks, yielding approximately 0.5% to 4% gains.
AI.conclusion: Enhancing perception via a reasoning-driven, low-cost optimization strategy is a promising direction for improving video reasoning performance.

title: Actor-Critic Pretraining for Proximal Policy Optimization
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) actor-critic algorithms enable autonomous learning but often require a large number of environment interactions, which limits their applicability in robotics. Leveraging expert data can reduce the number of required environment interactions. A common approach is actor pretraining, where the actor network is initialized via behavioral cloning on expert demonstrations and subsequently fine-tuned with RL. In contrast, the initialization of the critic network has received little attention, despite its central role in policy optimization. This paper proposes a pretraining approach for actor-critic algorithms like Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) that uses expert demonstrations to initialize both networks. The actor is pretrained via behavioral cloning, while the critic is pretrained using returns obtained from rollouts of the pretrained policy. The approach is evaluated on 15 simulated robotic manipulation and locomotion tasks. Experimental results show that actor-critic pretraining improves sample efficiency by 86.1% on average compared to no pretraining and by 30.9% to actor-only pretraining.

AI.tldr: Pretraining both actor and critic with expert data yields large sample-efficiency gains for PPO in robotics tasks.
AI.motivation: Robotics RL requires many environment interactions; initializing the critic is crucial for stable policy optimization; leveraging expert data can reduce sample complexity.
AI.method: Pretrain the actor via behavioral cloning on expert demonstrations; pretrain the critic using returns from rollouts of the pretrained policy; integrate within an actor-critic framework (PPO) to enable efficient fine-tuning.
AI.result: Evaluated on 15 simulated robotic manipulation and locomotion tasks; average sample-efficiency improvement of 86.1% over no pretraining and 30.9% over actor-only pretraining.
AI.conclusion: Initializing both actor and critic with expert data yields substantial sample-efficiency gains for actor-critic RL; supports broader use of expert data to pretrain critic networks and potentially benefits other algorithms.

title: NAU-QMUL: Utilizing BERT and CLIP for Multi-modal AI-Generated Image Detection
summary: With the aim of detecting AI-generated images and identifying the specific models responsible for their generation, we propose a multi-modal multi-task model. The model leverages pre-trained BERT and CLIP Vision encoders for text and image feature extraction, respectively, and employs cross-modal feature fusion with a tailored multi-task loss function. Additionally, a pseudo-labeling-based data augmentation strategy was utilized to expand the training dataset with high-confidence samples. The model achieved fifth place in both Tasks A and B of the `CT2: AI-Generated Image Detection' competition, with F1 scores of 83.16\% and 48.88\%, respectively. These findings highlight the effectiveness of the proposed architecture and its potential for advancing AI-generated content detection in real-world scenarios. The source code for our method is published on https://github.com/xxxxxxxxy/AIGeneratedImageDetection.

AI.tldr: A multi-modal multi-task model using BERT and CLIP encoders with cross-modal fusion and pseudo-labeling for AI-generated image detection and model attribution; ranked 5th in CT2 Tasks A/B with F1 83.16% and 48.88%; code on GitHub.
AI.motivation: To detect AI-generated imagery and identify the generating models by leveraging both text and image cues, addressing limitations of single-modality methods and aiming for real-world applicability.
AI.method: A multi-modal framework employing pre-trained BERT (text) and CLIP Vision (image) encoders for feature extraction, cross-modal feature fusion, and a tailored multi-task loss. Includes a pseudo-labeling-based data augmentation strategy to enlarge high-confidence samples.
AI.result: Fifth place in both CT2 Tasks A and B; F1 scores of 83.16% (Task A) and 48.88% (Task B).
AI.conclusion: The architecture is effective for AI-generated content detection and attribution and shows promise for real-world deployment; source code is publicly available.

title: Beyond State-Wise Mirror Descent: Offline Policy Optimization with Parameteric Policies
summary: We investigate the theoretical aspects of offline reinforcement learning (RL) under general function approximation. While prior works (e.g., Xie et al., 2021) have established the theoretical foundations of learning a good policy from offline data via pessimism, existing algorithms that are computationally tractable (often in an oracle-efficient sense), such as PSPI, only apply to finite and small action spaces. Moreover, these algorithms rely on state-wise mirror descent and require actors to be implicitly induced from the critic functions, failing to accommodate standalone policy parameterization which is ubiquitous in practice. In this work, we address these limitations and extend the theoretical guarantees to parameterized policy classes over large or continuous action spaces. When extending mirror descent to parameterized policies, we identify contextual coupling as the core difficulty, and show how connecting mirror descent to natural policy gradient leads to novel analyses, guarantees, and algorithmic insights, including a surprising unification between offline RL and imitation learning.

AI.tldr: Extends offline RL theory to parameterized policies over large/continuous action spaces by linking mirror descent with natural policy gradient, overcoming finite-action and critic-only constraints and unifying offline RL with imitation learning.
AI.motivation: Current theoretical results (e.g., pessimism-based) and algorithms like PSPI are restricted to finite action spaces and implicit policy extraction from critics; there is a gap for standalone, parameterized policies in large or continuous action domains.
AI.method: Generalize the mirror-descent framework to parameterized policies, addressing contextual coupling; connect mirror descent to natural policy gradient; derive novel analyses, guarantees, and algorithmic insights; reveal a unification with imitation learning.
AI.result: The work provides theoretical guarantees for parameterized policy classes in offline RL with large/continuous action spaces, offering new analyses and algorithmic insights and establishing a conceptual link to imitation learning.
AI.conclusion: The study broadens offline RL theory to practical policy parameterizations, delivering a unified perspective with imitation learning and guiding future algorithm design in continuous-action settings.

title: Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation in Remote Sensing via Hierarchical Attention Masking and Model Composition
comment: Published in the proceedings of the British Machine Vision Conference Workshops 2025
summary: In this paper, we propose ReSeg-CLIP, a new training-free Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation method for remote sensing data. To compensate for the problems of vision language models, such as CLIP in semantic segmentation caused by inappropriate interactions within the self-attention layers, we introduce a hierarchical scheme utilizing masks generated by SAM to constrain the interactions at multiple scales. We also present a model composition approach that averages the parameters of multiple RS-specific CLIP variants, taking advantage of a new weighting scheme that evaluates representational quality using varying text prompts. Our method achieves state-of-the-art results across three RS benchmarks without additional training.

AI.tldr: Proposes ReSeg-CLIP, a training-free, open-vocabulary RS segmentation method that uses SAM-based hierarchical masking and model-averaging of RS-specific CLIP variants with prompt-based weighting to achieve state-of-the-art results on three RS benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of vision-language models like CLIP in semantic segmentation for remote sensing (notably self-attention interaction issues) and to realize effective open-v vocabulary segmentation without additional training.
AI.method: Introduce a hierarchical scheme that uses SAM-generated masks to constrain CLIP interactions across multiple scales. Propose a model composition approach that averages parameters of multiple RS-specific CLIP variants, with a weighting scheme determined by evaluating representational quality using different text prompts.
AI.result: Attains state-of-the-art performance on three remote sensing benchmarks without any additional training.
AI.conclusion: The approach delivers a training-free, open-vocabulary RS segmentation framework that mitigates self-attention issues via hierarchical masking and boosts generalization through prompt-aware model averaging.

title: Learning to maintain safety through expert demonstrations in settings with unknown constraints: A Q-learning perspective
summary: Given a set of trajectories demonstrating the execution of a task safely in a constrained MDP with observable rewards but with unknown constraints and non-observable costs, we aim to find a policy that maximizes the likelihood of demonstrated trajectories trading the balance between being conservative and increasing significantly the likelihood of high-rewarding trajectories but with potentially unsafe steps. Having these objectives, we aim towards learning a policy that maximizes the probability of the most $promising$ trajectories with respect to the demonstrations. In so doing, we formulate the ``promise" of individual state-action pairs in terms of $Q$ values, which depend on task-specific rewards as well as on the assessment of states' safety, mixing expectations in terms of rewards and safety. This entails a safe Q-learning perspective of the inverse learning problem under constraints: The devised Safe $Q$ Inverse Constrained Reinforcement Learning (SafeQIL) algorithm is compared to state-of-the art inverse constraint reinforcement learning algorithms to a set of challenging benchmark tasks, showing its merits.

AI.tldr: SafeQIL is a safe Q-learning based inverse constrained RL approach for constrained MDPs with unknown costs, optimizing trajectory likelihood by balancing safety and reward.
AI.motivation: Address learning safe policies from demonstrations when constraints are unknown and costs are not observable; maximize likelihood of high-reward, safe trajectories; focus on promising trajectories.
AI.method: Formulate promise of state-action pairs via Q-values incorporating rewards and safety; propose SafeQIL algorithm that performs safe Q-learning for inverse constrained RL to maximize demonstration likelihood; compare to existing methods on benchmarks.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on challenging benchmarks shows merits over state-of-the-art inverse constrained RL algorithms, achieving favorable trade-offs between safety and reward and better alignment with demonstrations.
AI.conclusion: SafeQIL provides a principled framework for safe inverse RL under constraints, capturing the promise of state-action pairs and integrating safety into the learning objective; applicable to tasks with unknown costs and constrained MDPs.

title: Bandwidth-adaptive Cloud-Assisted 360-Degree 3D Perception for Autonomous Vehicles
summary: A key challenge for autonomous driving lies in maintaining real-time situational awareness regarding surrounding obstacles under strict latency constraints. The high processing requirements coupled with limited onboard computational resources can cause delay issues, particularly in complex urban settings. To address this, we propose leveraging Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication to partially offload processing to the cloud, where compute resources are abundant, thus reducing overall latency. Our approach utilizes transformer-based models to fuse multi-camera sensor data into a comprehensive Bird's-Eye View (BEV) representation, enabling accurate 360-degree 3D object detection. The computation is dynamically split between the vehicle and the cloud based on the number of layers processed locally and the quantization level of the features. To further reduce network load, we apply feature vector clipping and compression prior to transmission. In a real-world experimental evaluation, our hybrid strategy achieved a 72 \% reduction in end-to-end latency compared to a traditional onboard solution. To adapt to fluctuating network conditions, we introduce a dynamic optimization algorithm that selects the split point and quantization level to maximize detection accuracy while satisfying real-time latency constraints. Trace-based evaluation under realistic bandwidth variability shows that this adaptive approach improves accuracy by up to 20 \% over static parameterization with the same latency performance.

AI.tldr: Cloud-assisted V2X offloading with transformer BEV fusion for 360-degree 3D object detection; dynamic local/remote split and quantization with feature compression, achieving substantial latency reduction and accuracy gains under bandwidth variability.
AI.motivation: Autonomous driving requires real-time situational awareness with limited on-board compute; urban scenes impose high processing demands; offloading to cloud via V2X can reduce latency and enable heavier models.
AI.method: Hybrid pipeline: fuse multi-camera data into BEV with transformer models; split computation between vehicle and cloud based on number of local layers and feature quantization; apply feature clipping and compression; dynamic optimization algorithm selects split point and quantization to meet latency constraints; trace-based evaluation under realistic bandwidth variability.
AI.result: End-to-end latency reduced by 72% compared with traditional onboard solution; dynamic approach improves accuracy by up to 20% over static parameterization at the same latency; real-world experiments support viability of the approach.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic compute offloading via V2X and adaptive quantization is effective for real-time 3D detection and robust to network fluctuations; supports scalable deployment in connected autonomous systems.

title: Inferring Chronic Treatment Onset from ePrescription Data: A Renewal Process Approach
summary: Longitudinal electronic health record (EHR) data are often left-censored, making diagnosis records incomplete and unreliable for determining disease onset. In contrast, outpatient prescriptions form renewal-based trajectories that provide a continuous signal of disease management. We propose a probabilistic framework to infer chronic treatment onset by modeling prescription dynamics as a renewal process and detecting transitions from sporadic to sustained therapy via change-point detection between a baseline Poisson (sporadic prescribing) regime and a regime-specific Weibull (sustained therapy) renewal model.
