Using a nationwide ePrescription dataset of 2.4 million individuals, we show that the approach yields more temporally plausible onset estimates than naive rule-based triggering, substantially reducing implausible early detections under strong left censoring. Detection performance varies across diseases and is strongly associated with prescription density, highlighting both the strengths and limits of treatment-based onset inference.

AI.tldr: A renewal-process framework infers chronic treatment onset from prescription data by detecting transitions from sporadic to sustained therapy via a Poisson-to-Weibull change-point; applied to 2.4M individuals, improving onset plausibility over naive methods and highlighting density-dependent performance.
AI.motivation: Left-censored EHR data obscure true disease onset; outpatient prescriptions offer continuous management signals, enabling onset inference if modeled properly.
AI.method: Model prescriptions as a renewal process with a baseline Poisson regime (sporadic prescribing) and a regime-specific Weibull renewal model (sustained therapy); use change-point detection to identify transitions; validate on nationwide ePrescription data (2.4M individuals).
AI.result: More temporally plausible onset estimates than naive triggering; substantially reduces implausible early detections under strong left censoring; performance varies by disease and correlates with prescription density.
AI.conclusion: Treatment-based onset inference is promising but has limits; effectiveness hinges on prescription density; the approach provides a principled, data-driven alternative to EHR-based onset estimation.

title: FedNSAM:Consistency of Local and Global Flatness for Federated Learning
summary: In federated learning (FL), multi-step local updates and data heterogeneity usually lead to sharper global minima, which degrades the performance of the global model. Popular FL algorithms integrate sharpness-aware minimization (SAM) into local training to address this issue. However, in the high data heterogeneity setting, the flatness in local training does not imply the flatness of the global model. Therefore, minimizing the sharpness of the local loss surfaces on the client data does not enable the effectiveness of SAM in FL to improve the generalization ability of the global model. We define the \textbf{flatness distance} to explain this phenomenon. By rethinking the SAM in FL and theoretically analyzing the \textbf{flatness distance}, we propose a novel \textbf{FedNSAM} algorithm that accelerates the SAM algorithm by introducing global Nesterov momentum into the local update to harmonize the consistency of global and local flatness. \textbf{FedNSAM} uses the global Nesterov momentum as the direction of local estimation of client global perturbations and extrapolation. Theoretically, we prove a tighter convergence bound than FedSAM by Nesterov extrapolation. Empirically, we conduct comprehensive experiments on CNN and Transformer models to verify the superior performance and efficiency of \textbf{FedNSAM}. The code is available at https://github.com/junkangLiu0/FedNSAM.

AI.tldr: FedNSAM accelerates SAM in federated learning by integrating global Nesterov momentum into local updates to harmonize local/global flatness, yielding tighter convergence bounds and improved generalization efficiency on CNN/Transformer models; code available.
AI.motivation: Data heterogeneity in FL causes local sharpness to poorly reflect global flatness, so local SAM may not improve the global model; need to align local and global loss surface flatness.
AI.method: Introduce global Nesterov momentum into local updates to guide local perturbation estimation and extrapolation; define flatness distance; analyze convergence; prove tighter bound than FedSAM; validate empirically on CNN and Transformer architectures.
AI.result: Theoretical: tighter convergence bound with Nesterov extrapolation; empirical: FedNSAM achieves superior performance and efficiency over FedSAM on CNN/Transformer tasks.
AI.conclusion: FedNSAM effectively harmonizes local and global flatness in FL, improving generalization and efficiency of SAM in distributed settings; supported by theory and experiments and reproducible via released code.

title: DACESR: Degradation-Aware Conditional Embedding for Real-World Image Super-Resolution
summary: Multimodal large models have shown excellent ability in addressing image super-resolution in real-world scenarios by leveraging language class as condition information, yet their abilities in degraded images remain limited. In this paper, we first revisit the capabilities of the Recognize Anything Model (RAM) for degraded images by calculating text similarity. We find that directly using contrastive learning to fine-tune RAM in the degraded space is difficult to achieve acceptable results. To address this issue, we employ a degradation selection strategy to propose a Real Embedding Extractor (REE), which achieves significant recognition performance gain on degraded image content through contrastive learning. Furthermore, we use a Conditional Feature Modulator (CFM) to incorporate the high-level information of REE for a powerful Mamba-based network, which can leverage effective pixel information to restore image textures and produce visually pleasing results. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the REE can effectively help image super-resolution networks balance fidelity and perceptual quality, highlighting the great potential of Mamba in real-world applications. The source code of this work will be made publicly available at: https://github.com/nathan66666/DACESR.git

AI.tldr: Proposes Real Embedding Extractor (REE) with degradation-aware contrastive learning to adapt RAM for degraded images, then modulates REE features into a Mamba-based SR network via a Conditional Feature Modulator (CFM), achieving balanced fidelity and perceptual quality; code will be released.
AI.motivation: RAM's performance deteriorates on degraded images; there is a need for degradation-aware embeddings to guide super-resolution, enabling robust recognition and texture restoration in real-world scenarios.
AI.method: 1) Assess RAM on degraded images via text similarity; 2) find direct contrastive fine-tuning in degraded space ineffective; 3) introduce a degradation selection strategy to train Real Embedding Extractor (REE) with contrastive learning; 4) use Conditional Feature Modulator (CFM) to inject high-level REE information into a Mamba-based SR network, guiding restoration with effective pixel details; 5) end-to-end training to balance fidelity and perceptual quality.
AI.result: REE provides significant recognition gains on degraded content and helps SR networks balance fidelity with perceptual quality; extensive experiments validate the approach.
AI.conclusion: The REE + CFM approach demonstrates strong potential for real-world degraded-image SR, leveraging Mamba's capabilities; code will be publicly available for replication and further study.

title: ULW-SleepNet: An Ultra-Lightweight Network for Multimodal Sleep Stage Scoring
summary: Automatic sleep stage scoring is crucial for the diagnosis and treatment of sleep disorders. Although deep learning models have advanced the field, many existing models are computationally demanding and designed for single-channel electroencephalography (EEG), limiting their practicality for multimodal polysomnography (PSG) data. To overcome this, we propose ULW-SleepNet, an ultra-lightweight multimodal sleep stage scoring framework that efficiently integrates information from multiple physiological signals. ULW-SleepNet incorporates a novel Dual-Stream Separable Convolution (DSSC) Block, depthwise separable convolutions, channel-wise parameter sharing, and global average pooling to reduce computational overhead while maintaining competitive accuracy. Evaluated on the Sleep-EDF-20 and Sleep-EDF-78 datasets, ULW-SleepNet achieves accuracies of 86.9% and 81.4%, respectively, with only 13.3K parameters and 7.89M FLOPs. Compared to state-of-the-art methods, our model reduces parameters by up to 98.6% with only marginal performance loss, demonstrating its strong potential for real-time sleep monitoring on wearable and IoT devices. The source code for this study is publicly available at https://github.com/wzw999/ULW-SLEEPNET.

AI.tldr: Ultra-lightweight multimodal sleep-stage classifier (ULW-SleepNet) using a Dual-Stream Separable Convolution (DSSC) block; achieves competitive accuracy with dramatically fewer parameters and FLOPs, enabling real-time use on wearables.
AI.motivation: To overcome computational demands and single-channel limitation of existing deep learning sleep staging models by leveraging multimodal PSG data with an efficient architecture.
AI.method: Proposes DSSC Block with depthwise separable convolutions, channel-wise parameter sharing, and global average pooling to fuse multi-sensor signals; designed for ultra-lightweight operation; evaluated on Sleep-EDF-20/ Sleep-EDF-78; 13.3K parameters, 7.89M FLOPs.
AI.result: Achieves 86.9% (Sleep-EDF-20) and 81.4% (Sleep-EDF-78) accuracy; parameter count reduced by up to 98.6% compared to SOTA with marginal performance loss.
AI.conclusion: ULW-SleepNet is a viable real-time sleep staging solution for wearable/iot devices; source code available at the provided GitHub link.

title: A Theory of Random Graph Shift in Truncated-Spectrum vRKHS
summary: This paper develops a theory of graph classification under domain shift through a random-graph generative lens, where we consider intra-class graphs sharing the same random graph model (RGM) and the domain shift induced by changes in RGM components. While classic domain adaptation (DA) theories have well-underpinned existing techniques to handle graph distribution shift, the information of graph samples, which are itself structured objects, is less explored. The non-Euclidean nature of graphs and specialized architectures for graph learning further complicate a fine-grained analysis of graph distribution shifts. In this paper, we propose a theory that assumes RGM as the data generative process, exploiting its connection to hypothesis complexity in function space perspective for such fine-grained analysis. Building on a vector-valued reproducing kernel Hilbert space (vRKHS) formulation, we derive a generalization bound whose shift penalty admits a factorization into (i) a domain discrepancy term, (ii) a spectral-geometry term summarized by the accessible truncated spectrum, and (iii) an amplitude term that aggregates convergence and construction-stability effects. We empirically verify the insights on these terms in both real data and simulations.

AI.tldr: Proposes a theory for graph classification under domain shift using random graph models (RGM) and a vector-valued RKHS, yielding a generalization bound whose shift penalty factors into domain discrepancy, spectral-geometry (via a truncated spectrum), and an amplitude/stability component; validated empirically on real data and simulations.
AI.motivation: Graphs are non-Euclidean and their domain shifts arise from changes in the underlying generative process (RGM). Existing DA theories struggle to capture graph-structured data and fine-grained shift effects on hypothesis complexity.
AI.method: Model data with RGM as the generative process. Frame learning in a vector-valued RKHS (vRKHS). Derive a generalization bound for graph classification under distribution shift, where the shift penalty factorizes into three terms: (i) domain discrepancy, (ii) spectral-geometry (truncated spectrum), and (iii) amplitude (convergence and construction-stability). Validate insights through experiments on real data and simulated data.
AI.result: The shift penalty bound decomposes into interpretable components; empirical results support the relevance of domain discrepancy, spectral-geometry, and amplitude terms, demonstrating the theory's practical relevance for graph domain shift.
AI.conclusion: Provides a unified theoretical framework linking random-graph modeling, vRKHS-based generalization, and domain-shift analysis for graphs. Offers interpretable components to quantify and potentially control shift effects; validated with experiments.

title: SelfOccFlow: Towards end-to-end self-supervised 3D Occupancy Flow prediction
comment: Accepted version. Final version is published in IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters, DOI: 10.1109/LRA.2026.3665447
summary: Estimating 3D occupancy and motion at the vehicle's surroundings is essential for autonomous driving, enabling situational awareness in dynamic environments. Existing approaches jointly learn geometry and motion but rely on expensive 3D occupancy and flow annotations, velocity labels from bounding boxes, or pretrained optical flow models. We propose a self-supervised method for 3D occupancy flow estimation that eliminates the need for human-produced annotations or external flow supervision. Our method disentangles the scene into separate static and dynamic signed distance fields and learns motion implicitly through temporal aggregation. Additionally, we introduce a strong self-supervised flow cue derived from features' cosine similarities. We demonstrate the efficacy of our 3D occupancy flow method on SemanticKITTI, KITTI-MOT, and nuScenes.

AI.tldr: Self-supervised 3D occupancy flow estimation via disentangled static/dynamic SDFs with temporal aggregation and a cosine-similarity flow cue; achieves 3D motion understanding without human annotations across driving datasets (SemanticKITTI, KITTI-MOT, nuScenes).
AI.motivation: Eliminate dependence on expensive 3D occupancy/flow annotations and external flow supervision while enabling robust 3D motion understanding for autonomous driving.
AI.method: Decompose the scene into separate static and dynamic signed distance fields (SDFs). Learn motion implicitly through temporal aggregation of scene representations. Introduce a strong self-supervised flow cue derived from cosine similarities of features. Train end-to-end in a self-supervised framework.
AI.result: Demonstrates efficacy of the 3D occupancy flow method on SemanticKITTI, KITTI-MOT, and nuScenes, indicating viable self-supervised learning for 3D occupancy and motion without annotated data across multiple driving datasets.
AI.conclusion: Shows that 3D occupancy flow can be learned without human annotations by disentangling static/dynamic components and leveraging temporal aggregation plus feature-based self-supervision, with validation on standard autonomous-driving benchmarks.

title: LK Losses: Direct Acceptance Rate Optimization for Speculative Decoding
summary: Speculative decoding accelerates autoregressive large language model (LLM) inference by using a lightweight draft model to propose candidate tokens that are then verified in parallel by the target model. The speedup is significantly determined by the acceptance rate, yet standard training minimizes Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence as a proxy objective. While KL divergence and acceptance rate share the same global optimum, small draft models, having limited capacity, typically converge to suboptimal solutions where minimizing KL does not guarantee maximizing acceptance rate. To address this issue, we propose LK losses, special training objectives that directly target acceptance rate. Comprehensive experiments across four draft architectures and six target models, ranging from 8B to 685B parameters, demonstrate consistent improvements in acceptance metrics across all configurations compared to the standard KL-based training. We evaluate our approach on general, coding and math domains and report gains of up to 8-10% in average acceptance length. LK losses are easy to implement, introduce no computational overhead and can be directly integrated into any existing speculator training framework, making them a compelling alternative to the existing draft training objectives.

AI.tldr: LK losses directly optimize acceptance rate in speculative decoding for autoregressive LLMs, achieving consistent 8-10% gains in average accepted length across multiple draft architectures and target models (8B–685B), with no extra computational overhead.
AI.motivation: KL divergence-based training is an imperfect proxy for acceptance rate in speculative decoding; small draft models often converge to suboptimal solutions where minimizing KL does not maximize acceptance. Directly targeting acceptance rate should align training with the speedup objective.
AI.method: Introduce LK losses as training objectives that directly target acceptance rate. Evaluate across four draft architectures and six target models (8B–685B), covering general, coding, and math domains. Demonstrate improvements in acceptance metrics, with easy integration into existing speculator training frameworks and no additional computational overhead.
AI.result: LK losses yield consistent improvements in acceptance metrics across all configurations, with gains of up to 8-10% in average acceptance length. Demonstrates robustness across domains, and across a wide range of model sizes.
AI.conclusion: LK losses offer a simple, plug-in alternative to KL-based draft training that directly optimizes the speedup objective in speculative decoding, requiring no extra compute and applicable across diverse model scales and domains.

title: Ref-Adv: Exploring MLLM Visual Reasoning in Referring Expression Tasks
summary: Referring Expression Comprehension (REC) links language to region level visual perception. Standard benchmarks (RefCOCO, RefCOCO+, RefCOCOg) have progressed rapidly with multimodal LLMs but remain weak tests of visual reasoning and grounding: (i) many expressions are very short, leaving little reasoning demand; (ii) images often contain few distractors, making the target easy to find; and (iii) redundant descriptors enable shortcut solutions that bypass genuine text understanding and visual reasoning. We introduce Ref-Adv, a modern REC benchmark that suppresses shortcuts by pairing linguistically nontrivial expressions with only the information necessary to uniquely identify the target. The dataset contains referring expressions on real images, curated with hard distractors and annotated with reasoning facets including negation. We conduct comprehensive ablations (word order perturbations and descriptor deletion sufficiency) to show that solving Ref-Adv requires reasoning beyond simple cues, and we evaluate a broad suite of contemporary multimodal LLMs on Ref-Adv. Despite strong results on RefCOCO, RefCOCO+, and RefCOCOg, models drop markedly on Ref-Adv, revealing reliance on shortcuts and gaps in visual reasoning and grounding. We provide an in depth failure analysis and aim for Ref-Adv to guide future work on visual reasoning and grounding in MLLMs.

AI.tldr: Ref-Adv is a challenging REFERRING EXPRESSION COMPREHENSION benchmark that removes shortcut cues by pairing linguistically nontrivial expressions with minimal info to uniquely identify a target, using real images and hard distractors, revealing that state-of-the-art multimodal LLMs rely on shortcuts on standard benchmarks and struggle on Ref-Adv.
AI.motivation: Standard REC benchmarks enable shortcut reasoning and under-test true grounding; a rigorous benchmark is needed to assess genuine language-vision reasoning and grounding in MLLMs.
AI.method: Construct Ref-Adv by selecting real-image expressions that are linguistically nontrivial yet require the necessary information to identify the target; insert hard distractors; annotate with reasoning facets including negation; perform word-order perturbations and descriptor-deletion ablations; evaluate contemporary multimodal LLMs.
AI.result: Models show strong performance on RefCOCO/RefCOCO+/RefCOCOg but exhibit marked drops on Ref-Adv, indicating reliance on shortcuts; ablations demonstrate that solving Ref-Adv requires more than surface-level cues; in-depth failure analysis provided.
AI.conclusion: Ref-Adv provides a more robust benchmark for visual reasoning and grounding in multimodal LLMs and should guide future research towards reducing shortcut exploitation and improving genuine reasoning capabilities.

title: Hierarchical Concept-based Interpretable Models
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026
summary: Modern deep neural networks remain challenging to interpret due to the opacity of their latent representations, impeding model understanding, debugging, and debiasing. Concept Embedding Models (CEMs) address this by mapping inputs to human-interpretable concept representations from which tasks can be predicted. Yet, CEMs fail to represent inter-concept relationships and require concept annotations at different granularities during training, limiting their applicability. In this paper, we introduce Hierarchical Concept Embedding Models (HiCEMs), a new family of CEMs that explicitly model concept relationships through hierarchical structures. To enable HiCEMs in real-world settings, we propose Concept Splitting, a method for automatically discovering finer-grained sub-concepts from a pretrained CEM's embedding space without requiring additional annotations. This allows HiCEMs to generate fine-grained explanations from limited concept labels, reducing annotation burdens. Our evaluation across multiple datasets, including a user study and experiments on PseudoKitchens, a newly proposed concept-based dataset of 3D kitchen renders, demonstrates that (1) Concept Splitting discovers human-interpretable sub-concepts absent during training that can be used to train highly accurate HiCEMs, and (2) HiCEMs enable powerful test-time concept interventions at different granularities, leading to improved task accuracy.

AI.tldr: Hierarchical Concept Embedding Models (HiCEMs) extend Concept Embedding Models by incorporating hierarchical concept representations to capture inter-concept relationships. Concept Splitting automatically discovers fine-grained sub-concepts from a pretrained CEM embedding space without additional annotations, enabling fine-grained explanations and test-time interventions. Evaluated on multiple datasets including PseudoKitchens and a user study, demonstrating that discovered sub-concepts improve HiCEM training and that HiCEMs enable effective interventions and improved task accuracy.
AI.motivation: CEMs offer interpretability via concept mappings but miss inter-concept relationships and require multi-granularity annotations, limiting applicability. There is a need for scalable, hierarchy-aware interpretability with minimal labeling.
AI.method: Introduce Hierarchical Concept Embedding Models (HiCEMs) to model concept relationships through hierarchical structures. Propose Concept Splitting to automatically discover finer-grained sub-concepts from a pretrained CEM's embedding space without new annotations. Evaluate on several datasets including PseudoKitchens and conduct a user study to assess interpretability and intervention capability.
AI.result: Concept Splitting reveals human-interpretable sub-concepts not present during training, which can train high-quality HiCEMs. HiCEMs enable powerful test-time concept interventions at multiple granularities, leading to improved task accuracy.
AI.conclusion: HiCEMs with Concept Splitting offer scalable, interpretable representations that capture concept hierarchies and support granular, test-time interventions, reducing annotation burden while improving performance.

title: Experience-Guided Self-Adaptive Cascaded Agents for Breast Cancer Screening and Diagnosis with Reduced Biopsy Referrals
summary: We propose an experience-guided cascaded multi-agent framework for Breast Ultrasound Screening and Diagnosis, called BUSD-Agent, that aims to reduce diagnostic escalation and unnecessary biopsy referrals. Our framework models screening and diagnosis as a two-stage, selective decision-making process. A lightweight `screening clinic' agent, restricted to classification models as tools, selectively filters out benign and normal cases from further diagnostic escalation when malignancy risk and uncertainty are estimated as low. Cases that have higher risks are escalated to the `diagnostic clinic' agent, which integrates richer perception and radiological description tools to make a secondary decision on biopsy referral. To improve agent performance, past records of pathology-confirmed outcomes along with image embeddings, model predictions, and historical agent actions are stored in a memory bank as structured decision trajectories. For each new case, BUSD-Agent retrieves similar past cases based on image, model response and confidence similarity to condition the agent's current decision policy. This enables retrieval-conditioned in-context adaptation that dynamically adjusts model trust and escalation thresholds from prior experiences without parameter updates. Evaluation across 10 breast ultrasound datasets shows that the proposed experience-guided workflow reduces diagnostic escalation in BUSD-Agent from 84.95% to 58.72% and overall biopsy referrals from 59.50% to 37.08%, compared to the same architecture without trajectory conditioning, while improving average screening specificity by 68.48% and diagnostic specificity by 6.33%.

AI.tldr: A two-stage, experience-guided multi-agent framework (BUSD-Agent) for breast ultrasound screening and diagnosis using memory-driven retrieval to adapt escalation thresholds, reducing unnecessary biopsies and improving specificity without retraining.
AI.motivation: Address high diagnostic escalation and biopsy referral rates in breast ultrasound screening by leveraging past pathology-confirmed outcomes and decision trajectories to condition current policies, enabling selective, non-parametric adaptation.
AI.method: A cascaded architecture with: (1) a screening clinic agent (classification tools) that filters out benign/normal cases when malignancy risk and uncertainty are low; (2) a diagnostic clinic agent with richer perception and radiological description tools that decides on biopsy referral for higher-risk cases. Past outcomes, image embeddings, model predictions, and historical actions are stored in a memory bank as structured decision trajectories. For new cases, the system retrieves similar past cases by image, model response, and confidence to condition the agent’s current policy (retrieval-conditioned in-context adaptation) without updating model parameters.
AI.result: Evaluated on 10 breast ultrasound datasets: diagnostic escalation reduced from 84.95% to 58.72%; biopsy referrals reduced from 59.50% to 37.08%; average screening specificity improved by 68.48% and diagnostic specificity by 6.33% relative to a version without trajectory conditioning.
AI.conclusion: Retrieval-conditioned, memory-guided adaptation enhances decision efficiency and accuracy in breast ultrasound screening and diagnosis without parameter updates, suggesting practical utility across diverse datasets.

title: SegMate: Asymmetric Attention-Based Lightweight Architecture for Efficient Multi-Organ Segmentation
summary: State-of-the-art models for medical image segmentation achieve excellent accuracy but require substantial computational resources, limiting deployment in resource-constrained clinical settings. We present SegMate, an efficient 2.5D framework that achieves state-of-the-art accuracy, while considerably reducing computational requirements. Our efficient design is the result of meticulously integrating asymmetric architectures, attention mechanisms, multi-scale feature fusion, slice-based positional conditioning, and multi-task optimization. We demonstrate the efficiency-accuracy trade-off of our framework across three modern backbones (EfficientNetV2-M, MambaOut-Tiny, FastViT-T12). We perform experiments on three datasets: TotalSegmentator, SegTHOR and AMOS22. Compared with the vanilla models, SegMate reduces computation (GFLOPs) by up to 2.5x and memory footprint (VRAM) by up to 2.1x, while generally registering performance gains of around 1%. On TotalSegmentator, we achieve a Dice score of 93.51% with only 295MB peak GPU memory. Zero-shot cross-dataset evaluations on SegTHOR and AMOS22 demonstrate strong generalization, with Dice scores of up to 86.85% and 89.35%, respectively. We release our open-source code at https://github.com/andreibunea99/SegMate.

AI.tldr: SegMate is a 2.5D segmentation framework that reduces computation and memory while maintaining or improving accuracy, validated with three backbones and three datasets; achieves substantial efficiency gains and competitive Dice scores; open-sourced.
AI.motivation: State-of-the-art medical image segmentation models deliver high accuracy but demand heavy computation, hindering deployment in resource-limited clinical settings.
AI.method: SegMate integrates asymmetric architectures, attention mechanisms, multi-scale feature fusion, slice-based positional conditioning, and multi-task optimization. It is evaluated across three backbones (EfficientNetV2-M, MambaOut-Tiny, FastViT-T12) on TotalSegmentator, SegTHOR, and AMOS22, highlighting efficiency-accuracy trade-offs.
AI.result: GFLOPs reduced up to 2.5x and VRAM down up to 2.1x with typical accuracy gains around 1%. On TotalSegmentator, Dice=93.51% with 295MB peak GPU memory. Zero-shot cross-dataset results: SegTHOR Dice=86.85%, AMOS22 Dice=89.35%.
AI.conclusion: SegMate demonstrates that substantial efficiency can be achieved without sacrificing performance in medical image segmentation, enabling deployment in resource-constrained settings; open-source code is released.

title: Intrinsic Lorentz Neural Network
summary: Real-world data frequently exhibit latent hierarchical structures, which can be naturally represented by hyperbolic geometry. Although recent hyperbolic neural networks have demonstrated promising results, many existing architectures remain partially intrinsic, mixing Euclidean operations with hyperbolic ones or relying on extrinsic parameterizations. To address it, we propose the \emph{Intrinsic Lorentz Neural Network} (ILNN), a fully intrinsic hyperbolic architecture that conducts all computations within the Lorentz model. At its core, the network introduces a novel \emph{point-to-hyperplane} fully connected layer (FC), replacing traditional Euclidean affine logits with closed-form hyperbolic distances from features to learned Lorentz hyperplanes, thereby ensuring that the resulting geometric decision functions respect the inherent curvature. Around this fundamental layer, we design intrinsic modules: GyroLBN, a Lorentz batch normalization that couples gyro-centering with gyro-scaling, consistently outperforming both LBN and GyroBN while reducing training time. We additionally proposed a gyro-additive bias for the FC output, a Lorentz patch-concatenation operator that aligns the expected log-radius across feature blocks via a digamma-based scale, and a Lorentz dropout layer. Extensive experiments conducted on CIFAR-10/100 and two genomic benchmarks (TEB and GUE) illustrate that ILNN achieves state-of-the-art performance and computational cost among hyperbolic models and consistently surpasses strong Euclidean baselines. The code is available at \href{https://github.com/Longchentong/ILNN}{\textcolor{magenta}{this url}}.

AI.tldr: ILNN is a fully intrinsic Lorentz neural network for hyperbolic geometry, using a novel point-to-hyperplane FC layer with hyperbolic distance, plus intrinsic modules (GyroLBN, gyro bias, patch-concatenation, Lorentz dropout). It achieves state-of-the-art or strong Euclidean baselines on CIFAR-10/100 and two genomic benchmarks, with reduced training time. Code available.
AI.motivation: Real-world data often have latent hierarchical structures best captured by hyperbolic geometry. Many hyperbolic nets mix Euclidean and hyperbolic computations, or rely on extrinsic parameterizations. A fully intrinsic Lorentz-based architecture aims to respect curvature and improve efficiency and performance.
AI.method: Introduce Intrinsic Lorentz Neural Network (ILNN) fully operating in the Lorentz model. Core: a point-to-hyperplane fully connected layer that uses closed-form hyperbolic distances to learned Lorentz hyperplanes for logits. Intrinsic modules include GyroLBN (Lorentz batch normalization with gyro-centering/scaling), a gyro-additive bias for FC, a Lorentz patch-concatenation operator with a digamma-based scale, and Lorentz dropout. Training and evaluations on CIFAR-10/100 and two genomic benchmarks (TEB, GUE).
AI.result: ILNN demonstrates state-of-the-art performance and favorable computational cost among hyperbolic models and consistently surpasses strong Euclidean baselines on the tested datasets.
AI.conclusion: A fully intrinsic Lorentz architecture with novel intrinsic layers and a point-to-hyperplane FC layer effectively leverages hyperbolic geometry to model hierarchical structure, offering improved accuracy and efficiency over existing hyperbolic and Euclidean baselines. Code is available online.

title: Half-Truths Break Similarity-Based Retrieval
summary: When a text description is extended with an additional detail, image-text similarity should drop if that detail is wrong. We show that CLIP-style dual encoders often violate this intuition: appending a plausible but incorrect object or relation to an otherwise correct description can increase the similarity score. We call such cases half-truths. On COCO, CLIP prefers the correct shorter description only 40.6% of the time, and performance drops to 32.9% when the added detail is a relation. We trace this vulnerability to weak supervision on caption parts: contrastive training aligns full sentences but does not explicitly enforce that individual entities and relations are grounded. We propose CS-CLIP (Component-Supervised CLIP), which decomposes captions into entity and relation units, constructs a minimally edited foil for each unit, and fine-tunes the model to score the correct unit above its foil while preserving standard dual-encoder inference. CS-CLIP raises half-truth accuracy to 69.3% and improves average performance on established compositional benchmarks by 5.7 points, suggesting that reducing half-truth errors aligns with broader gains in compositional understanding. Code is publicly available at: https://github.com/kargibora/CS-CLIP

AI.tldr: CS-CLIP reduces half-truth errors in CLIP-style models by grounding caption components (entities and relations), showing improved accuracy on half-truth detection and compositional benchmarks; code released at GitHub.
AI.motivation: Address weak supervision in CLIP training where full-sentence alignment does not guarantee grounding of individual entities and relations, leading to half-truth failures when adding plausible but incorrect details.
AI.method: Decompose captions into entity and relation units, generate minimally edited foils for each unit, and fine-tune the model to prefer the correct unit over the foil while preserving standard dual-encoder inference.
AI.result: Half-truth accuracy on COCO improves to 69.3%; average performance on compositional benchmarks increases by 5.7 points.
AI.conclusion: Reducing half-truth errors aligns with improved compositional understanding and indicates grounding improvements can benefit broader multimodal reasoning; code is publicly available.

title: MINT: Multimodal Imaging-to-Speech Knowledge Transfer for Early Alzheimer's Screening
summary: Alzheimer's disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder in which mild cognitive impairment (MCI) marks a critical transition between aging and dementia. Neuroimaging modalities, such as structural MRI, provide biomarkers of this transition; however, their high costs and infrastructure needs limit their deployment at a population scale. Speech analysis offers a non-invasive alternative, but speech-only classifiers are developed independently of neuroimaging, leaving decision boundaries biologically ungrounded and limiting reliability on the subtle CN-versus-MCI distinction. We propose MINT (Multimodal Imaging-to-Speech Knowledge Transfer), a three-stage cross-modal framework that transfers biomarker structure from MRI into a speech encoder at training time. An MRI teacher, trained on 1,228 subjects, defines a compact neuroimaging embedding space for CN-versus-MCI classification. A residual projection head aligns speech representations to this frozen imaging manifold via a combined geometric loss, adapting speech to the learned biomarker space while preserving imaging encoder fidelity. The frozen MRI classifier, which is never exposed to speech, is applied to aligned embeddings at inference and requires no scanner. Evaluation on ADNI-4 shows aligned speech achieves performance comparable to speech-only baselines (AUC 0.720 vs 0.711) while requiring no imaging at inference, demonstrating that MRI-derived decision boundaries can ground speech representations. Multimodal fusion improves over MRI alone (0.973 vs 0.958). Ablation studies identify dropout regularization and self-supervised pretraining as critical design decisions. To our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of MRI-to-speech knowledge transfer for early Alzheimer's screening, establishing a biologically grounded pathway for population-level cognitive triage without neuroimaging at inference.

AI.tldr: Introduces MINT, a cross-modal framework that transfers MRI-derived biomarker structure into a speech encoder to enable MRI-grounded, imaging-free speech classification of CN vs MCI, achieving competitive AUC and improved fusion performance on ADNI-4.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on costly neuroimaging for large-scale Alzheimer's screening by grounding speech-based classifiers in biologically meaningful MRI-derived decision boundaries; enable population-level triage without scanners at inference.
AI.method: A three-stage pipeline: (1) Train an MRI teacher on 1,228 subjects to define a compact imaging embedding space for CN vs MCI classification; (2) Use a residual projection head to align speech representations to the frozen MRI manifold via a combined geometric loss, adapting speech to the biomarker space while preserving MRI encoder fidelity; (3) Apply the frozen MRI classifier to the aligned speech embeddings at inference, with no exposure to imaging data; optional multimodal fusion enhances performance; ablation studies highlight dropout regularization and self-supervised pretraining as critical.
AI.result: On ADNI-4, aligned speech achieves AUC 0.720 (close to speech-only baseline 0.711); multimodal fusion yields AUC 0.973 (MRI-alone 0.958); the approach requires no MRI at inference while maintaining biomarker-grounded decision boundaries.
AI.conclusion: MRI-to-speech knowledge transfer can biologically ground speech-based screening for Alzheimer's, enabling population-scale cognitive triage without neuroimaging at inference and offering a pathway to combine imaging-derived biomarkers with scalable audio-based screening.

title: Foundation World Models for Agents that Learn, Verify, and Adapt Reliably Beyond Static Environments
comment: AAMAS 2026, Blue Sky Idea Track. 4 pages, 1 Figure
summary: The next generation of autonomous agents must not only learn efficiently but also act reliably and adapt their behavior in open worlds. Standard approaches typically assume fixed tasks and environments with little or no novelty, which limits world models' ability to support agents that must evolve their policies as conditions change. This paper outlines a vision for foundation world models: persistent, compositional representations that unify reinforcement learning, reactive/program synthesis, and abstraction mechanisms. We propose an agenda built around four components: (i) learnable reward models from specifications to support optimization with clear objectives; (ii) adaptive formal verification integrated throughout learning; (iii) online abstraction calibration to quantify the reliability of the model's predictions; and (iv) test-time synthesis and world-model generation guided by verifiers. Together, these components enable agents to synthesize verifiable programs, derive new policies from a small number of interactions, and maintain correctness while adapting to novelty. The resulting framework positions foundation world models as a substrate for learning, reasoning, and adaptation, laying the groundwork for agents that not only act well but can explain and justify the behavior they adopt.

AI.tldr: A framework for foundation world models: persistent, compositional representations that unify reinforcement learning, reactive/program synthesis, and abstraction to enable verifiable, adaptive agents in open worlds.
AI.motivation: Agents must operate reliably in open-world environments with novelty and changing conditions. Fixed-task assumptions limit adaptability and reliability; need persistent, compositional world models that support learning, reasoning, and verification.
AI.method: Proposes a four-component agenda: (i) learnable reward models from specifications; (ii) adaptive formal verification embedded in learning; (iii) online abstraction calibration to quantify prediction reliability; (iv) test-time synthesis and world-model generation guided by verifiers.
AI.result: A conceptual framework/agenda rather than empirical results, outlining how agents can synthesize verifiable programs, derive new policies from limited interactions, and maintain correctness while adapting to novelty.
AI.conclusion: Foundation world models can serve as a substrate for learning, reasoning, and adaptation, enabling agents that act well, explain their behavior, and justify decisions in evolving environments.

title: Leveraging Geometric Prior Uncertainty and Complementary Constraints for High-Fidelity Neural Indoor Surface Reconstruction
summary: Neural implicit surface reconstruction with signed distance function has made significant progress, but recovering fine details such as thin structures and complex geometries remains challenging due to unreliable or noisy geometric priors. Existing approaches rely on implicit uncertainty that arises during optimization to filter these priors, which is indirect and inefficient, and masking supervision in high-uncertainty regions further leads to under-constrained optimization. To address these issues, we propose GPU-SDF, a neural implicit framework for indoor surface reconstruction that leverages geometric prior uncertainty and complementary constraints. We introduce a self-supervised module that explicitly estimates prior uncertainty without auxiliary networks. Based on this estimation, we design an uncertainty-guided loss that modulates prior influence rather than discarding it, thereby retaining weak but informative cues. To address regions with high prior uncertainty, GPU-SDF further incorporates two complementary constraints: an edge distance field that strengthens boundary supervision and a multi-view consistency regularization that enforces geometric coherence. Extensive experiments confirm that GPU-SDF improves the reconstruction of fine details and serves as a plug-and-play enhancement for existing frameworks. Source code will be available at https://github.com/IRMVLab/GPU-SDF

AI.tldr: GPU-SDF introduces a self-supervised prior-uncertainty estimator for neural implicit surface reconstruction, using an uncertainty-guided loss and two complementary constraints (edge distance field and multi-view consistency) to preserve fine details. It serves as a plug‑in enhancement for existing methods; source code will be released.
AI.motivation: Fine geometric priors are noisy in neural implicit SDF-based reconstructions, and relying on implicit uncertainty alone is indirect and inefficient. Modulating, rather than discarding, priors and addressing high-uncertainty regions is needed to recover thin structures and complex geometries.
AI.method: A self-supervised module estimates prior uncertainty without auxiliary networks. An uncertainty-guided loss modulates prior influence rather than masking them. Two complementary constraints are added: (1) an edge distance field to strengthen boundary supervision, and (2) a multi-view consistency regularization to enforce geometric coherence. GPU-SDF is designed as a plug‑in to improve existing frameworks.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate improved reconstruction of fine details in indoor scenes. The approach acts as a plug‑in enhancement for existing neural implicit surface methods, and code will be available at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware geometric priors with edge-distance supervision and multi-view consistency effectively enhance neural implicit surface reconstruction, enabling better recovery of thin structures and complex geometries.

title: InfoNCE Induces Gaussian Distribution
summary: Contrastive learning has become a cornerstone of modern representation learning, allowing training with massive unlabeled data for both task-specific and general (foundation) models. A prototypical loss in contrastive training is InfoNCE and its variants. In this work, we show that the InfoNCE objective induces Gaussian structure in representations that emerge from contrastive training. We establish this result in two complementary regimes. First, we show that under certain alignment and concentration assumptions, projections of the high-dimensional representation asymptotically approach a multivariate Gaussian distribution. Next, under less strict assumptions, we show that adding a small asymptotically vanishing regularization term that promotes low feature norm and high feature entropy leads to similar asymptotic results. We support our analysis with experiments on synthetic and CIFAR-10 datasets across multiple encoder architectures and sizes, demonstrating consistent Gaussian behavior. This perspective provides a principled explanation for commonly observed Gaussianity in contrastive representations. The resulting Gaussian model enables principled analytical treatment of learned representations and is expected to support a wide range of applications in contrastive learning.

AI.tldr: InfoNCE training induces Gaussian structure in learned representations under two regimes, with theory and experiments supporting Gaussianity and enabling analytical treatment.
AI.motivation: Explain and leverage the empirically observed Gaussianity of contrastive representations to enable principled analytical analysis and broader applications.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis in two regimes: (1) with alignment and concentration assumptions, high-dimensional representations projected to multivariate Gaussian; (2) under weaker assumptions, add a vanishing regularization term that enforces low norm and high entropy, yielding similar Gaussian behavior. Empirical validation on synthetic data and CIFAR-10 across multiple encoders and sizes.
AI.result: Proves and demonstrates that InfoNCE leads to asymptotic Gaussianity of representations under the proposed conditions; experiments show consistent Gaussian behavior.
AI.conclusion: A Gaussian model for contrastive representations provides a principled analytical framework for understanding and exploiting learned representations, with broad potential applications in contrastive and foundation-model training.

title: PointCoT: A Multi-modal Benchmark for Explicit 3D Geometric Reasoning
summary: While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) demonstrate proficiency in 2D scenes, extending their perceptual intelligence to 3D point cloud understanding remains a significant challenge. Current approaches focus primarily on aligning 3D features with pre-trained models. However, they typically treat geometric reasoning as an implicit mapping process. These methods bypass intermediate logical steps and consequently suffer from geometric hallucinations. They confidently generate plausible responses that fail to ground in precise structural details. To bridge this gap, we present PointCoT, a novel framework that empowers MLLMs with explicit Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning for 3D data. We advocate for a \textit{Look, Think, then Answer} paradigm. In this approach, the model is supervised to generate geometry-grounded rationales before predicting final answers. To facilitate this, we construct Point-Reason-Instruct, a large-scale benchmark comprising $\sim$86k instruction-tuning samples with hierarchical CoT annotations. By leveraging a dual-stream multi-modal architecture, our method synergizes semantic appearance with geometric truth. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PointCoT achieves state-of-the-art performance on complex reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: PointCoT introduces explicit chain-of-thought reasoning for 3D MLLMs, via Look–Think–Then–Answer, a large-scale Point-Reason-Instruct benchmark (~86k samples) and a dual-stream architecture, achieving state-of-the-art on complex 3D reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Current 3D MLLM approaches rely on implicit feature alignment and often suffer geometric hallucinations; there is a need for geometry-grounded, stepwise reasoning to improve reliability and grounding in 3D data.
AI.method: Proposes Look, Think, then Answer: supervise generation of geometry-grounded rationales before final answers. Introduces Point-Reason-Instruct, ~86k instruction-tuning samples with hierarchical CoT annotations. Employs a dual-stream multi-modal architecture that fuses semantic appearance with geometric truth.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on complex reasoning tasks and enhanced grounding in geometric details.
AI.conclusion: Explicit CoT reasoning for 3D data enhances grounding and reliability in MLLMs for point cloud understanding; the Point-Reason-Instruct benchmark supports scalable instruction tuning and sets a new direction for geometry-aware multimodal reasoning.

title: RewardUQ: A Unified Framework for Uncertainty-Aware Reward Models
summary: Reward models are central to aligning large language models (LLMs) with human preferences. Yet most approaches rely on pointwise reward estimates that overlook the epistemic uncertainty in reward models arising from limited human feedback. Recent work suggests that quantifying this uncertainty can reduce the costs of human annotation via uncertainty-guided active learning and mitigate reward overoptimization in LLM post-training. However, uncertainty-aware reward models have so far been adopted without thorough comparison, leaving them poorly understood. This work introduces a unified framework, RewardUQ, to systematically evaluate uncertainty quantification for reward models. We compare common methods along standard metrics measuring accuracy and calibration, and we propose a new ranking strategy incorporating both dimensions for a simplified comparison. Our experimental results suggest that model size and initialization have the most meaningful impact on performance, and most prior work could have benefited from alternative design choices. To foster the development and evaluation of new methods and aid the deployment in downstream applications, we release our open-source framework as a Python package. Our code is available at https://github.com/lasgroup/rewarduq.

AI.tldr: Introduces RewardUQ, a unified framework to evaluate uncertainty quantification for reward models, with a comparative study on accuracy and calibration and a new ranking metric; finds model size and initialization most influence performance; releases open-source code.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of thorough comparison for uncertainty-aware reward models and to quantify epistemic uncertainty due to limited human feedback, enabling more efficient annotation and reducing reward overoptimization.
AI.method: Proposes RewardUQ, a unified evaluation framework that systematically compares uncertainty quantification methods for reward models along standard accuracy and calibration metrics, and introduces a ranking strategy that combines both criteria; provides open-source Python package.
AI.result: Experimental results indicate that model size and initialization have the most meaningful impact on reward-model performance, and that many prior approaches could have benefited from alternative design choices; the framework and code are released to facilitate future research and deployment.
AI.conclusion: RewardUQ enables systematic development and evaluation of uncertainty-aware reward models and supports downstream deployment; the authors provide an open-source Python package at the referenced GitHub repository.

title: pathsig: A GPU-Accelerated Library for Truncated and Projected Path Signatures
summary: Path signatures provide a rich representation of sequential data, with strong theoretical guarantees and good performance in a variety of machine-learning tasks. While signatures have progressed from fixed feature extractors to trainable components of machine-learning models, existing libraries often lack the required scalability for large-scale, gradient-based learning. To address this gap, this paper introduces pathsig, a PyTorch-native library that computes path signatures directly in the word basis. By using CUDA kernels to update signature coefficients in parallel over prefix-closed word sets, pathsig achieves high GPU throughput and near-minimal peak memory. Compared with other libraries, pathsig achieves 10-30x speedups for computation of truncated signatures and up to 4-10x speedups in training that require backpropagation through the signature. Beyond regular truncation, pathsig supports projections of the (infinite-dimensional) signature onto user-specified sets of words and anisotropic truncation motivated by inhomogeneous path regularity, enabling more compact representations that can reduce dimensionality, redundancy, and computational cost.

AI.tldr: CUDA-accelerated PyTorch library for path signatures that enables scalable gradient-based learning via word-basis computations, with word-set projections and anisotropic truncation, achieving large speedups and reduced memory.
AI.motivation: Path signatures offer a rich, theoretically grounded representation for sequential data, but existing libraries struggle to scale for large-scale, gradient-based learning due to computational and memory bottlenecks; integration with PyTorch and GPU acceleration are needed.
AI.method: Compute signatures directly in the word basis using CUDA kernels that update signature coefficients in parallel over prefix-closed word sets; supports backpropagation through the signature; enables high GPU throughput with near-minimal peak memory; additionally provides projections of the infinite-dimensional signature onto user-specified word sets and anisotropic truncation to adapt to varying path regularity.
AI.result: Significant performance gains over existing libraries: 10-30x speedups for truncated signatures and 4-10x speedups in training that requires backpropagation; high GPU throughput and low memory footprint; ability to obtain more compact representations via projections and anisotropic truncation, reducing dimensionality, redundancy, and computational costs.
AI.conclusion: Pathsig addresses the scalability gap for path-signature-based learning by delivering a GPU-accelerated, controllable, and memory-efficient implementation that supports flexible truncation schemes and backpropagation, enabling more practical large-scale applications.

title: Leveraging Non-linear Dimension Reduction and Random Walk Co-occurrence for Node Embedding
summary: Leveraging non-linear dimension reduction techniques, we remove the low dimension constraint from node embedding and propose COVE, an explainable high dimensional embedding that, when reduced to low dimension with UMAP, slightly increases performance on clustering and link prediction tasks. The embedding is inspired by neural embedding methods that use co-occurrence on a random walk as an indication of similarity, and is closely related to a diffusion process. Extending on recent community detection benchmarks, we find that a COVE UMAP HDBSCAN pipeline performs similarly to the popular Louvain algorithm.

AI.tldr: Proposes COVE, an explainable high-dimensional node embedding; via co-occurrence on random walks and diffusion-inspired modeling; reduced with UMAP; achieves slight gains in clustering and link prediction; COVE-UMAP-HDBSCAN matches Louvain on community detection.
AI.motivation: Remove the fixed low-dimensional constraint in node embeddings, enable explainability, and assess how non-linear dimensionality reduction (UMAP) affects downstream tasks.
AI.method: Develop COVE embedding based on co-occurrence from random walks, framed as a diffusion process; apply non-linear dimension reduction (UMAP) to obtain a lower-dimensional representation; use HDBSCAN for clustering; benchmark on clustering, link prediction, and community detection tasks against baseline methods like Louvain.
AI.result: Using UMAP to reduce COVE yields slight performance gains in clustering and link prediction; the COVE-UMAP-HDBSCAN pipeline performs similarly to Louvain on community detection benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: High-dimensional, explainable embeddings can be effective after non-linear reduction, yielding competitive performance with established community detection methods; motivates further exploration and benchmarking across tasks and datasets.

title: CC-VQA: Conflict- and Correlation-Aware Method for Mitigating Knowledge Conflict in Knowledge-Based Visual Question Answering
summary: Knowledge-based visual question answering (KB-VQA) demonstrates significant potential for handling knowledge-intensive tasks. However, conflicts arise between static parametric knowledge in vision language models (VLMs) and dynamically retrieved information due to the static model knowledge from pre-training. The outputs either ignore retrieved contexts or exhibit inconsistent integration with parametric knowledge, posing substantial challenges for KB-VQA. Current knowledge conflict mitigation methods primarily adapted from language-based approaches, focusing on context-level conflicts through engineered prompting strategies or context-aware decoding mechanisms. However, these methods neglect the critical role of visual information in conflicts and suffer from redundant retrieved contexts, which impair accurate conflict identification and effective mitigation. To address these limitations, we propose \textbf{CC-VQA}: a novel training-free, conflict- and correlation-aware method for KB-VQA. Our method comprises two core components: (1) Vision-Centric Contextual Conflict Reasoning, which performs visual-semantic conflict analysis across internal and external knowledge contexts; and (2) Correlation-Guided Encoding and Decoding, featuring positional encoding compression for low-correlation statements and adaptive decoding using correlation-weighted conflict scoring. Extensive evaluations on E-VQA, InfoSeek, and OK-VQA benchmarks demonstrate that CC-VQA achieves state-of-the-art performance, yielding absolute accuracy improvements of 3.3\% to 6.4\% compared to existing methods. Code is available at https://github.com/cqu-student/CC-VQA.

AI.tldr: CC-VQA is a training-free, conflict- and correlation-aware KB-VQA method with two components: Vision-Centric Contextual Conflict Reasoning and Correlation-Guided Encoding/Decoding. It addresses visual-information conflicts and redundant retrieved context, achieving state-of-the-art results on E-VQA, InfoSeek, and OK-VQA with 3.3–6.4 percentage point accuracy gains.
AI.motivation: Knowledge-based VQA struggles due to conflicts between static parametric knowledge in VLMs and dynamically retrieved knowledge, especially when visual context matters for identifying conflicts and when retrieved context is redundant. Existing prompt-/context-based mitigation neglect visual factors and over-rely on contexts.
AI.method: Two-part approach: (1) Vision-Centric Contextual Conflict Reasoning analyzes conflicts across internal (model) and external (retrieved) knowledge using visual-semantic cues; (2) Correlation-Guided Encoding/Decoding compresses low-correlation statements via positional encoding compression and uses correlation-weighted conflict scoring to adapt decoding.
AI.result: CC-VQA achieves state-of-the-art performance on E-VQA, InfoSeek, and OK-VQA, with absolute accuracy gains of 3.3%–6.4% over existing methods.
AI.conclusion: A training-free, conflict- and correlation-aware VQA framework that explicitly handles visual information in conflicts and reduces redundant retrieval context, leading to robust KB-VQA performance without model retraining.

title: Adaptive Correlation-Weighted Intrinsic Rewards for Reinforcement Learning
summary: We propose ACWI (Adaptive Correlation Weighted Intrinsic), an adaptive intrinsic reward scaling framework designed to dynamically balance intrinsic and extrinsic rewards for improved exploration in sparse reward reinforcement learning. Unlike conventional approaches that rely on manually tuned scalar coefficients, which often result in unstable or suboptimal performance across tasks, ACWI learns a state dependent scaling coefficient online. Specifically, ACWI introduces a lightweight Beta Network that predicts the intrinsic reward weight directly from the agent state through an encoder based architecture. The scaling mechanism is optimized using a correlation based objective that encourages alignment between the weighted intrinsic rewards and discounted future extrinsic returns. This formulation enables task adaptive exploration incentives while preserving computational efficiency and training stability. We evaluate ACWI on a suite of sparse reward environments in MiniGrid. Experimental results demonstrate that ACWI consistently improves sample efficiency and learning stability compared to fixed intrinsic reward baselines, achieving superior performance with minimal computational overhead.

AI.tldr: ACWI introduces an online, state-dependent intrinsic reward weighting via a Beta Network, using a correlation-based objective to align intrinsic rewards with extrinsic returns, improving exploration in sparse-reward RL with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Fixed intrinsic reward coefficients are brittle across tasks, often causing unstable or suboptimal learning in sparse reward settings; a dynamic, state-conditioned weighting can better balance exploration and exploitation.
AI.method: A lightweight Beta Network predicts a scalar intrinsic weight from the agent state using an encoder-based architecture. The weight scales intrinsic rewards; the scaling is trained with a correlation-based objective that aligns weighted intrinsic rewards with discounted future extrinsic returns, enabling online, efficient adaptation.
AI.result: In MiniGrid sparse reward environments, ACWI improves sample efficiency and training stability compared with fixed intrinsic reward baselines, achieving superior performance with minimal computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: ACWI offers a practical, task-adaptive mechanism to balance intrinsic and extrinsic rewards, preserving stability and efficiency while enhancing exploration in sparse-reward RL; the approach appears generalizable to similar settings with online weighting.

title: GDA-YOLO11: Amodal Instance Segmentation for Occlusion-Robust Robotic Fruit Harvesting
summary: Occlusion remains a critical challenge in robotic fruit harvesting, as undetected or inaccurately localised fruits often results in substantial crop losses. To mitigate this issue, we propose a harvesting framework using a new amodal segmentation model, GDA-YOLO11, which incorporates architectural improvements and an updated asymmetric mask loss. The proposed model is trained on a modified version of a public citrus dataset and evaluated on both the base dataset and occlusion-sensitive subsets with varying occlusion levels. Within the framework, full fruit masks, including invisible regions, are inferred by GDA-YOLO11, and picking points are subsequently estimated using the Euclidean distance transform. These points are then projected into 3D coordinates for robotic harvesting execution. Experiments were conducted using real citrus fruits in a controlled environment simulating occlusion scenarios. Notably, to the best of our knowledge, this study provides the first practical demonstration of amodal instance segmentation in robotic fruit harvesting. GDA-YOLO11 achieves a precision of 0.844, recall of 0.846, mAP@50 of 0.914, and mAP@50:95 of 0.636, outperforming YOLO11n by 5.1%, 1.3%, and 1.0% in precision, mAP@50, and mAP@50:95, respectively. The framework attains harvesting success rates of 92.59%, 85.18%, 48.14%, and 22.22% at zero to high occlusion levels, improving success by 3.5% under medium and high occlusion. These findings demonstrate that GDA-YOLO11 enhances occlusion robust segmentation and streamlines perception-to-action integration, paving the way for more reliable autonomous systems in agriculture.

AI.tldr: Proposes GDA-YOLO11 for amodal segmentation in robotic fruit harvesting; improves occlusion robustness, achieves high accuracy on citrus dataset; demonstrates first practical amodal segmentation in this domain; includes perception-to-action pipeline with 3D pickup.
AI.motivation: Occlusion in robotic fruit harvesting leads to crop losses; requires full fruit masks including occluded regions to guide harvesting.
AI.method: Architectural improvements to YOLO-based amodal segmentation: GDA-YOLO11 with updated asymmetric mask loss; train on a modified citrus dataset; evaluate on base and occlusion subsets; infer full masks; pick points via Euclidean distance transform; project to 3D for robot execution; validate with real citrus in occlusion scenarios.
AI.result: Precision 0.844, Recall 0.846, mAP@50 0.914, mAP@50:95 0.636; outruns YOLO11n by 5.1/1.3/1.0 percentage points; harvesting success at varying occlusion levels (92.59%, 85.18%, 48.14%, 22.22%) with +3.5% improvement under medium/high occlusion.
AI.conclusion: GDA-YOLO11 advances occlusion-robust amodal segmentation and enables streamlined perception-to-action in robotic harvesting; claims first practical demonstration in this domain.

title: Neural Diffusion Intensity Models for Point Process Data
summary: Cox processes model overdispersed point process data via a latent stochastic intensity, but both nonparametric estimation of the intensity model and posterior inference over intensity paths are typically intractable, relying on expensive MCMC methods. We introduce Neural Diffusion Intensity Models, a variational framework for Cox processes driven by neural SDEs. Our key theoretical result, based on enlargement of filtrations, shows that conditioning on point process observations preserves the diffusion structure of the latent intensity with an explicit drift correction. This guarantees the variational family contains the true posterior, so that ELBO maximization coincides with maximum likelihood estimation under sufficient model capacity. We design an amortized encoder architecture that maps variable-length event sequences to posterior intensity paths by simulating the drift-corrected SDE, replacing repeated MCMC runs with a single forward pass. Experiments on synthetic and real-world data demonstrate accurate recovery of latent intensity dynamics and posterior paths, with orders-of-magnitude speedups over MCMC-based methods.

AI.tldr: Neural Diffusion Intensity Models (NDIM) for Cox processes use neural SDEs to model latent intensity, with a theoretical drift correction ensuring the posterior is within the variational family; enables ELBO maximization to match MLE under sufficient capacity. An amortized encoder maps event sequences to posterior intensity paths, replacing MCMC with a single forward pass; experiments show accurate latent dynamics recovery and large speedups on synthetic and real data.
AI.motivation: Cox processes with latent stochastic intensities are powerful for overdispersed point process data but nonparametric intensity estimation and posterior path inference are computationally intractable with MCMC. A scalable variational approach is needed.
AI.method: Model latent intensity with neural SDEs; prove (via enlargement of filtrations) that conditioning on observations yields a drift-corrected diffusion, keeping the diffusion structure; design a variational family that contains the true posterior; use an amortized encoder to map variable-length event sequences to posterior intensity paths by simulating the drift-corrected SDE; replace repeated MCMC with a single forward pass.
AI.result: Empirical results on synthetic and real-world data show accurate recovery of latent intensity dynamics and posterior paths, with orders of magnitude speedups over MCMC-based methods.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a scalable, principled variational approach for Cox processes driven by neural diffusions, with theoretical guarantees and practical efficiency; it enables reliable inference of latent dynamic intensities from event data.

title: SwitchCraft: Training-Free Multi-Event Video Generation with Attention Controls
summary: Recent advances in text-to-video diffusion models have enabled high-fidelity and temporally coherent videos synthesis. However, current models are predominantly optimized for single-event generation. When handling multi-event prompts, without explicit temporal grounding, such models often produce blended or collapsed scenes that break the intended narrative. To address this limitation, we present SwitchCraft, a training-free framework for multi-event video generation. Our key insight is that uniform prompt injection across time ignores the correspondence between events and frames. To this end, we introduce Event-Aligned Query Steering (EAQS), which steers frame-level attention to align with relevant event prompts. Furthermore, we propose Auto-Balance Strength Solver (ABSS), which adaptively balances steering strength to preserve temporal consistency and visual fidelity. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SwitchCraft substantially improves prompt alignment, event clarity, and scene consistency compared with existing baselines, offering a simple yet effective solution for multi-event video generation.

AI.tldr: SwitchCraft is a training-free framework for multi-event video generation that aligns frame-level prompts with events to prevent blending or collapse across scenes, using Event-Aligned Query Steering (EAQS) and Auto-Balance Strength Solver (ABSS) to maintain temporal consistency and fidelity.
AI.motivation: Current text-to-video diffusion models struggle with multi-event prompts due to temporal misalignment when injecting prompts uniformly across all frames, leading to blended narratives and inconsistent scenes.
AI.method: Introduce EAQS to steer frame-level attention toward event-relevant prompts, replacing uniform prompt injection. Propose ABSS to adaptively balance steering strength to preserve temporal consistency and visual fidelity without additional training.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show improved prompt alignment, clearer event delineation, and stronger scene consistency compared with existing baselines for multi-event video generation.
AI.conclusion: SwitchCraft provides a simple, training-free approach to multi-event video generation by aligning frame-level attention with events and dynamically balancing steering strength, achieving better narrative coherence compared to prior methods.

title: Agentic AI-RAN: Enabling Intent-Driven, Explainable and Self-Evolving Open RAN Intelligence
summary: Open RAN (O-RAN) exposes rich control and telemetry interfaces across the Non-RT RIC, Near-RT RIC, and distributed units, but also makes it harder to operate multi-tenant, multi-objective RANs in a safe and auditable manner. In parallel, agentic AI systems with explicit planning, tool use, memory, and self-management offer a natural way to structure long-lived control loops. This article surveys how such agentic controllers can be brought into O-RAN: we review the O-RAN architecture, contrast agentic controllers with conventional ML/RL xApps, and organise the task landscape around three clusters: network slice life-cycle, radio resource management (RRM) closed loops, and cross-cutting security, privacy, and compliance. We then introduce a small set of agentic primitives (Plan-Act-Observe-Reflect, skills as tool use, memory and evidence, and self-management gates) and show, in a multi-cell O-RAN simulation, how they improve slice life-cycle and RRM performance compared to conventional baselines and ablations that remove individual primitives. Security, privacy, and compliance are discussed as architectural constraints and open challenges for standards-aligned deployments. This framework achieves an average 8.83\% reduction in resource usage across three classic network slices.

AI.tldr: Agentic controllers in O-RAN can improve multi-tenant safety and efficiency by structuring long-lived control loops; a small set of primitives (Plan-Act-Observe-Reflect, tool-use, memory, self-management) are proposed and shown to improve slice lifecycle management and RRM in simulations, achieving ~8.83% reduction in resource usage across three network slices; security/privacy/compliance are recognized as architectural constraints and open challenges.
AI.motivation: O-RAN enables rich control/telemetry but complicates safe/auditable operation of multi-tenant, multi-objective networks. Agentic AI can provide structured, long-lived control loops within the O-RAN ecosystem.
AI.method: Survey of O-RAN architecture; contrast agentic controllers with conventional ML/RL xApps; organize tasks into three clusters (slice life-cycle, RRM closed loops, cross-cutting security/privacy/compliance); define agentic primitives (Plan-Act-Observe-Reflect, tool-use, memory/evidence, self-management gates); validate in a multi-cell O-RAN simulation comparing against baselines and ablations.
AI.result: Demonstrates average 8.83% reduction in resource usage across three classic network slices; shows improved performance over baselines and ablations that remove individual primitives.
AI.conclusion: Agentic controllers are a promising direction for O-RAN to enable safe, auditable, and efficient multi-tenant operation, but security/privacy/compliance and standards-aligned deployment remain key open challenges.

title: Thinking with Images as Continuous Actions: Numerical Visual Chain-of-Thought
summary: Recent multimodal large language models (MLLMs) increasingly rely on visual chain-of-thought to perform region-grounded reasoning over images. However, existing approaches ground regions via either textified coordinates-causing modality mismatch and semantic fragmentation or fixed-granularity patches that both limit precise region selection and often require non-trivial architectural changes. In this paper, we propose Numerical Visual Chain-of-Thought (NV-CoT), a framework that enables MLLMs to reason over images using continuous numerical coordinates. NV-CoT expands the MLLM action space from discrete vocabulary tokens to a continuous Euclidean space, allowing models to directly generate bounding-box coordinates as actions with only minimal architectural modification. The framework supports both supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning. In particular, we replace categorical token policies with a Gaussian (or Laplace) policy over coordinates and introduce stochasticity via reparameterized sampling, making NV-CoT fully compatible with GRPO-style policy optimization. Extensive experiments on three benchmarks against eight representative visual reasoning baselines demonstrate that NV-CoT significantly improves localization precision and final answer accuracy, while also accelerating training convergence, validating the effectiveness of continuous-action visual reasoning in MLLMs. The code is available in https://github.com/kesenzhao/NV-CoT.

AI.tldr: NV-CoT enables visual reasoning in multimodal LLMs by using a continuous action space for bounding-box coordinates, improving localization and answer accuracy with minimal architecture changes.
AI.motivation: Grounding regions with textified coordinates causes modality mismatch and fragmentation, and fixed-granularity patches limit precision and require complex changes. A continuous-coordinate approach can improve region selection and integration with LLMs.
AI.method: Extend MLLMs with a continuous action space for coordinates. Replace categorical token policies with Gaussian (or Laplace) coordinates policies and use reparameterized sampling. Compatible with GRPO-style policy optimization. Supports both supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning. Code released for reproducibility.
AI.result: Experiments on three benchmarks against eight baselines show significant gains in localization precision and final answer accuracy, plus faster training convergence, validating the effectiveness of continuous-action visual reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Continuous-action visual reasoning with NV-CoT is effective and practical for region-grounded reasoning in MLLMs, offering precision and training efficiency with minimal architectural changes; code is available for reproduction.

title: Learning with a Budget: Identifying the Best Arm with Resource Constraints
comment: A preliminary version of this work, titled 'Best Arm Identification with Resource Constraints,' was presented at the 27th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics (AISTATS 2024). This manuscript extends the original conference paper by providing improved theoretical results and more generalized conclusions, aiming for future journal submission. arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2402.19090
summary: In many applications, evaluating the effectiveness of different alternatives comes with varying costs or resource usage. Motivated by such heterogeneity, we study the Best Arm Identification with Resource Constraints (BAIwRC) problem, where an agent seeks to identify the best alternative (aka arm) in the presence of resource constraints. Each arm pull consumes one or more types of limited resources. We make two key contributions. First, we propose the Successive Halving with Resource Rationing (SH-RR) algorithm, which integrates resource-aware allocation into the classical successive halving framework on best arm identification. The SH-RR algorithm unifies the theoretical analysis for both the stochastic and deterministic consumption settings, with a new \textit{effective consumption measure

AI.tldr: Proposes SH-RR for Best Arm Identification with Resource Constraints and a unifying theory across consumption models via an effective consumption measure.
AI.motivation: Addresses resource-heterogeneous settings where each arm pull consumes different limited resources; existing BAI methods fail to account for such costs, motivating a resource-aware, theoretically grounded solution.
AI.method: Introduces Successive Halving with Resource Rationing (SH-RR), which embeds resource-aware allocation into the classical successive halving framework. The analysis covers both stochastic and deterministic consumption settings using a new effective consumption measure.
AI.result: Delivers a unified theoretical analysis for both consumption settings, enabled by the effective consumption measure.
