AI.conclusion: SH-RR advances BAI under resource constraints by integrating resource-awareness with a halving scheme and establishing a unified analytical framework; opens avenues for empirical validation and extensions to broader resource models.

title: SpikeTrack: A Spike-driven Framework for Efficient Visual Tracking
summary: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) promise energy-efficient vision, but applying them to RGB visual tracking remains difficult: Existing SNN tracking frameworks either do not fully align with spike-driven computation or do not fully leverage neurons' spatiotemporal dynamics, leading to a trade-off between efficiency and accuracy. To address this, we introduce SpikeTrack, a spike-driven framework for energy-efficient RGB object tracking. SpikeTrack employs a novel asymmetric design that uses asymmetric timestep expansion and unidirectional information flow, harnessing spatiotemporal dynamics while cutting computation. To ensure effective unidirectional information transfer between branches, we design a memory-retrieval module inspired by neural inference mechanisms. This module recurrently queries a compact memory initialized by the template to retrieve target cues and sharpen target perception over time. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SpikeTrack achieves the state-of-the-art among SNN-based trackers and remains competitive with advanced ANN trackers. Notably, it surpasses TransT on LaSOT dataset while consuming only 1/26 of its energy. To our knowledge, SpikeTrack is the first spike-driven framework to make RGB tracking both accurate and energy efficient. The code and models are available at https://github.com/faicaiwawa/SpikeTrack.

AI.tldr: SpikeTrack introduces a spike-driven RGB object tracker with asymmetric timeline expansion and unidirectional information flow, plus a memory-retrieval module that queries a template-initialized memory to maintain target cues, achieving state-of-the-art performance among SNN trackers and competitive energy efficiency (≈1/26 of TransT on LaSOT).
AI.motivation: Address the efficiency-accuracy trade-off in spike-based tracking by leveraging spatiotemporal dynamics in an energy-efficient, spike-driven framework; align computation with spike-driven hardware and memory mechanisms.
AI.method: An asymmetric spike-driven architecture with timestep expansion and unidirectional information flow between branches; a memory-retrieval module that recurrently queries a compact, template-initialized memory to retrieve target cues and sharpen perception over time; end-to-end spike-driven design.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance among SNN-based trackers and competitive with strong ANN trackers; surpasses TransT on LaSOT while consuming only 1/26 of TransT’s energy.
AI.conclusion: SpikeTrack is the first spike-driven RGB tracking framework that delivers both high accuracy and significant energy efficiency; code and models are released.

title: What You Read is What You Classify: Highlighting Attributions to Text and Text-Like Inputs
summary: At present, there are no easily understood explainable artificial intelligence (AI) methods for discrete token inputs, like text. Most explainable AI techniques do not extend well to token sequences, where both local and global features matter, because state-of-the-art models, like transformers, tend to focus on global connections. Therefore, existing explainable AI algorithms fail by (i) identifying disparate tokens of importance, or (ii) assigning a large number of tokens a low value of importance. This method for explainable AI for tokens-based classifiers generalizes a mask-based explainable AI algorithm for images. It starts with an Explainer neural network that is trained to create masks to hide information not relevant for classification. Then, the Hadamard product of the mask and the continuous values of the classifier's embedding layer is taken and passed through the classifier, changing the magnitude of the embedding vector but keeping the orientation unchanged. The Explainer is trained for a taxonomic classifier for nucleotide sequences and it is shown that the masked segments are less relevant to classification than the unmasked ones. This method focused on the importance the token as a whole (i.e., a segment of the input sequence), producing a human-readable explanation.

AI.tldr: Proposes a mask-based XAI method for token-based classifiers by learning masks on an Explainer network, applying Hadamard product to embeddings to reveal token-segment importance, evaluated on a nucleotide taxonomic classifier; aims to produce human-readable explanations and address token-level interpretability where existing methods fail.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of explainable AI methods that are easily understood for discrete token inputs (e.g., text, DNA sequences). Current models (e.g., transformers) emphasize global connections, causing existing XAI tools to misidentify important tokens or spread importance thin across many tokens.
AI.method: Train an Explainer neural network to generate masks highlighting information relevant to classification. Multiply the mask with the classifier's embedding values via Hadamard product (mask modulates magnitude, not orientation) and feed the result back into the classifier. The approach is validated on a taxonomic classifier for nucleotide sequences, showing masked segments are less relevant than unmasked ones, yielding a token-segment level, human-readable explanation.
AI.result: Demonstrates that masking parts of the input embedding affects classification, indicating which token segments drive the decision; provides a readable explanation at the segment (token) level rather than focusing on individual tokens or diffuse global importance.
AI.conclusion: The method generalizes a mask-based XAI approach from images to token-based inputs, offering a human-readable explanation by assessing the relevance of token segments via learned masks and embedding perturbation, potentially improving interpretability for sequence classifiers.

title: Venus: Benchmarking and Empowering Multimodal Large Language Models for Aesthetic Guidance and Cropping
summary: The widespread use of smartphones has made photography ubiquitous, yet a clear gap remains between ordinary users and professional photographers, who can identify aesthetic issues and provide actionable shooting guidance during capture. We define this capability as aesthetic guidance (AG) -- an essential but largely underexplored domain in computational aesthetics. Existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) primarily offer overly positive feedback, failing to identify issues or provide actionable guidance. Without AG capability, they cannot effectively identify distracting regions or optimize compositional balance, thus also struggling in aesthetic cropping, which aims to refine photo composition through reframing after capture. To address this, we introduce AesGuide, the first large-scale AG dataset and benchmark with 10,748 photos annotated with aesthetic scores, analyses, and guidance. Building upon it, we propose Venus, a two-stage framework that first empowers MLLMs with AG capability through progressively complex aesthetic questions and then activates their aesthetic cropping power via CoT-based rationales. Extensive experiments show that Venus substantially improves AG capability and achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in aesthetic cropping, enabling interpretable and interactive aesthetic refinement across both stages of photo creation. Code is available at https://github.com/PKU-ICST-MIPL/Venus_CVPR2026.

AI.tldr: AesGuide is the first large-scale aesthetic guidance (AG) dataset for photos, enabling actionable guidance; Venus is a two-stage framework that equips multimodal LLMs with AG capabilities and then leverages chain-of-thought (CoT) based reasoning for aesthetic cropping, achieving SOTA results and providing interpretable refinement.
AI.motivation: There is a gap between everyday photography and professional aesthetic guidance. Current multimodal LLMs give overly positive feedback and fail to identify issues or provide actionable guidance, hindering tasks like cropping for improved composition.
AI.method: Create AesGuide with 10,748 photos annotated with aesthetic scores, analyses, and guidance. Develop Venus, a two-stage framework: (1) instill AG capability in MLLMs via progressively complex aesthetic questions; (2) enable aesthetic cropping via CoT-based rationales to guide reframing after capture.
AI.result: Venus substantially improves AG capability and achieves state-of-the-art performance in aesthetic cropping. The approach yields interpretable and interactive aesthetic refinement across both stages of photo creation; code is released.
AI.conclusion: This work introduces the first large-scale AG dataset and benchmark and demonstrates a practical, interactive pipeline (AG guidance + cropping) to advance computational aesthetics in photo capture and post-processing.

title: Sandwiching Polynomials for Geometric Concepts with Low Intrinsic Dimension
summary:
Recent work has shown the surprising power of low-degree sandwiching polynomial approximators in the context of challenging learning settings such as learning with distribution shift, testable learning, and learning with contamination. A pair of sandwiching polynomials approximate a target function in expectation while also providing pointwise upper and lower bounds on the function's values. In this paper, we give a new method for constructing low-degree sandwiching polynomials that yield greatly improved degree bounds for several fundamental function classes and marginal distributions. In particular, we obtain degree $\mathrm{poly}(k)$ sandwiching polynomials for functions of $k$ halfspaces under the Gaussian distribution, improving exponentially over the prior $2^{O(k)}$ bound. More broadly, our approach applies to function classes that are low-dimensional and have smooth boundary.
  In contrast to prior work, our proof is relatively simple and directly uses the smoothness of the target function's boundary to construct sandwiching Lipschitz functions, which are amenable to results from high-dimensional approximation theory. For low-dimensional polynomial threshold functions (PTFs) with respect to Gaussians, we obtain doubly exponential improvements without applying the FT-mollification method of Kane used in the best previous result.

AI.tldr: New method for constructing low-degree sandwiching polynomials yields poly(k)-degree for k halfspaces under Gaussian, improving exponentially over the prior 2^{O(k)} bound; extends to low-dimensional smooth-boundary function classes; uses smooth boundary to build Lipschitz sandwiching functions, enabling high-dimensional approximation results; for low-dimensional polynomial threshold functions under Gaussian, achieves doubly exponential improvements without FT-mollification.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of prior high-degree bounds in sandwiching polynomial approximators for challenging learning settings (distribution shift, contamination, etc.). Seek tighter degree bounds to enable efficient learning under Gaussian and related distributions.
AI.method: Constructs sandwiching Lipschitz functions by leveraging the smooth boundary of target functions; applies high-dimensional approximation theory; avoids FT-mollification; achieves poly(k) degrees for k halfspaces under Gaussian; obtains improvements for low-dimensional PTFs.
AI.result: Poly(k)-degree sandwiching polynomials for functions of k halfspaces under Gaussian; exponential improvement over previous 2^{O(k)} bounds; broader applicability to low-dimensional smooth-boundary function classes; doubly exponential improvements for low-dim PTFs under Gaussian without FT-mollification.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach simplifies the construction of sandwiching polynomials and yields substantially improved degree bounds, enhancing the applicability of sandwiching techniques in learning with distribution shift, contamination, and related settings.

title: Accelerating Masked Image Generation by Learning Latent Controlled Dynamics
summary: Masked Image Generation Models (MIGMs) have achieved great success, yet their efficiency is hampered by the multiple steps of bi-directional attention. In fact, there exists notable redundancy in their computation: when sampling discrete tokens, the rich semantics contained in the continuous features are lost. Some existing works attempt to cache the features to approximate future features. However, they exhibit considerable approximation error under aggressive acceleration rates. We attribute this to their limited expressivity and the failure to account for sampling information. To fill this gap, we propose to learn a lightweight model that incorporates both previous features and sampled tokens, and regresses the average velocity field of feature evolution. The model has moderate complexity that suffices to capture the subtle dynamics while keeping lightweight compared to the original base model. We apply our method, MIGM-Shortcut, to two representative MIGM architectures and tasks. In particular, on the state-of-the-art Lumina-DiMOO, it achieves over 4x acceleration of text-to-image generation while maintaining quality, significantly pushing the Pareto frontier of masked image generation. The code and model weights are available at https://github.com/Kaiwen-Zhu/MIGM-Shortcut.

AI.tldr: Introduces MIGM-Shortcut, a lightweight velocity-field predictor that leverages past features and sampled tokens to accelerate masked image generation; reports ~4x speedup on Lumina-DiMOO with preserved quality.
AI.motivation: Redundancy in MIGMs arises from bi-directional attention during discrete-token sampling, causing loss of rich semantics when caching features. Existing feature-caching methods exhibit large approximation errors under aggressive acceleration due to limited expressivity and ignoring sampling information.
AI.method: Train a lightweight model that regresses the average velocity field of feature evolution by fusing previous features and sampled tokens; moderate complexity to capture dynamics while staying lightweight; applied to two MIGM architectures; evaluation on text-to-image tasks, notably Lumina-DiMOO; code and weights released.
AI.result: Achieves over 4× acceleration of text-to-image generation on Lumina-DiMOO while maintaining quality; demonstrates improved Pareto frontier for MIGMs; validated across two architectures.
AI.conclusion: A small, expressive regressor suffices to model feature evolution dynamics in MIGMs, enabling substantial speedups with minimal quality loss and broad applicability across MIGM architectures.

title: Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning for Large-Scale Tote Allocation in Human-Robot Collaborative Fulfillment Centers
summary: Optimizing the consolidation process in container-based fulfillment centers requires trading off competing objectives such as processing speed, resource usage, and space utilization while adhering to a range of real-world operational constraints. This process involves moving items between containers via a combination of human and robotic workstations to free up space for inbound inventory and increase container utilization. We formulate this problem as a large-scale Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning (MORL) task with high-dimensional state spaces and dynamic system behavior. Our method builds on recent theoretical advances in solving constrained RL problems via best-response and no-regret dynamics in zero-sum games, enabling principled minimax policy learning. Policy evaluation on realistic warehouse simulations shows that our approach effectively trades off objectives, and we empirically observe that it learns a single policy that simultaneously satisfies all constraints, even if this is not theoretically guaranteed. We further introduce a theoretical framework to handle the problem of error cancellation, where time-averaged solutions display oscillatory behavior. This method returns a single iterate whose Lagrangian value is close to the minimax value of the game. These results demonstrate the promise of MORL in solving complex, high-impact decision-making problems in large-scale industrial systems.

AI.tldr: MORL formulation for container consolidation using best-response/no-regret dynamics; demonstrates a single policy achieving all constraints in simulations with an error cancellation framework to mitigate oscillations.
AI.motivation: Optimize competing objectives (speed, resource usage, space) under real-world constraints in large-scale container fulfillment; traditional methods struggle with high-dimensional MORL.
AI.method: Constrained MORL via zero-sum games; minimax policy learning; best-response and no-regret dynamics; introduces error cancellation framework to select a single iterate with near-minimax Lagrangian.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation in warehouse simulations shows the learned policy trades off objectives and can satisfy all constraints; policy shows oscillatory behavior addressed by framework.
AI.conclusion: MORL shows promise for complex industrial decision problems; potential to generalize; theoretical and empirical contributions; further work on guarantees and real-world deployment.

title: Ordinal Diffusion Models for Color Fundus Images
summary: It has been suggested that generative image models such as diffusion models can improve performance on clinically relevant tasks by offering deep learning models supplementary training data. However, most conditional diffusion models treat disease stages as independent classes, ignoring the continuous nature of disease progression. This mismatch is problematic in medical imaging because continuous pathological processes are typically only observed through coarse, discrete but ordered labels as in ophthalmology for diabetic retinopathy (DR). We propose an ordinal latent diffusion model for generating color fundus images that explicitly incorporates the ordered structure of DR severity into the generation process. Instead of categorical conditioning, we used a scalar disease representation, enabling a smooth transition between adjacent stages. We evaluated our approach using visual realism metrics and classification-based clinical consistency analysis on the EyePACS dataset. Compared to a standard conditional diffusion model, our model reduced the Fréchet inception distance for four of the five DR stages and increased the quadratic weighted $κ$ from 0.79 to 0.87. Furthermore, interpolation experiments showed that the model captured a continuous spectrum of disease progression learned from ordered, coarse class labels.

AI.tldr: An ordinal latent diffusion model for DR severity in color fundus images uses a scalar, ordered disease representation instead of categorical conditioning, enabling smooth progression between stages. It outperforms standard conditional diffusion in most DR stages (lower FID on 4/5 stages) and improves clinical agreement (quadratic weighted kappa 0.87 from 0.79) with results suggesting a continuous progression spectrum learned from coarse labels.
AI.motivation: Medical diffusion models often treat disease stages as independent classes, ignoring the natural ordinal progression of diseases like diabetic retinopathy (DR). This mismatch can limit the realism and clinical relevance of generated images and their utility for downstream tasks.
AI.method: Develop an ordinal latent diffusion model that conditions generation on a scalar (ordered) representation of DR severity rather than discrete categories. Train and evaluate on EyePACS color fundus images, comparing to a standard conditional diffusion model. Assess using visual realism metrics and a classification-based clinical consistency analysis, including interpolation experiments to demonstrate learned continuity of disease progression.
AI.result: The proposed model reduced Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) for four of the five DR stages and increased quadratic weighted kappa κ from 0.79 to 0.87. Interpolation experiments showed the model captured a continuous spectrum of disease progression.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating ordinal structure into diffusion-based generation yields more clinically consistent and visually realistic fundus images, enabling a smooth, continuous representation of DR progression and improving downstream evaluation metrics.

title: Flow-Based Density Ratio Estimation for Intractable Distributions with Applications in Genomics
summary: Estimating density ratios between pairs of intractable data distributions is a core problem in probabilistic modeling, enabling principled comparisons of sample likelihoods under different data-generating processes across conditions and covariates. While exact-likelihood models such as normalizing flows offer a promising approach to density ratio estimation, naive flow-based evaluations are computationally expensive, as they require simulating costly likelihood integrals for each distribution separately. In this work, we leverage condition-aware flow matching to derive a single dynamical formulation for tracking density ratios along generative trajectories. We demonstrate competitive performance on simulated benchmarks for closed-form ratio estimation, and show that our method supports versatile tasks in single-cell genomics data analysis, where likelihood-based comparisons of cellular states across experimental conditions enable treatment effect estimation and batch correction evaluation.

AI.tldr: A method to estimate density ratios between intractable data distributions efficiently by using condition-aware flow matching to track density ratios along generative trajectories with a single dynamical formulation; validated on synthetic benchmarks and applicable to single-cell genomics for treatment effect estimation and batch correction.
AI.motivation: Density ratios between complex, intractable distributions are essential for comparing sample likelihoods across conditions, but exact likelihood estimation via normalizing flows is costly because it requires separate likelihood computations for each distribution. A unified, trajectory-based approach could reduce computational burden while enabling robust cross-condition comparisons.
AI.method: Introduce condition-aware flow matching to derive a single dynamical system that propagates density ratios along generative trajectories. This avoids separate likelihood integration per distribution and leverages a flow-based generative model to track the ratio continuously as data evolve under conditioning. The approach is evaluated on simulated benchmarks for closed-form ratio estimation.
AI.result: The method achieves competitive performance with closed-form baselines on synthetic density-ratio estimation tasks. In addition, it demonstrates applicability to single-cell genomics analyses by facilitating likelihood-based comparisons of cellular states across conditions, aiding treatment-effect estimation and batch-correction evaluation.
AI.conclusion: A unified, trajectory-aware density-ratio estimation framework based on condition-aware flow matching can efficiently and accurately compare intractable distributions across conditions, with practical utility in single-cell genomics and potentially broader probabilistic modeling tasks.

title: Interpretable Debiasing of Vision-Language Models for Social Fairness
comment: 25 pages, 30 figures, 13 Tables Accepted to CVPR 2026
summary: The rapid advancement of Vision-Language models (VLMs) has raised growing concerns that their black-box reasoning processes could lead to unintended forms of social bias. Current debiasing approaches focus on mitigating surface-level bias signals through post-hoc learning or test-time algorithms, while leaving the internal dynamics of the model largely unexplored. In this work, we introduce an interpretable, model-agnostic bias mitigation framework, DeBiasLens, that localizes social attribute neurons in VLMs through sparse autoencoders (SAEs) applied to multimodal encoders. Building upon the disentanglement ability of SAEs, we train them on facial image or caption datasets without corresponding social attribute labels to uncover neurons highly responsive to specific demographics, including those that are underrepresented. By selectively deactivating the social neurons most strongly tied to bias for each group, we effectively mitigate socially biased behaviors of VLMs without degrading their semantic knowledge. Our research lays the groundwork for future auditing tools, prioritizing social fairness in emerging real-world AI systems.

AI.tldr: A framework (DeBiasLens) audits and mitigates social bias in Vision-Language Models by unsupervisedly identifying 'social attribute' neurons via sparse autoencoders and selectively deactivating those neurons linked to bias, preserving semantic knowledge and enabling auditing.
AI.motivation: VLMs exhibit black-box social biases; need interpretable, modular debiasing that preserves performance and enables auditing.
AI.method: Train sparse autoencoders on multimodal encoders using unlabeled facial images or captions to disentangle and identify neurons highly responsive to demographic attributes. Deactivate top-bias neurons per group to reduce biased outputs. The approach is model-agnostic and post-hoc.
AI.result: Effective mitigation of socially biased behaviors without degrading semantic knowledge, with potential to serve as a foundation for auditing tools.
AI.conclusion: DeBiasLens advances interpretable, targeted bias mitigation in VLMs and lays groundwork for fairness auditing in real-world AI systems.

title: The Stability of Online Algorithms in Performative Prediction
summary: The use of algorithmic predictions in decision-making leads to a feedback loop where the models we deploy actively influence the data distributions we see, and later use to retrain on. This dynamic was formalized by Perdomo et al. 2020 in their work on performative prediction. Our main result is an unconditional reduction showing that any no-regret algorithm deployed in performative settings converges to a (mixed) performatively stable equilibrium: a solution in which models actively shape data distributions in ways that their own predictions look optimal in hindsight. Prior to our work, all positive results in this area made strong restrictions on how models influenced distributions. By using a martingale argument and allowing randomization, we avoid any such assumption and sidestep recent hardness results for finding stable models. Lastly, on a more conceptual note, our connection sheds light on why common algorithms, like gradient descent, are naturally stabilizing and prevent runaway feedback loops. We hope our work enables future technical transfer of ideas between online optimization and performativity.

AI.tldr: Any no-regret algorithm deployed in performative settings converges to a (mixed) performatively stable equilibrium, via a martingale-based unconditional argument with randomization; explains why gradient-based updates can be stabilizing and enables transfer between online optimization and performativity.
AI.motivation: Address the feedback loop where deployed predictive models actively shape the data distributions they are trained on; prior positive results required strong, restrictive assumptions on distribution shifts; seek unconditional convergence guarantees.
AI.method: Unconditional reduction using martingale arguments and randomization within the performative prediction framework (Perdomo et al. 2020); avoids restrictions on how models influence distributions and sidesteps recent hardness results.
AI.result: Proves convergence of any no-regret algorithm to a (mixed) performatively stable equilibrium in performative settings; provides a conceptual explanation for the stabilizing nature of gradient descent.
AI.conclusion: Links online optimization with performativity, enabling technical transfer, and suggesting that common learning dynamics naturally resist runaway feedback loops; opens avenues for broader results and rates under relaxed assumptions.

title: SR3R: Rethinking Super-Resolution 3D Reconstruction With Feed-Forward Gaussian Splatting
summary: 3D super-resolution (3DSR) aims to reconstruct high-resolution (HR) 3D scenes from low-resolution (LR) multi-view images. Existing methods rely on dense LR inputs and per-scene optimization, which restricts the high-frequency priors for constructing HR 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) to those inherited from pretrained 2D super-resolution (2DSR) models. This severely limits reconstruction fidelity, cross-scene generalization, and real-time usability. We propose to reformulate 3DSR as a direct feed-forward mapping from sparse LR views to HR 3DGS representations, enabling the model to autonomously learn 3D-specific high-frequency geometry and appearance from large-scale, multi-scene data. This fundamentally changes how 3DSR acquires high-frequency knowledge and enables robust generalization to unseen scenes. Specifically, we introduce SR3R, a feed-forward framework that directly predicts HR 3DGS representations from sparse LR views via the learned mapping network. To further enhance reconstruction fidelity, we introduce Gaussian offset learning and feature refinement, which stabilize reconstruction and sharpen high-frequency details. SR3R is plug-and-play and can be paired with any feed-forward 3DGS reconstruction backbone: the backbone provides an LR 3DGS scaffold, and SR3R upscales it to an HR 3DGS. Extensive experiments across three 3D benchmarks demonstrate that SR3R surpasses state-of-the-art (SOTA) 3DSR methods and achieves strong zero-shot generalization, even outperforming SOTA per-scene optimization methods on unseen scenes.

AI.tldr: A feed-forward SR approach (SR3R) maps sparse LR views to high-resolution 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) representations, enabling 3DSR without per-scene optimization and improving cross-scene generalization and fidelity; it is plug-and-play with any 3DGS backbone and includes Gaussian offset learning and feature refinement.
AI.motivation: Current 3D super-resolution (3DSR) methods rely on dense LR inputs and per-scene optimization, which limits high-frequency priors and generalization. A data-driven, feed-forward framework could learn 3D-specific high-frequency geometry and appearance from diverse scenes to improve fidelity and zero-shot generalization.
AI.method: Introduce SR3R, a feed-forward mapping network that directly predicts HR 3DGS representations from sparse LR views. Enhance with Gaussian offset learning and feature refinement to stabilize and sharpen high-frequency details. Plug-and-play: pairs with any feed-forward 3DGS backbone (LR 3DGS scaffold + SR3R upscaling) for HR 3DGS reconstruction.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across three 3D benchmarks show SR3R surpasses state-of-the-art (SOTA) 3DSR methods and exhibits strong zero-shot generalization, even outperforming SOTA per-scene optimization methods on unseen scenes.
AI.conclusion: SR3R enables robust, real-time-capable 3DSR by learning 3D-specific priors from multi-scene data, providing a versatile and generalizable alternative to per-scene optimization with practical plug-and-play integration.

title: An Efficient Unsupervised Federated Learning Approach for Anomaly Detection in Heterogeneous IoT Networks
summary: Federated learning (FL) is an effective paradigm for distributed environments such as the Internet of Things (IoT), where data from diverse devices with varying functionalities remains localized while contributing to a shared global model. By eliminating the need to transmit raw data, FL inherently preserves privacy. However, the heterogeneous nature of IoT data, stemming from differences in device capabilities, data formats, and communication constraints, poses significant challenges to maintaining both global model performance and privacy. In the context of IoT-based anomaly detection, unsupervised FL offers a promising means to identify abnormal behavior without centralized data aggregation. Nevertheless, feature heterogeneity across devices complicates model training and optimization, hindering effective implementation. In this study we propose an efficient unsupervised FL framework that enhances anomaly detection by leveraging shared features from two distinct IoT datasets: one focused on anomaly detection and the other on device identification, while preserving dataset-specific features. To improve transparency and interpretability, we employ explainable AI techniques, such as SHAP, to identify key features influencing local model decisions. Experiments conducted on real-world IoT datasets demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms conventional FL approaches in anomaly detection accuracy. This work underscores the potential of using shared features from complementary datasets to optimize unsupervised federated learning and achieve superior anomaly detection results in decentralized IoT environments.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised federated learning framework for IoT anomaly detection using shared features from two complementary datasets, with SHAP-based interpretability, achieving higher anomaly detection accuracy than baselines.
AI.motivation: IoT data heterogeneity and privacy concerns hinder global model performance; unsupervised FL can learn from distributed data without sharing raw data; leveraging complementary datasets aims to improve anomaly detection while preserving dataset-specific features.
AI.method: Proposes an efficient unsupervised FL framework that shares features across two IoT datasets (anomaly detection and device identification) while preserving dataset-specific features; uses SHAP to identify influential local features for transparency.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on real-world IoT datasets shows significant improvement in anomaly detection accuracy over conventional FL approaches, demonstrating benefits of cross-dataset shared features and interpretability.
AI.conclusion: Shared features from complementary datasets can enhance unsupervised FL in decentralized IoT, with SHAP providing interpretable explanations; suggests broader applicability and future research into privacy-preserving cross-dataset feature sharing.

title: Steering and Rectifying Latent Representation Manifolds in Frozen Multi-modal LLMs for Video Anomaly Detection
summary: Video anomaly detection (VAD) aims to identify abnormal events in videos. Traditional VAD methods generally suffer from the high costs of labeled data and full training, thus some recent works have explored leveraging frozen multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) in a tuning-free manner to perform VAD. However, their performance is limited as they directly inherit pre-training biases and cannot adapt internal representations to specific video contexts, leading to difficulties in handling subtle or ambiguous anomalies. To address these limitations, we propose a novel intervention framework, termed SteerVAD, which advances MLLM-based VAD by shifting from passively reading to actively steering and rectifying internal representations. Our approach first leverages the gradient-free representational separability analysis (RSA) to identify top attention heads as latent anomaly experts (LAEs) which are most discriminative for VAD. Then a hierarchical meta-controller (HMC) generates dynamic rectification signals by jointly conditioning on global context and these LAE outputs. The signals execute targeted, anisotropic scaling directly upon the LAE representation manifolds, amplifying anomaly-relevant dimensions while suppressing inherent biases. Extensive experiments on mainstream benchmarks demonstrate our method achieves state-of-the-art performance among tuning-free approaches requiring only 1% of training data, establishing it as a powerful new direction for video anomaly detection. The code will be released upon the publication.

AI.tldr: SteerVAD introduces a tuning-free VAD framework that actively steers frozen MLLMs using gradient-free RSA to identify latent anomaly experts and a hierarchical meta-controller to apply anisotropic rectification, achieving state-of-the-art results with minimal labeled data.
AI.motivation: Overcome the limitations of existing tuning-free MLLM-based VAD that inherit pre-training biases and lack adaptation to specific video contexts, hindering detection of subtle/ambiguous anomalies.
AI.method: Identify top attention heads most discriminative for VAD via gradient-free representational separability analysis (RSA) to form latent anomaly experts (LAEs). A hierarchical meta-controller (HMC) generates dynamic rectification signals conditioned on global context and LAE outputs, applying anisotropic scaling to LAE representations to amplify anomaly-relevant dimensions and suppress biases.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on mainstream benchmarks shows state-of-the-art performance among tuning-free approaches, achieving this with only ~1% of training data.
AI.conclusion: SteerVAD presents a promising paradigm for video anomaly detection by actively steering internal MLLM representations; code release promised, validating the practical viability of targeted representation rectification.

title: Comparing Classical and Quantum Variational Classifiers on the XOR Problem
comment: 32 pages, 17 figures. Code and experiment scripts available at https://github.com/mseilkhan/XOR-research-Quantum-ML-vs-Classic
summary:
Quantum machine learning applies principles such as superposition and entanglement to data processing and optimization. Variational quantum models operate on qubits in high-dimensional Hilbert spaces and provide an alternative approach to model expressivity. We compare classical models and a variational quantum classifier on the XOR problem. Logistic regression, a one-hidden-layer multilayer perceptron, and a two-qubit variational quantum classifier with circuit depths 1 and 2 are evaluated on synthetic XOR datasets with varying Gaussian noise and sample sizes using accuracy and binary cross-entropy.
  Performance is determined primarily by model expressivity. Logistic regression and the depth-1 quantum circuit fail to represent XOR reliably, whereas the multilayer perceptron and the depth-2 quantum circuit achieve perfect test accuracy under representative conditions. Robustness analyses across noise levels, dataset sizes, and random seeds confirm that circuit depth is decisive for quantum performance on this task. Despite matching accuracy, the multilayer perceptron achieves lower binary cross-entropy and substantially shorter training time. Hardware execution preserves the global XOR structure but introduces structured deviations in the decision function. Overall, deeper variational quantum classifiers can match classical neural networks in accuracy on low-dimensional XOR benchmarks, but no clear empirical advantage in robustness or efficiency is observed in the examined settings.

AI.tldr: Deeper variational quantum classifiers (depth-2) can match classical neural networks on a simple XOR task in accuracy, but show no robust or efficiency advantage over classical models in the studied settings.
AI.motivation: Assess expressivity and robustness of variational quantum classifiers versus classical models on a minimal nonlinear problem (XOR) across noise, dataset size, and seeds.
AI.method: Compare logistic regression, one-hidden-layer MLP, and two-qubit variational quantum classifiers with circuit depths 1 and 2 on synthetic XOR datasets with varying Gaussian noise and sample sizes; evaluate accuracy and binary cross-entropy; include hardware experiments analyzing performance and training time.
AI.result: Depth-2 quantum circuit achieves perfect test accuracy under representative conditions; depth-1 fails; MLP also achieves perfect accuracy and trains faster with lower cross-entropy; robustness analysis indicates circuit depth is the decisive factor; hardware execution preserves overall XOR structure but introduces structured decision-function deviations.
AI.conclusion: Deeper VQCs can match classical neural networks on this low-dimensional benchmark, but do not yet offer advantages in robustness or efficiency; further work needed on larger, more complex tasks and hardware-aware strategies to unlock potential quantum gains.

title: GuardAlign: Test-time Safety Alignment in Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) have achieved remarkable progress in vision-language reasoning tasks, yet ensuring their safety remains a critical challenge. Recent input-side defenses detect unsafe images with CLIP and prepend safety prefixes to prompts, but they still suffer from inaccurate detection in complex scenes and unstable safety signals during decoding. To address these issues, we propose GuardAlign, a training-free defense framework that integrates two strategies. First, OT-enhanced safety detection leverages optimal transport to measure distribution distances between image patches and unsafe semantics, enabling accurate identification of malicious regions without additional computational cost. Second, cross-modal attentive calibration strengthens the influence of safety prefixes by adaptively reallocating attention across layers, ensuring that safety signals remain consistently activated throughout generation. Extensive evaluations on six representative MLLMs demonstrate that GuardAlign reduces unsafe response rates by up to 39% on SPA-VL, while preserving utility, achieving an improvement on VQAv2 from 78.51% to 79.21%.

AI.tldr: GuardAlign is a training-free defense for LVLMs that combines OT-enhanced unsafe-region detection with cross-modal attentive calibration to keep safety prompts active during generation, reducing unsafe outputs while maintaining utility.
AI.motivation: Vision-language models face safety challenges; existing input-side defenses (e.g., CLIP-based detection with safety prefixes) suffer from inaccurate detection in complex scenes and unstable safety signals during decoding.
AI.method: 1) OT-enhanced safety detection uses optimal transport to measure distribution distances between image patches and unsafe semantics to accurately identify malicious regions without extra computation. 2) Cross-modal attentive calibration adaptively reallocates attention across layers to strengthen the influence of safety prefixes, keeping safety signals engaged throughout generation.
AI.result: Evaluations on six representative MLLMs show GuardAlign reduces unsafe response rates by up to 39% on SPA-VL and preserves utility, with VQAv2 accuracy improving from 78.51% to 79.21%.
AI.conclusion: GuardAlign is an effective training-free defense for LVLM safety, improving safety without sacrificing performance and generalizing across multiple models.

title: Adaptive Combinatorial Experimental Design: Pareto Optimality for Decision-Making and Inference
summary: In this paper, we provide the first investigation into adaptive combinatorial experimental design, focusing on the trade-off between regret minimization and statistical power in combinatorial multi-armed bandits (CMAB). While minimizing regret requires repeated exploitation of high-reward arms, accurate inference on reward gaps requires sufficient exploration of suboptimal actions. We formalize this trade-off through the concept of Pareto optimality and establish equivalent conditions for Pareto-efficient learning in CMAB. We consider two relevant cases under different information structures, i.e., full-bandit feedback and semi-bandit feedback, and propose two algorithms MixCombKL and MixCombUCB respectively for these two cases. We provide theoretical guarantees showing that both algorithms are Pareto optimal, achieving finite-time guarantees on both regret and estimation error of arm gaps. Our results further reveal that richer feedback significantly tightens the attainable Pareto frontier, with the primary gains arising from improved estimation accuracy under our proposed methods. Taken together, these findings establish a principled framework for adaptive combinatorial experimentation in multi-objective decision-making.

AI.tldr: Proposes a Pareto-optimal framework for adaptive combinatorial experimentation in CMAB, balancing regret minimization and estimation power. Introduces MixCombKL (full-bandit) and MixCombUCB (semi-bandit), with finite-time guarantees showing Pareto-optimality and that richer feedback tightens the Pareto frontier.
AI.motivation: To resolve the inherent trade-off between exploiting high-reward combinatorial actions (low regret) and exploring suboptimal actions to accurately infer reward gaps, enabling principled multi-objective learning in CMAB.
AI.method: Formalize Pareto-efficient learning in CMAB under full-bandit and semi-bandit feedback. Develop two algorithms, MixCombKL and MixCombUCB, tailored to each information structure, and derive finite-time guarantees on both regret and arm-gap estimation error.
AI.result: Both algorithms are Pareto-optimal, achieving finite-time bounds on regret and estimation error. Richer feedback tightens the Pareto frontier, with substantial gains from improved gap estimation.
AI.conclusion: Provides a principled framework for adaptive combinatorial experimentation in multi-objective decision-making, clarifying the trade-off between regret and statistical power and showing how feedback richness enhances performance.

title: Look Carefully: Adaptive Visual Reinforcements in Multimodal Large Language Models for Hallucination Mitigation
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable progress in vision-language reasoning, yet they remain vulnerable to hallucination, where generated content deviates from visual evidence. Existing mitigation strategies either require costly supervision during training or introduce additional latency at inference time. Recent vision enhancement methods attempt to address this issue by reinforcing visual tokens during decoding, but they typically inject all tokens indiscriminately, which causes interference from background regions and distracts the model from critical cues. To overcome this challenge, we propose Adaptive Visual Reinforcement (AIR), a training-free framework for MLLMs. AIR consists of two components. Prototype-based token reduction condenses the large pool of visual tokens into a compact subset to suppress redundancy. OT-guided patch reinforcement quantifies the alignment between hidden states and patch embeddings to selectively integrate the most consistent patches into feed-forward layers. As a result, AIR enhances the model's reliance on salient visual information and effectively mitigates hallucination. Extensive experiments across representative MLLMs demonstrate that AIR substantially reduces hallucination while preserving general capabilities, establishing it as an effective solution for building reliable MLLMs.

AI.tldr: AIR is a training-free framework for multimodal LLMs that reduces hallucination by selectively reinforcing visual information: prototype-based token reduction to remove redundancy and OT-guided patch reinforcement to inject the most consistent patches into feed-forward layers, focusing on salient cues.
AI.motivation: Hallucination in vision-language reasoning remains problematic in MLLMs. Existing remedies either require costly supervised training or incur inference latency, and indiscriminate injection of visual tokens can introduce distracting background information.
AI.method: Two components: (1) Prototype-based token reduction to condense a large pool of visual tokens into a compact subset, reducing redundancy. (2) OT-guided patch reinforcement that uses optimal transport to quantify alignment between hidden states and patch embeddings, selectively integrating the most consistent patches into the feed-forward layers.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on representative MLLMs show AIR substantially reduces hallucination while preserving general capabilities, achieved in a training-free manner.
AI.conclusion: AIR offers a practical, training-free solution to mitigate hallucination in MLLMs by focusing reinforcement on salient visual information, enhancing reliability without sacrificing core abilities.

title: Time Series Foundation Models as Strong Baselines in Transportation Forecasting: A Large-Scale Benchmark Analysis
summary: Accurate forecasting of transportation dynamics is essential for urban mobility and infrastructure planning. Although recent work has achieved strong performance with deep learning models, these methods typically require dataset-specific training, architecture design and hyper-parameter tuning. This paper evaluates whether general-purpose time-series foundation models can serve as forecasters for transportation tasks by benchmarking the zero-shot performance of the state-of-the-art model, Chronos-2, across ten real-world datasets covering highway traffic volume and flow, urban traffic speed, bike-sharing demand, and electric vehicle charging station data. Under a consistent evaluation protocol, we find that, even without any task-specific fine-tuning, Chronos-2 delivers state-of-the-art or competitive accuracy across most datasets, frequently outperforming classical statistical baselines and specialized deep learning architectures, particularly at longer horizons. Beyond point forecasting, we evaluate its native probabilistic outputs using prediction-interval coverage and sharpness, demonstrating that Chronos-2 also provides useful uncertainty quantification without dataset-specific training. In general, this study supports the adoption of time-series foundation models as a key baseline for transportation forecasting research.

AI.tldr: Zero-shot evaluation of Chronos-2 on ten real-world transportation datasets shows state-of-the-art or competitive forecasting performance without task-specific fine-tuning, with strong uncertainty quantification, supporting time-series foundation models as robust baselines for transportation forecasting.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on dataset-specific training and architecture tuning in transportation forecasting by assessing whether general-purpose time-series foundation models can serve as robust, zero-shot forecasters.
AI.method: Benchmark Chronos-2 in a zero-shot setting across ten real-world datasets encompassing highway traffic volume and flow, urban traffic speed, bike-sharing demand, and electric vehicle charging station data. Use a consistent evaluation protocol, compare to classical statistical baselines and specialized DL architectures, assess point forecasts and probabilistic outputs (prediction intervals, sharpness).
AI.result: Chronos-2 achieves state-of-the-art or competitive accuracy on most datasets, particularly at longer horizons, often surpassing traditional baselines and domain-specific models. It also provides meaningful uncertainty quantification via its native probabilistic outputs without dataset-specific training.
AI.conclusion: Time-series foundation models can serve as strong, general-purpose baselines for transportation forecasting, motivating broader adoption and evaluation of foundation-model-based forecasting in the field.

title: Spatio-Temporal Garment Reconstruction Using Diffusion Mapping via Pattern Coordinates
comment: arXiv admin note: text overlap with arXiv:2504.08353
summary: Reconstructing 3D clothed humans from monocular images and videos is a fundamental problem with applications in virtual try-on, avatar creation, and mixed reality. Despite significant progress in human body recovery, accurately reconstructing garment geometry, particularly for loose-fitting clothing, remains an open challenge. We propose a unified framework for high-fidelity 3D garment reconstruction from both single images and video sequences. Our approach combines Implicit Sewing Patterns (ISP) with a generative diffusion model to learn expressive garment shape priors in 2D UV space. Leveraging these priors, we introduce a mapping model that establishes correspondences between image pixels, UV pattern coordinates, and 3D geometry, enabling accurate and detailed garment reconstruction from single images. We further extend this formulation to dynamic reconstruction by introducing a spatio-temporal diffusion scheme with test-time guidance to enforce long-range temporal consistency. We also develop analytic projection-based constraints that preserve image-aligned geometry in visible regions while enforcing coherent completion in occluded areas over time. Although trained exclusively on synthetically simulated cloth data, our method generalizes well to real-world imagery and consistently outperforms existing approaches on both tight- and loose-fitting garments. The reconstructed garments preserve fine geometric detail while exhibiting realistic dynamic motion, supporting downstream applications such as texture editing, garment retargeting, and animation.

AI.tldr: A unified framework for 3D garment reconstruction from monocular images/videos using Implicit Sewing Patterns and diffusion priors in UV space, enabling single-image and temporally-cohesive dynamic reconstruction with synthetic training and real-world generalization.
AI.motivation: Accurate reconstruction of garment geometry, especially loose-fitting clothing, from single images or video remains challenging due to occlusions, non-rigid deformations, and the need for temporal consistency in videos.
AI.method: Integrates Implicit Sewing Patterns (ISP) with a generative diffusion model to learn expressive garment shape priors in 2D UV space. Introduces a mapping model that links image pixels, UV pattern coordinates, and 3D geometry. Extends to dynamic reconstruction via a spatio-temporal diffusion scheme with test-time guidance, and analytic projection-based constraints to preserve visible geometry while coherently completing occluded regions over time.
AI.result: Outperforms existing approaches for both tight and loose garments, preserving fine geometric details and enabling realistic dynamic motion. Demonstrates strong generalization from synthetic training data to real-world imagery and supports downstream tasks like texture editing, garment retargeting, and animation.
AI.conclusion: The framework delivers high-fidelity, temporally coherent 3D garment reconstruction from both single images and videos, bridging static and dynamic garment modeling and showing robust real-world generalization.

title: Chunk-wise Attention Transducers for Fast and Accurate Streaming Speech-to-Text
summary: We propose Chunk-wise Attention Transducer (CHAT), a novel extension to RNN-T models that processes audio in fixed-size chunks while employing cross-attention within each chunk. This hybrid approach maintains RNN-T's streaming capability while introducing controlled flexibility for local alignment modeling. CHAT significantly reduces the temporal dimension that RNN-T must handle, yielding substantial efficiency improvements: up to 46.2% reduction in peak training memory, up to 1.36X faster training, and up to 1.69X faster inference. Alongside these efficiency gains, CHAT achieves consistent accuracy improvements over RNN-T across multiple languages and tasks -- up to 6.3% relative WER reduction for speech recognition and up to 18.0% BLEU improvement for speech translation. The method proves particularly effective for speech translation, where RNN-T's strict monotonic alignment hurts performance. Our results demonstrate that the CHAT model offers a practical solution for deploying more capable streaming speech models without sacrificing real-time constraints.

AI.tldr: Chunk-wise Attention Transducer (CHAT) extends RNN-T with per-chunk cross-attention, preserving streaming while enhancing local alignment, yielding memory/speed gains and accuracy improvements.
AI.motivation: RNN-T's strict monotonic alignment limits performance and efficiency; need for flexible local alignment within streaming constraints to improve ASR and speech translation, especially translation where monotonicity hurts.
AI.method: Process audio in fixed-size chunks; apply cross-attention within each chunk; hybridize with RNN-T to maintain streaming; reduces temporal dimension for RNN-T; enables efficient training/inference.
AI.result: Up to 46.2% peak training memory reduction; up to 1.36x faster training; up to 1.69x faster inference; up to 6.3% relative WER reduction for ASR; up to 18.0% BLEU gain for speech translation; particularly beneficial for translation.
AI.conclusion: CHAT offers a practical, efficient way to deploy more capable streaming speech models without violating real-time constraints; improves performance across languages/tasks.

title: Quant Experts: Token-aware Adaptive Error Reconstruction with Mixture of Experts for Large Vision-Language Models Quantization
comment: 13 pages, 6 figures, including appendix, Accepted at CVPR 2026
summary: Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) has emerged as an effective technique for alleviating the substantial computational and memory overheads of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) by compressing both weights and activations without retraining the full model. Existing PTQ methods primarily rely on static identification and global compensation of sensitive or outlier channels, yet they often overlook the distributional differences of these important channels across inputs, leading to unsatisfactory quantization. In this work, we observe that the distributions and occurrence frequencies of important channels vary significantly both across modalities and among tokens, even within the same modality. Accordingly, we propose \textbf{Quant Experts (QE)}, a token-aware adaptive error compensation with mixture-of-experts for VLMs quantization. QE divides the important channels into token-independent and token-dependent groups. For the former, a shared expert is designed for most tokens to compensate for global quantization error using a low-rank adapter. For the latter, routed experts including multiple routed low-rank adapters are elaborated to compensate for local quantization error related to specific tokens. Extensive experiments demonstrate that QE consistently enhances task accuracy across various quantization settings and model scales, ranging from 2B to 70B parameters, while maintaining performance comparable to full-precision models.

AI.tldr: Quant Experts (QE) for PTQ in Vision-Language Models uses token-aware mixture-of-experts to adapt error compensation, splitting important channels into token-independent and token-dependent groups with a shared expert and routed experts, yielding consistent accuracy gains across 2B–70B models and approaching full-precision performance.
AI.motivation: Existing PTQ approaches rely on static, global compensation for sensitive/outlier channels and ignore input distribution differences of important channels across modalities and tokens, leading to suboptimal quantization.
AI.method: QE introduces a token-aware adaptive error compensation framework using a mixture of experts. Token-independent channels are handled by a shared expert leveraging a low-rank adapter for global quantization error. Token-dependent channels are addressed by routed experts consisting of multiple routed low-rank adapters to compensate for token-specific quantization error.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that QE consistently improves task accuracy across diverse quantization settings and model scales (from 2B to 70B parameters) while maintaining performance comparable to full-precision models.
AI.conclusion: QE provides a robust, scalable token-aware quantization correction for Vision-Language Models, bridging the gap between PTQ and FP performance. The mixture-of-experts approach effectively handles distributional differences across modalities and tokens.

title: Histopathology Image Normalization via Latent Manifold Compaction
summary: Batch effects arising from technical variations in histopathology staining protocols, scanners, and acquisition pipelines pose a persistent challenge for computational pathology, hindering cross-batch generalization and limiting reliable deployment of models across clinical sites. In this work, we introduce Latent Manifold Compaction (LMC), an unsupervised representation learning framework that performs image harmonization by learning batch-invariant embeddings from a single source dataset through explicit compaction of stain-induced latent manifolds. This allows LMC to generalize to target domain data unseen during training. Evaluated on three challenging public and in-house benchmarks, LMC substantially reduces batch-induced separations across multiple datasets and consistently outperforms state-of-the-art normalization methods in downstream cross-batch classification and detection tasks, enabling superior generalization.

AI.tldr: Latent Manifold Compaction (LMC) is an unsupervised framework that learns batch-invariant image embeddings by explicitly compacting stain-induced latent manifolds, trained on a single source dataset. It aims to harmonize histopathology images to improve cross-batch generalization and targets downstream tasks.
AI.motivation: Histopathology batch effects from staining, scanners, and acquisition pipelines hinder generalization across clinical sites. Existing normalization methods often fail to fully bridge domain gaps. A data-efficient, unsupervised approach that yields batch-invariant representations could improve deployment to unseen domains.
AI.method: LMC learns batch-invariant embeddings by explicit compaction of stain-induced latent manifolds from a single source dataset, effectively performing image harmonization without requiring labeled target-domain data.
AI.result: On three challenging public and in-house benchmarks, LMC reduces batch-induced separations across multiple datasets and consistently outperforms state-of-the-art normalization methods in downstream cross-batch classification and detection tasks, enabling superior generalization.
AI.conclusion: LMC provides a novel, unsupervised strategy for histopathology harmonization that yields improved cross-batch generalization and outperforms existing normalization baselines on downstream tasks.

title: EvalMVX: A Unified Benchmarking for Neural 3D Reconstruction under Diverse Multiview Setups
summary: Recent advancements in neural surface reconstruction have significantly enhanced 3D reconstruction. However, current real world datasets mainly focus on benchmarking multiview stereo (MVS) based on RGB inputs. Multiview photometric stereo (MVPS) and multiview shape from polarization (MVSfP), though indispensable on high-fidelity surface reconstruction and sparse inputs, have not been quantitatively assessed together with MVS. To determine the working range of different MVX (MVS, MVSfP, and MVPS) techniques, we propose EvalMVX, a real-world dataset containing $25$ objects, each captured with a polarized camera under $20$ varying views and $17$ light conditions including OLAT and natural illumination, leading to $8,500$ images. Each object includes aligned ground-truth 3D mesh, facilitating quantitative benchmarking of MVX methods simultaneously. Based on our EvalMVX, we evaluate $13$ MVX methods published in recent years, record the best-performing methods, and identify open problems under diverse geometric details and reflectance types. We hope EvalMVX and the benchmarking results can inspire future research on multiview 3D reconstruction.

AI.tldr: EvalMVX introduces a real-world benchmark for multiview reconstruction across MVS, MVPS, and MVSfP, enabling quantitative benchmarking over 25 objects, 20 views, 17 lighting conditions, with ground-truth meshes; evaluates 13 MVX methods to reveal strengths and open challenges.
AI.motivation: To jointly benchmark MVX techniques (MVS, MVPS, MVSfP) in real-world conditions and map their working range across geometric details and reflectance types, addressing gaps in existing datasets that focus mainly on RGB-based MVS.
AI.method: Construct EvalMVX dataset: 25 objects captured with a polarized camera under 20 views and 17 light conditions (including OLAT and natural illumination), totaling 8,500 images, with aligned ground-truth 3D meshes. Evaluate 13 MVX methods and analyze performance across geometry/reflectance variants.
AI.result: Identified best-performing MVX methods and enumerated open problems across diverse geometric details and reflectance types; provided a comprehensive benchmark for simultaneous MVX method evaluation.
AI.conclusion: EvalMVX enables systematic benchmarking of MVX techniques and is expected to drive future research in multiview 3D reconstruction by filling current evaluation gaps.

title: FoV-Net: Rotation-Invariant CAD B-rep Learning via Field-of-View Ray Casting
summary: Learning directly from boundary representations (B-reps) has significantly advanced 3D CAD analysis. However, state-of-the-art B-rep learning methods rely on absolute coordinates and normals to encode global context, making them highly sensitive to rotations. Our experiments reveal that models achieving over 95% accuracy on aligned benchmarks can collapse to as low as 10% under arbitrary $\mathbf{SO}(3)$ rotations. To address this, we introduce FoV-Net, the first B-rep learning framework that captures both local surface geometry and global structural context in a rotation-invariant manner. Each face is represented by a Local Reference Frame (LRF) UV-grid that encodes its local surface geometry, and by Field-of-View (FoV) grids that capture the surrounding 3D context by casting rays and recording intersections with neighboring faces. Lightweight CNNs extract per-face features, which are propagated over the B-rep graph using a graph attention network. FoV-Net achieves state-of-the-art performance on B-rep classification and segmentation benchmarks, demonstrating robustness to arbitrary rotations while also requiring less training data to achieve strong results.

AI.tldr: FoV-Net provides a rotation-invariant B-rep learning framework by encoding local geometry with LRF UV-grids and global context with Field-of-View grids, combined with a graph attention network, achieving state-of-the-art rotation robustness and data efficiency on B-rep classification and segmentation.
AI.motivation: B-rep learning methods that rely on absolute coordinates and normals are highly sensitive to rotations; alignment-based benchmarks overstate performance; a rotation-invariant representation is needed for arbitrary orientations.
AI.method: Represent each face with a Local Reference Frame (LRF) UV-grid encoding local surface geometry and Field-of-View (FoV) grids capturing surrounding 3D context via ray casting to intersect neighboring faces. Extract per-face features with lightweight CNNs and propagate them over the B-rep graph using a Graph Attention Network.
AI.result: FoV-Net achieves state-of-the-art performance on B-rep classification and segmentation benchmarks, with robustness to arbitrary rotations and improved data efficiency (less training data needed).
AI.conclusion: FoV-Net delivers a rotation-invariant B-rep learning framework that enhances robustness to pose variation and reduces data requirements, suggesting broader applicability to CAD analysis under arbitrary orientations.

title: Efficient Discovery of Approximate Causal Abstractions via Neural Mechanism Sparsification
summary: Neural networks are hypothesized to implement interpretable causal mechanisms, yet verifying this requires finding a causal abstraction -- a simpler, high-level Structural Causal Model (SCM) faithful to the network under interventions. Discovering such abstractions is hard: it typically demands brute-force interchange interventions or retraining. We reframe the problem by viewing structured pruning as a search over approximate abstractions. Treating a trained network as a deterministic SCM, we derive an Interventional Risk objective whose second-order expansion yields closed-form criteria for replacing units with constants or folding them into neighbors. Under uniform curvature, our score reduces to activation variance, recovering variance-based pruning as a special case while clarifying when it fails. The resulting procedure efficiently extracts sparse, intervention-faithful abstractions from pretrained networks, which we validate via interchange interventions.

AI.tldr: Frames learning of interpretable causal abstractions from neural nets as a structured-pruning problem, introducing Interventional Risk whose second-order expansion yields pruning criteria; under uniform curvature it reduces to activation variance; yields efficient, intervention-faithful abstractions and is validated by interchange interventions.
AI.motivation: To verify that networks implement interpretable causal mechanisms without brute-force interventions or retraining; provide a scalable method to extract faithful causal abstractions.
AI.method: Treat a trained network as a deterministic SCM; define Interventional Risk; perform second-order expansion to derive closed-form criteria for replacing a unit with a constant or folding it into a neighbor; show variance-based pruning as a special case under uniform curvature; apply to pretrained models to extract sparse, faithful abstractions.
AI.result: Derives an efficient pruning-based procedure that yields sparse, intervention-faithful abstractions; demonstrates validity via interchange interventions.
AI.conclusion: Links pruning with causal abstraction discovery, clarifies limits of variance-based pruning, and offers a scalable route to interpretable models by extracting faithful abstractions from pretrained networks.

title: DiffusionHarmonizer: Bridging Neural Reconstruction and Photorealistic Simulation with Online Diffusion Enhancer
comment: For more details and updates, please visit our project website: https://research.nvidia.com/labs/sil/projects/diffusion-harmonizer
summary: Simulation is essential to the development and evaluation of autonomous robots such as self-driving vehicles. Neural reconstruction is emerging as a promising solution as it enables simulating a wide variety of scenarios from real-world data alone in an automated and scalable way. However, while methods such as NeRF and 3D Gaussian Splatting can produce visually compelling results, they often exhibit artifacts particularly when rendering novel views, and fail to realistically integrate inserted dynamic objects, especially when they were captured from different scenes. To overcome these limitations, we introduce DiffusionHarmonizer, an online generative enhancement framework that transforms renderings from such imperfect scenes into temporally consistent outputs while improving their realism. At its core is a single-step temporally-conditioned enhancer that is converted from a pretrained multi-step image diffusion model, capable of running in online simulators on a single GPU. The key to training it effectively is a custom data curation pipeline that constructs synthetic-real pairs emphasizing appearance harmonization, artifact correction, and lighting realism. The result is a scalable system that significantly elevates simulation fidelity in both research and production environments.

AI.tldr: Proposes DiffusionHarmonizer: an online, single-GPU, temporally-conditioned diffusion-based enhancer that boosts realism and temporal consistency of renderings from NeRF/3D Gaussian Splatting in autonomous-robot simulation.
AI.motivation: Artifacts in novel-view renderings and poor integration of inserted dynamic objects across scenes reduce simulation fidelity; a scalable online enhancement is needed for safer, more realistic autonomous-robot testing.
AI.method: A single-step temporally-conditioned enhancer derived from a pretrained multi-step image diffusion model, designed for online use in simulators on one GPU. It uses a custom data-curation pipeline to create synthetic-real pairs emphasizing appearance harmonization, artifact correction, and lighting realism.
AI.result: Improved simulation fidelity in both research and production environments; the method is scalable and suitable for online deployment.
AI.conclusion: DiffusionHarmonizer offers a practical, online solution to elevate realism and temporal consistency of renderings from imperfect scene reconstructions, enabling more reliable autonomous-robot simulation.

title: Who Guards the Guardians? The Challenges of Evaluating Identifiability of Learned Representations
summary: Identifiability in representation learning is commonly evaluated using standard metrics (e.g., MCC, DCI, R^2) on synthetic benchmarks with known ground-truth factors. These metrics are assumed to reflect recovery up to the equivalence class guaranteed by identifiability theory. We show that this assumption holds only under specific structural conditions: each metric implicitly encodes assumptions about both the data-generating process (DGP) and the encoder. When these assumptions are violated, metrics become misspecified and can produce systematic false positives and false negatives. Such failures occur both within classical identifiability regimes and in post-hoc settings where identifiability is most needed. We introduce a taxonomy separating DGP assumptions from encoder geometry, use it to characterise the validity domains of existing metrics, and release an evaluation suite for reproducible stress testing and comparison.

AI.tldr: Standard identifiability metrics (e.g., MCC, DCI, R^2) are not universally valid proxies for identifiability in representation learning; their validity depends on implicit assumptions about the data-generating process (DGP) and encoder geometry, and violating these assumptions yields systematic misclassification (false positives/negatives). A taxonomy separates DGP and encoder considerations and a reproducible stress-testing suite is released to map metric validity domains.
AI.motivation: Presented metrics for measuring identifiability are often treated as universally meaningful, but their efficacy rests on specific structural assumptions. The work motivates clarifying these assumptions and providing tools to assess metric reliability across regimes.
AI.method: Introduce a taxonomy that partitions assumptions about the data-generating process (DGP) and encoder geometry; use it to map the validity domains of existing metrics; conduct stress-testing across regimes; release an evaluation suite for reproducible benchmarking.
AI.result: Show that metric misspecification arises under violations of DGP or encoder assumptions, leading to false positives and negatives in both classical identifiability regimes and post-hoc settings; provide a formal framework (taxonomy) and experimental suite for robust assessment.
AI.conclusion: Metrics for identifiability must be interpreted within their domain of validity; a taxonomy and stress-testing suite provide principled guidance for when and how to use these metrics and to compare methods reliably.

title: Memory Caching: RNNs with Growing Memory
summary: Transformers have been established as the de-facto backbones for most recent advances in sequence modeling, mainly due to their growing memory capacity that scales with the context length. While plausible for retrieval tasks, it causes quadratic complexity and so has motivated recent studies to explore viable subquadratic recurrent alternatives. Despite showing promising preliminary results in diverse domains, such recurrent architectures underperform Transformers in recall-intensive tasks, often attributed to their fixed-size memory. In this paper, we introduce Memory Caching (MC), a simple yet effective technique that enhances recurrent models by caching checkpoints of their memory states (a.k.a. hidden states). Memory Caching allows the effective memory capacity of RNNs to grow with sequence length, offering a flexible trade-off that interpolates between the fixed memory (i.e., $O(L)$ complexity) of RNNs and the growing memory (i.e., $O(L^2)$ complexity) of Transformers. We propose four variants of MC, including gated aggregation and sparse selective mechanisms, and discuss their implications on both linear and deep memory modules. Our experimental results on language modeling, and long-context understanding tasks show that MC enhances the performance of recurrent models, supporting its effectiveness. The results of in-context recall tasks indicate that while Transformers achieve the best accuracy, our MC variants show competitive performance, close the gap with Transformers, and performs better than state-of-the-art recurrent models.

AI.tldr: Memory Caching (MC) lets RNNs scale their effective memory with sequence length by caching hidden states, trading memory complexity between O(L) and O(L^2). It proposes four MC variants with gated aggregation and sparse selective mechanisms, analyzes their impact on linear and deep memory modules, and shows improvements on language modeling and long-context tasks; in in-context recall, Transformers remain best but MC variants are competitive and outperform state-of-the-art recurrent models.
AI.motivation: Transformers dominate sequence modeling but have quadratic memory/compute due to attention. Subquadratic recurrent models exist but struggle on recall-intensive tasks due to fixed memory. MC aims to flexibly extend RNN memory, bridging the gap between fixed-memory RNNs and growing-memory Transformers.
AI.method: Introduce Memory Caching (MC) by caching checkpoints of memory/hidden states to extend RNN memory capacity. Propose four MC variants, including gated aggregation and sparse selective mechanisms. Discuss implications for linear versus deep memory modules. Evaluate on language modeling and long-context understanding tasks, with in-context recall benchmarks comparing against Transformers and state-of-the-art recurrent baselines.
AI.result: MC enhances recurrent models, improving performance on language modeling and long-context tasks. In in-context recall benchmarks, Transformers achieve the best accuracy, but MC variants are competitive, closing the gap with Transformers and outperforming state-of-the-art recurrent models.
AI.conclusion: MC provides a simple, effective, and tunable way to scale RNN memory with sequence length, offering a flexible trade-off between O(L) and O(L^2) memory. It narrows the gap to Transformers on recall tasks and sets a foundation for further refinements of memory-enabled recurrent architectures.

title: FocusTrack: One-Stage Focus-and-Suppress Framework for 3D Point Cloud Object Tracking
summary: In 3D point cloud object tracking, the motion-centric methods have emerged as a promising avenue due to its superior performance in modeling inter-frame motion. However, existing two-stage motion-based approaches suffer from fundamental limitations: (1) error accumulation due to decoupled optimization caused by explicit foreground segmentation prior to motion estimation, and (2) computational bottlenecks from sequential processing. To address these challenges, we propose FocusTrack, a novel one-stage paradigms tracking framework that unifies motion-semantics co-modeling through two core innovations: Inter-frame Motion Modeling (IMM) and Focus-and-Suppress Attention. The IMM module employs a temp-oral-difference siamese encoder to capture global motion patterns between adjacent frames.
